**(b)** A Le-Chatelier test on OPC 43 cement gave the following observations:
- Initial pointer distance: $L_1 = 14.5$ mm
- Final pointer distance after boiling: $L_2 = 22.0$ mm
- Permissible limit as per IS 8112: **10 mm**

Determine whether the cement is sound. If you were an engineer, would you approve this cement for use in a coastal bridge project? Justify. **[7 marks]**

### Model Solution to (a)

**Apparatus:** A small split cylinder of brass, with two glass plates, two indicator pointers (150 mm long), rubber bands/clips, a 100 g balance (0.1 g accuracy), a trowel, a water bath, and a boiling arrangement.

**Procedure:**
1. Weigh **100 g** of cement and mix with **0.78 × P** grams of water, where P is the water required for standard consistency (%).
2. Fill the Le-Chatelier mould (placed on a glass plate) with the paste **without voids**, cover with the second glass plate, and apply light pressure.
3. Submerge the mould in water at **27 ± 2 °C** for **24 hours**.
4. Measure the distance between the indicator tips — this is $L_1$ in mm.
5. Return the mould to the water, bring to **boil in 25–30 minutes**, and maintain a **vigorous boil for 1 hour**.
6. Cool the mould to room temperature over **15 minutes** and re-measure the distance — this is $L_2$.

**Calculation:**
$$\text{Expansion} = L_2 - L_1 \; (\text{mm})$$

**Acceptance criteria (IS 269, 8112, 12269):**
- For OPC (all grades): expansion $\le 10$ mm.
- For low-heat and sulphate-resisting cement: expansion $\le 5$ mm.

**Precautions:** No air entrapment, use of standard consistency water, full rolling boil, immediate measurement after cooling. (7 marks)

### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Calculate observed expansion.**
$$\Delta L = L_2 - L_1 = 22.0 - 14.5 = 7.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with IS 8112 limit.**
The IS 8112 acceptance limit for OPC 43 by Le-Chatelier is **$\le 10$ mm**.

**Step 3 — Verdict.**
Since $7.5 \text{ mm} < 10 \text{ mm}$, the cement **conforms** to the soundness requirement and is technically **acceptable**.

**Step 4 — Engineering judgement for a coastal bridge.**
Although the cement passes the Le-Chatelier test, a responsible engineer should:
- **Recommend the autoclave test** as a secondary confirmation (Le-Chatelier is less sensitive than the autoclave).
- **Specify sulphate-resisting cement (SRC)** for a coastal environment with chlorides and sulphates, rather than ordinary OPC 43.
- **Check specific gravity, setting times, and compressive strength** at 3, 7, and 28 days.
- **Insist on manufacturer test certificates** for each batch.
- **Conduct alkali-silica reactivity (ASR) tests** for the proposed aggregates.

**Conclusion:** The cement passes the soundness test numerically, but for a **coastal bridge**, additional tests and a **better-suited cement grade** are recommended. **Conditional approval** with a switch to SRC is the prudent engineering call. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice 2

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]

**(a)** Explain the **Autoclave method** for determining the soundness of cement as per IS 4031 (Part 3). How is it superior to the Le-Chatelier method? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An OPC 53 cement sample was tested in the autoclave. The length-comparator readings are:
- Effective gauge length $L_0 = 250$ mm
- Initial reading $L_1 = 1.45$ mm
- Final reading $L_2 = 2.97$ mm

Calculate the autoclave expansion percentage. Is the cement sound as per IS 12269? Comment on the structural safety implications if this cement is used in a high-rise raft foundation. **[7 marks]**

### Model Solution to (a)

**Apparatus:** A high-pressure autoclave capable of maintaining $2.0 \pm 0.05$ MPa of saturated steam, a length comparator with 0.002 mm accuracy, moulds of $25 \times 25 \times 250$ mm with embedded gauge studs, a balance, and a trowel.

**Procedure:**
1. Prepare cement paste of standard consistency and cast into the moulds.
2. Cure in moist air for **24 hours**, then demould carefully.
3. Measure initial length $L_1$ using the comparator at $27 \pm 2$ °C.
4. Place specimens in the autoclave with sufficient water; raise pressure to **2.0 MPa over 60–90 minutes**.
5. Hold at 2.0 MPa for **3 hours**.
6. Release pressure slowly over 60–90 minutes (avoid thermal shock).
7. Cool to room temperature and measure final length $L_2$.

**Formula:**
$$E_a = \dfrac{L_2 - L_1}{L_0} \times 100 \; (\%)$$

**Acceptance (IS 12269 for OPC 53):** $E_a \le 0.8 \,\%$.

**Why Autoclave is superior to Le-Chatelier:**
- **Speed:** Completes in ~5 hours vs 27 hours.
- **Sensitivity:** Detects *all* free lime, including coarse, dead-burnt, and less-reactive fractions that Le-Chatelier misses.
- **Reproducibility:** Standardized steam pressure gives consistent results across labs.
- **Industrial preference:** Cement plants use autoclave as the QC benchmark; Le-Chatelier is largely an academic/acceptance test. (7 marks)

### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Apply the autoclave expansion formula.**
$$E_a = \dfrac{L_2 - L_1}{L_0} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**
$$L_2 - L_1 = 2.97 - 1.45 = 1.52 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Division and multiplication:**
$$E_a = \dfrac{1.52}{250} \times 100 = 0.00608 \times 100 = 0.608 \,\%$$

**Step 4 — Compare with IS 12269 limit.**
Permissible autoclave expansion for OPC 53: **$\le 0.8 \,\%$**.

**Step 5 — Verdict.**
Since $0.608 \,\% < 0.8 \,\%$, the cement **conforms** to IS 12269 and is **sound**.

**Step 6 — Structural safety implications for a high-rise raft foundation.**

A raft foundation for a high-rise building:
- Is a **massive concrete element** (often 1–2 m thick) where heat of hydration is a real concern.
- Carries **enormous column and wall loads** — any post-setting expansion will induce undesirable tensile stresses and cracking.
- Has a **design life of 100+ years**, making long-term chemical stability essential.

**Implications of using a marginally sound cement ($0.608 \,\%$):**
- The cement is technically sound, but the result is **only 24 % below the upper limit** — relatively tight margin.
- For a high-rise raft, engineers should **insist on a margin of at least 30–40 %** below the limit.
- It is advisable to **request a re-test** of a fresh sample and also verify the **chemical composition** (free CaO, MgO, and SO₃ content).
- Combining this cement with **pozzolanic admixtures (fly ash, GGBS)** is a good practice to consume any residual free lime through secondary pozzolanic reaction.

**Conclusion:** Cement is sound per IS 12269, but for a critical raft foundation, **supplementary chemical verification and use of blended cement** are recommended best practices. (7 marks)

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on Soundness questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the two methods:** Stating the autoclave limit (0.8 %) for the Le-Chatelier test, or vice versa, is an instant 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Wrong acceptance limit for LHSC/SRC:** These cements have stricter Le-Chatelier limit (5 mm) and stricter autoclave limit (0.6 %). Defaulting to OPC values loses marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the × 100 factor** in the autoclave formula — single most common numerical error.
> 4. **Incomplete procedure:** Writing "boil the mould for 1 hour" without specifying "bring to boil in 25–30 minutes" loses a half-mark.
> 5. **Not stating the unit of expansion:** Always write "**mm**" for Le-Chatelier and "**%**" for autoclave. Mixing units in the verdict is a marked error.
> 6. **Skipping the conclusion:** Always end with a clear "**the cement is sound / unsound**" statement. Ambiguous verdicts invite partial deductions.
> 7. **Forgetting to mention IS code numbers** (IS 4031 Part 3, IS 269/8112/12269) — KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for citing the correct code.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute KTU revision summary — Soundness of Cement:**
>
> - **Definition:** Ability of cement to retain volume after setting without delayed expansion.
> - **Three causes of unsoundness:** Free lime (CaO), free magnesia (MgO), excess gypsum.
> - **Two IS methods:** Le-Chatelier (mm) and Autoclave (%).
> - **IS Code for procedure:** IS 4031 (Part 3) — 1988 (Reaffirmed 2019).
> - **IS Code for specification:** IS 269 (OPC 33), IS 8112 (OPC 43), IS 12269 (OPC 53), IS 1489 (PPC).
> - **Le-Chatelier limit:** $\le 10$ mm for OPC; $\le 5$ mm for LHSC / SRC.
> - **Autoclave limit:** $\le 0.8 \,\%$ for OPC; $\le 0.6 \,\%$ for LHSC / SRC.
> - **Le-Chatelier mould:** Split brass cylinder, 30 mm dia × 30 mm height, 150 mm pointers.
> - **Autoclave conditions:** 2.0 ± 0.05 MPa saturated steam for 3 hours.
> - **Autoclave formula:** $E_a = \dfrac{L_2 - L_1}{L_0} \times 100 \; (\%)$.
> - **Le-Chatelier formula:** $\Delta L = L_2 - L_1$ (in mm).
> - **Volume expansion of free lime:** CaO → Ca(OH)₂ approximately doubles solid volume (~+100 %).
> - **Volume expansion of magnesia:** MgO → Mg(OH)₂ approximately +117 % volume change.
> - **Why Autoclave is preferred industrially:** Faster, more sensitive, more reproducible, detects all free-lime fractions.
> - **Test temperature for both methods:** 27 ± 2 °C (Indian Standard Testing Temperature).
> - **Atmospheric pressure correction:** Apply for labs in high-altitude regions (Munnar, Wayanad, Ladakh).
> - **Viva-favourite lines:** "Dead burnt lime is the most dangerous form of free CaO." / "Le-Chatelier detects only reactive free lime; autoclave detects total free lime." / "Soundness failures cause cracking that appears 2–5 years after construction."
> - **Practical engineering tie-in:** Always pair soundness results with chemical analysis of SO₃, CaO, MgO; and use blended cements (PPC/PSC) for extra safety in critical structures.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test on Coarse and Fine Aggregate: Sieve Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL508 - CONCRETE LAB (MT-2)
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Test on Coarse and Fine Aggregate: Sieve Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sieve Analysis (Gradation Test):** A fundamental laboratory test conducted to determine the **particle size distribution** of fine and coarse aggregates by passing a known weight of dried aggregate through a series of standard sieves arranged in descending order of aperture size, and measuring the weight retained on each sieve. The test is governed by **IS 2386 (Part I) - 1963** and the grading requirements are specified in **IS 383 - 2016**.

The result of a sieve analysis is expressed as the **percentage by mass passing** through each sieve, plotted as a **grading curve** (also called a particle size distribution curve). From this distribution, two critically important derived parameters are computed:

- **Fineness Modulus (FM)** — a single empirical number that represents the *coarseness* or *fineness* of an aggregate.
- **Grading Zone Classification** — categorisation of fine aggregate into **Zone I (coarsest) to Zone IV (finest)** as per IS 383.

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective (KTU Board Exam Favourite):** To determine the **gradation, fineness modulus, and grading zone** of the given sample of fine aggregate and coarse aggregate, and to assess its **suitability for concrete production** under IS 383 - 2016.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you have a mixed jar of nuts and bolts of different sizes — some big, some tiny, some in-between. If you pour this mixture through a stack of colanders (sieves) — the biggest holes at the top and the smallest at the bottom — every layer will catch only the pieces that are **too big to pass through it**. The very top colander catches the largest pieces, the bottom one catches only the finest dust. If you now weigh what each colander held, you have effectively *fingerprinted* the size distribution of your mixture.

**A sieve analysis does precisely this for construction aggregates.** It is the civil engineer's way of asking: *"What sizes of stones and sand do I have, and in what proportion?"* This is critically important because:

- If all particles are the **same size**, there will be large voids between them → you need **more cement paste** to fill those voids → uneconomical concrete.
- If particles are **well-graded** (a smooth mix of large, medium, and small sizes), the smaller particles **nest into the gaps** between larger ones, producing a **dense, economical, and strong concrete** with minimum void content.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Mantra:** *"Well-graded aggregate = Less cement paste = Stronger, denser, and cheaper concrete."* Sieve analysis is the **quality control tool** that tells us whether our aggregate follows this golden rule.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Standard Sieve Series (Fine Aggregate):** **4.75 mm, 2.36 mm, 1.18 mm, 600 µm, 300 µm, 150 µm** (plus pan).
- **Standard Sieve Series (Coarse Aggregate):** **63 mm, 40 mm, 20 mm, 16 mm, 12.5 mm, 10 mm, 4.75 mm** (plus pan) — depending on nominal size.
- **Minimum Sample Mass (Fine Aggregate, as per IS 2386 Part I):** **500 grams** for aggregate smaller than 4.75 mm.
- **Minimum Sample Mass (Coarse Aggregate):** Ranges from **2 kg (for 10 mm nominal)** to **20 kg (for 63 mm nominal)**.
- **Fineness Modulus Range (Typical):** Fine aggregate **2.0 – 4.0**; Coarse aggregate **6.0 – 8.0**.
- **Standard Sieving Time:** **10 minutes minimum** (mechanical sieving) or until less than **1%** of residue passes any sieve in 1 minute.

---

## 1.4 Why Sieve Analysis is the Most Repeated Lab Test in Concrete Technology

| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Workability** | Finer sand = more surface area = more water demand |
| **Strength** | Better gradation = lower water/cement ratio = higher strength |
| **Economy** | Well-graded aggregate reduces cement consumption |
| **Durability** | Dense packing reduces permeability |
| **Pumpability** | Gap-graded or single-sized aggregates cause pump blockages |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Principle of Sieve Analysis

The test is founded on a simple physical principle: when a granular material is agitated through a stack of woven-wire sieves, **particles are separated according to their intermediate dimension** (the second-largest dimension, since the smallest dimension aligns with the aperture). The aperture of the next sieve below is approximately **half** of the sieve above, which is the basis of the **R20 series of sieves** in IS 460.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **R20 Series Rule:** Each successive sieve in the standard series has an aperture that is the **$10^{(1/10)} \approx 1.259$** times the next finer sieve, OR in simplified form, every alternate sieve has half the aperture.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (Why & How)

1. **Drying the Sample:** Aggregates contain moisture that causes particles to stick together, leading to under-estimation of fines. Drying in an **oven at 100 °C to 110 °C** until constant mass is achieved ensures only the dry particles are sieved.

2. **Weighing the Dry Sample ($W_1$):** This is the **reference mass** against which all percentages are calculated. Typical values: **500 g** (fine) or **2–20 kg** (coarse).

3. **Stacking the Sieves in Descending Order:** Sieves are arranged from **largest aperture at the top** to **smallest at the bottom**, with a **collecting pan** at the very bottom. The 4.75 mm sieve is the **boundary** between fine and coarse aggregate.

4. **Mechanical Sieving for ~10 Minutes:** A **sieve shaker** provides consistent vertical and horizontal motion. Hand sieving is permitted but less reproducible. The endpoint is reached when **additional sieving time does not alter the retained mass by more than 1%** on any individual sieve.

5. **Weighing Retained Mass on Each Sieve ($W_i$):** Mass retained on the $i^{th}$ sieve is recorded. **The sum of all retained masses should equal the original sample mass within ±1%**; otherwise, the test must be repeated.

6. **Calculation of Gradation Parameters:** The masses are converted into percentage retained, cumulative percentage retained, and percentage passing for each sieve.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER THESE 4 EQUATIONS — they appear in nearly every KTU Board Exam question on this topic.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:-:|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Percentage Retained on $i^{th}$ sieve** | $P_{R,i} = \dfrac{W_i}{W_1} \times 100$ | $W_i$ = mass retained on sieve $i$; $W_1$ = total dry sample mass |
| 2 | **Cumulative % Retained on $i^{th}$ sieve** | $C_{R,i} = \sum_{j=1}^{i} P_{R,j}$ | Running total from coarsest sieve downwards |
| 3 | **Percentage Passing $i^{th}$ sieve** | $P_{P,i} = 100 - C_{R,i}$ | Also called "% finer than" the sieve aperture |
| 4 | **Fineness Modulus (FM)** | $FM = \dfrac{\sum C_{R,i}}{100}$ | Sum of **cumulative % retained** on the **standard IS sieves** only, divided by 100 |
| 5 | **Grading Modulus (alternative)** | $GM = \dfrac{\sum P_{P,i} \text{ (coarser than 150 µm)}}{100}$ | Less commonly used; IS prefers FM |

### Standard Sieve Sets Used in FM Calculation

| Aggregate Type | Standard Sieves for FM |
|---|---|
| **Fine Aggregate** | **150 µm, 300 µm, 600 µm, 1.18 mm, 2.36 mm, 4.75 mm, 10 mm** (7 sieves) |
| **Coarse Aggregate** | **4.75 mm, 10 mm, 12.5 mm, 20 mm, 40 mm, 63 mm, 80 mm** (7 sieves) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Some students forget to include the **150 µm pan** cumulative retained or the **topmost larger sieve** cumulative retained. The convention is: **cumulative % retained is taken from ALL sieves from the coarsest down to the 150 µm sieve (inclusive).**

---

## 2.4 Classification Limits (IS 383 - 2016)

### Fine Aggregate Grading Zones (Percentage Passing by Mass)

| Sieve Size | Zone I (Coarse) | Zone II (Medium) | Zone III (Medium-Fine) | Zone IV (Fine) |
|------------|:---------------:|:----------------:|:----------------------:|:--------------:|
| **4.75 mm** | 90 – 100 | 90 – 100 | 90 – 100 | 90 – 100 |
| **2.36 mm** | 60 – 95 | 75 – 100 | 85 – 100 | 95 – 100 |
| **1.18 mm** | 30 – 70 | 55 – 90 | 75 – 100 | 90 – 100 |
| **600 µm**  | 15 – 34 | 35 – 59 | 60 – 79 | 80 – 100 |
| **300 µm**  | 5 – 20 | 8 – 30 | 12 – 40 | 15 – 50 |
| **150 µm**  | 0 – 10 | 0 – 10 | 0 – 10 | 0 – 15 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The **finer the zone, the higher the % passing on smaller sieves**. Zone II sand is the most preferred for general-purpose concrete. Zone IV is generally avoided unless specifically required.

### Coarse Aggregate Classification

- **Single-sized aggregates:** e.g., 20 mm nominal — predominantly 10–20 mm.
- **Graded aggregates:** e.g., 20 mm graded — contains a range from 4.75 mm to 20 mm.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Sieve Analysis Matters |
|---|---|
| **Concrete Mix Design (IS 10262)** | Determines the **coarse aggregate fraction (by mass)** and water content |
| **Bituminous Mix Design** | Marshall & Superpave require specific gradation envelopes |
| **Filter Design in Earth Dams** | Filter material must satisfy **Terzaghi's filter criteria** based on gradation |
| **Mortar & Plaster Work** | Sand zone selection directly affects workability and finish |
| **Railway Ballast** | Coarse aggregate size limits (40–63 mm) governed by gradation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 Worked-Out Problem: Sieve Analysis of Fine Aggregate

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Board Exam Style):**
> A 500 g sample of dry fine aggregate was sieved through the standard set of IS sieves. The mass retained on each sieve is given below. Calculate: **(i)** Percentage retained, **(ii)** Cumulative percentage retained, **(iii)** Percentage passing, **(iv)** Fineness modulus, and **(v)** Identify the **grading zone** of the aggregate.

### Given Data Table

| Sieve No. | Sieve Size (mm) | Mass Retained $W_i$ (g) |
|:---------:|:---------------:|:----------------------:|
| 1 | 4.75 | 25 |
| 2 | 2.36 | 55 |
| 3 | 1.18 | 75 |
| 4 | 0.600 | 90 |
| 5 | 0.300 | 110 |
| 6 | 0.150 | 95 |
| 7 (Pan) | < 0.150 | 50 |
| **Total** | — | **$\sum W_i = 500$ g** ✓ |

**Verification:** $\sum W_i = 25 + 55 + 75 + 90 + 110 + 95 + 50 = 500$ g → matches the original sample mass.

---

### Step 1: Calculate Percentage Retained on Each Sieve

Using Formula: $P_{R,i} = \dfrac{W_i}{W_1} \times 100$, where $W_1 = 500$ g.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{R}(4.75 \text{ mm}) &= \frac{25}{500} \times 100 = 5.00\,\% \\[4pt]
P_{R}(2.36 \text{ mm}) &= \frac{55}{500} \times 100 = 11.00\,\% \\[4pt]
P_{R}(1.18 \text{ mm}) &= \frac{75}{500} \times 100 = 15.00\,\% \\[4pt]
P_{R}(0.600 \text{ mm}) &= \frac{90}{500} \times 100 = 18.00\,\% \\[4pt]
P_{R}(0.300 \text{ mm}) &= \frac{110}{500} \times 100 = 22.00\,\% \\[4pt]
P_{R}(0.150 \text{ mm}) &= \frac{95}{500} \times 100 = 19.00\,\% \\[4pt]
P_{R}(\text{Pan}) &= \frac{50}{500} \times 100 = 10.00\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Check:** Sum of all $P_R$ should equal **100%** (within ±1%).

$$
\sum P_R = 5 + 11 + 15 + 18 + 22 + 19 + 10 = 100\,\% \;\;\checkmark
$$

---

### Step 2: Calculate Cumulative Percentage Retained

Using Formula: $C_{R,i} = \sum_{j=1}^{i} P_{R,j}$ (running total from coarsest to finest).

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{R}(4.75 \text{ mm}) &= 5.00\,\% \\[4pt]
C_{R}(2.36 \text{ mm}) &= 5.00 + 11.00 = 16.00\,\% \\[4pt]
C_{R}(1.18 \text{ mm}) &= 16.00 + 15.00 = 31.00\,\% \\[4pt]
C_{R}(0.600 \text{ mm}) &= 31.00 + 18.00 = 49.00\,\% \\[4pt]
C_{R}(0.300 \text{ mm}) &= 49.00 + 22.00 = 71.00\,\% \\[4pt]
C_{R}(0.150 \text{ mm}) &= 71.00 + 19.00 = 90.00\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip:** The **cumulative % retained at 150 µm (last sieve) + % passing 150 µm** should always equal 100%. Here: $90 + 10 = 100$ ✓

---

### Step 3: Calculate Percentage Passing (% Finer)

Using Formula: $P_{P,i} = 100 - C_{R,i}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{P}(4.75 \text{ mm}) &= 100 - 5 = 95.00\,\% \\[4pt]
P_{P}(2.36 \text{ mm}) &= 100 - 16 = 84.00\,\% \\[4pt]
P_{P}(1.18 \text{ mm}) &= 100 - 31 = 69.00\,\% \\[4pt]
P_{P}(0.600 \text{ mm}) &= 100 - 49 = 51.00\,\% \\[4pt]
P_{P}(0.300 \text{ mm}) &= 100 - 71 = 29.00\,\% \\[4pt]
P_{P}(0.150 \text{ mm}) &= 100 - 90 = 10.00\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 4: Calculate Fineness Modulus (FM)

Using Formula: $FM = \dfrac{\sum C_{R,i}}{100}$, summed over the standard IS sieves (from 4.75 mm down to 150 µm).

$$
\begin{aligned}
FM &= \frac{C_R(4.75) + C_R(2.36) + C_R(1.18) + C_R(0.600) + C_R(0.300) + C_R(0.150)}{100} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5.00 + 16.00 + 31.00 + 49.00 + 71.00 + 90.00}{100} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{262.00}{100} \\[4pt]
&= \boxed{2.62}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 5: Identify the Grading Zone

Compare the % passing values with the IS 383 - 2016 limits:

| Sieve | Calculated % Passing | Zone I | Zone II | Zone III | Zone IV | Match |
|:-----:|:-------------------:|:------:|:-------:|:--------:|:-------:|:-----:|
| 4.75 mm | 95.00 | 90–100 | 90–100 | 90–100 | 90–100 | All ✓ |
| 2.36 mm | 84.00 | 60–95 | 75–100 | 85–100 | 95–100 | **I, II** |
| 1.18 mm | 69.00 | 30–70 | 55–90 | 75–100 | 90–100 | **I, II** |
| 600 µm  | 51.00 | 15–34 | 35–59 | 60–79 | 80–100 | **II** |
| 300 µm  | 29.00 | 5–20 | 8–30 | 12–40 | 15–50 | **I, II, III** |
| 150 µm  | 10.00 | 0–10 | 0–10 | 0–10 | 0–15 | All ✓ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Verdict:** The aggregate falls in **Grading Zone II** (medium sand), as the **600 µm value of 51%** is the most discriminating and uniquely fits Zone II. This is the **most preferred zone** for general-purpose concrete.

**FM Interpretation:** A fineness modulus of **2.62** lies in the typical range (2.2 – 2.6 for Zone II sand), confirming the classification.

---

## 3.2 Complete Results Summary Table (Model Answer Format for KTU)

| Sieve Size (mm) | Mass Retained (g) | % Retained | Cumulative % Retained | % Passing |
|:---------------:|:-----------------:|:----------:|:---------------------:|:---------:|
| 4.75  | 25  | 5.00  | 5.00  | 95.00 |
| 2.36  | 55  | 11.00 | 16.00 | 84.00 |
| 1.18  | 75  | 15.00 | 31.00 | 69.00 |
| 0.600 | 90  | 18.00 | 49.00 | 51.00 |
| 0.300 | 110 | 22.00 | 71.00 | 29.00 |
| 0.150 | 95  | 19.00 | 90.00 | 10.00 |
| Pan   | 50  | 10.00 | —     | —      |
| **Total** | **500** | **100** | **FM = 2.62** | **Zone II** |

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Fineness Modulus Concept (Theoretical Justification)

The fineness modulus is essentially a **weighted average sieve size index**. Consider a sand sample where:

- 5% is coarser than 4.75 mm → contributes 5
- 16% is coarser than 2.36 mm → contributes 16
- 31% is coarser than 1.18 mm → contributes 31
- ... and so on.

If we add all these cumulative percentages and divide by 100, we obtain a **single numerical index** of the average coarseness. A **higher FM** means the aggregate is **coarser** (more material retained on larger sieves, less passing finer ones). This is why FM is the *opposite* of fineness in intuitive terms — a sand with FM = 4.0 is "fineness modulus high" but physically "coarse."

Mathematically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
FM &= \frac{1}{100}\sum_{i} C_{R,i} \\[4pt]
   &= \frac{1}{100}\left[(C_{R,1}) + (C_{R,1} + P_{R,2}) + \ldots + (C_{R,n-1} + P_{R,n})\right] \\[4pt]
   &= \frac{1}{100}\left[n \cdot C_{R,1} + (n-1) \cdot P_{R,2} + \ldots + 1 \cdot P_{R,n}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms FM is a **weighted mean** of the cumulative retained values, which makes it a robust single-parameter index of gradation.

```mermaid
    H --> I[Collection Pan: less than 150 micron]
    B --> J[Mechanical Sieve Shaker]
    J --> K[Vibration for 10 minutes]
    K --> L[Weigh each Retained Fraction]
    L --> M[Calculate Percentage Retained]
    M --> N[Calculate Cumulative Percentage Retained]
    N --> O[Calculate Percentage Passing]
    O --> P[Compute Fineness Modulus]
```

## 4.3 IS 383 Grading Zone Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Fine Aggregate Sample] --> A[Calculate Percent Passing on All Sieves]
    A --> B{Compare 600 micron value with IS 383 limits}
    B --> C1[15 to 34 percent: Zone I Coarse Sand]
    B --> C2[35 to 59 percent: Zone II Medium Sand]
    B --> C3[60 to 79 percent: Zone III Fine Sand]
    B --> C4[80 to 100 percent: Zone IV Very Fine Sand]
    C1 --> D1[Check FM typically 2.6 to 3.2]
    C2 --> D2[Check FM typically 2.2 to 2.6]
    C3 --> D3[Check FM typically 1.6 to 2.2]
    C4 --> D4[Check FM typically 1.0 to 1.6]
    D1 --> E1[Decision: Suitable for general concrete]
    D2 --> E2[Decision: Preferred Zone for concrete]
    D3 --> E3[Decision: Acceptable with care]
```

---

## 4.4 Typical Grading Curve Schematic (Description for Exam Drawing)

While the grading curve is a **semi-log plot** (% passing on Y-axis, sieve aperture in mm on log-scale X-axis), the **shape characteristics** that examiners look for are:

- **Smooth, S-shaped curve descending from top-left to bottom-right.**
- **No horizontal steps** (which would indicate **gap-graded** aggregate — particles missing in a particular size range).
- **No sudden vertical drops** (which would indicate **single-sized** aggregate).
- **Curve lying between upper and lower limit envelopes** of the corresponding IS zone.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Drawing Tip:** Always label **both axes with units**, mark **sieve sizes in descending order** along the X-axis, and **plot % passing on Y-axis from 0 to 100%**. Mark the **zone limits as dotted lines** to show the envelope.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "Fineness Modulus" of an aggregate. What is its significance in concrete mix design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fineness Modulus (FM) is an empirical index of the coarseness or fineness of an aggregate, defined as **the sum of cumulative percentages retained on the standard IS sieves (from 150 µm to the largest size) divided by 100**. Numerically, a higher FM indicates a coarser aggregate. In concrete mix design, FM is used to (i) classify fine aggregate into grading zones (I to IV), (ii) proportion the coarse aggregate content, and (iii) estimate the water demand — a coarser sand (higher FM) has lower water requirement and improved workability for the same water-cement ratio. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the standard IS sieves used in the sieve analysis of fine aggregate. What is the minimum dry sample mass required as per IS 2386 (Part I)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The standard IS sieves used for fine aggregate gradation are: **4.75 mm, 2.36 mm, 1.18 mm, 600 µm, 300 µm, and 150 µm**, arranged with a collecting pan at the bottom. The minimum dry sample mass required for fine aggregate passing the 4.75 mm sieve is **500 grams** as per IS 2386 (Part I) - 1963. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, MT-2 Module 2]`

> **Q.A (a)** Describe the **procedure for sieve analysis of fine aggregate** as per IS 2386 (Part I) - 1963, with a neat sketch of the apparatus. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q.A (b)** The results of a sieve analysis on 1000 g of fine aggregate are given below. Determine the **fineness modulus, percentage passing each sieve, and identify the grading zone** as per IS 383. **(7 marks)**

| Sieve Size (mm) | Mass Retained (g) |
|:---------------:|:-----------------:|
| 4.75  | 50  |
| 2.36  | 120 |
| 1.18  | 180 |
| 0.600 | 220 |
| 0.300 | 230 |
| 0.150 | 150 |
| Pan   | 50  |

---

### Model Solution — Q.A (a) [7 Marks]

**Apparatus Required (1 mark):**
1. Set of IS sieves: **4.75 mm, 2.36 mm, 1.18 mm, 600 µm, 300 µm, 150 µm** with lid and collecting pan.
2. Mechanical **sieve shaker** (or hand-sieving equipment).
3. **Electronic weighing balance** with accuracy of 0.1 g.
4. **Drying oven** (100 °C – 110 °C).
5. Cleaning brush for sieves.

**Procedure (5 marks):**
1. Obtain a representative sample of fine aggregate weighing **at least 500 g** (here 1000 g is taken). 
2. **Dry the sample** in an oven at **100 °C to 110 °C** until constant mass is achieved (typically 24 hours). Allow to cool to room temperature.
3. Weigh the dried sample to the nearest **0.1 g** and record as $W_1 = 1000$ g.
4. Arrange the sieves in **descending order of aperture size** — 4.75 mm at top, 150 µm at bottom, with the pan at the very bottom. Fit a **lid on top** to prevent loss of dust.
5. Transfer the entire sample to the top sieve (4.75 mm) carefully, place the lid, and mount the stack on a **mechanical sieve shaker**. Sieve for **10 minutes minimum**, or until additional sieving does not change the retained mass on any sieve by more than **1%** in 1 minute of further sieving.
6. Carefully remove each sieve from the stack and **weigh the mass retained on each sieve** to the nearest 0.1 g. Record as $W_i$.
7. **Verify:** The sum of all retained masses ($\sum W_i$) should be within **±1%** of $W_1$. If not, the test is invalid and must be repeated.
8. Calculate the **% retained, cumulative % retained, % passing, and fineness modulus** from the recorded data.

**Sketch (1 mark):** A labelled diagram of the sieve stack (top to bottom): Lid → 4.75 mm → 2.36 mm → 1.18 mm → 600 µm → 300 µm → 150 µm → Pan, mounted on a sieve shaker. [Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.]

---

### Model Solution — Q.A (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Verify total mass retained (½ mark):**

$$
\sum W_i = 50 + 120 + 180 + 220 + 230 + 150 + 50 = 1000 \text{ g} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 2: Calculate % Retained, Cumulative % Retained, % Passing (3½ marks):**

| Sieve (mm) | $W_i$ (g) | $P_R = \dfrac{W_i}{1000} \times 100$ | $C_R$ | $P_P = 100 - C_R$ |
|:----------:|:---------:|:-------------------------------------:|:-----:|:-----------------:|
| 4.75  | 50  | 5.0  | 5.0  | 95.0 |
| 2.36  | 120 | 12.0 | 17.0 | 83.0 |
| 1.18  | 180 | 18.0 | 35.0 | 65.0 |
| 0.600 | 220 | 22.0 | 57.0 | 43.0 |
| 0.300 | 230 | 23.0 | 80.0 | 20.0 |
| 0.150 | 150 | 15.0 | 95.0 | 5.0  |
| Pan   | 50  | 5.0  | —    | —    |
| **Total** | **1000** | **100** | — | — |

**Step 3: Calculate Fineness Modulus (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FM &= \frac{\sum C_R}{100} = \frac{5.0 + 17.0 + 35.0 + 57.0 + 80.0 + 95.0}{100} \\[4pt]
   &= \frac{289.0}{100} = \boxed{2.89}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Identify the Grading Zone (1 mark):**

Comparing % passing with IS 383 limits (key sieve: 600 µm = 43%):
- Zone I: 15–34% → 43% does NOT fit
- **Zone II: 35–59% → 43% FITS ✓**
- Zone III: 60–79% → 43% does NOT fit
- Zone IV: 80–100% → 43% does NOT fit

**Conclusion:** The fine aggregate belongs to **Grading Zone II (Medium Sand)** with a **fineness modulus of 2.89**, which is **suitable for general-purpose concrete production.** **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, MT-2 Module 2]`

> **Q.B (a)** What do you understand by **"well-graded" and "gap-graded" aggregates**? Illustrate your answer with neat sketches of grading curves. State the **IS 383 grading zones for fine aggregate** with the corresponding percentage passing limits. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q.B (b)** A coarse aggregate sample weighing **10 kg** was subjected to sieve analysis and the results are tabulated below. Plot the **grading curve** on a semi-log graph and determine the **nominal maximum size, fineness modulus, and grading type** (single-sized or graded).

| Sieve Size (mm) | Mass Retained (kg) |
|:---------------:|:-----------------:|
| 40   | 0.0  |
| 20   | 1.5  |
| 16   | 2.5  |
| 12.5 | 3.0  |
| 10   | 1.8  |
| 4.75 | 1.0  |
| Pan  | 0.2  |

---

### Model Solution — Q.B (a) [7 Marks]

**Well-Graded Aggregate (3 marks):**
A well-graded aggregate is one in which the particle size distribution is **continuous and smooth**, with particles of all sizes present in appropriate proportions — from the largest to the smallest. Such an aggregate has:
- **Minimum void content** (smaller particles fill the gaps between larger ones).
- **Maximum density** for a given compaction effort.
- **Lower cement paste requirement** → economical concrete.
- The grading curve is **smooth, concave-shaped**, and resembles an "S" descending from top-left to bottom-right.

**Gap-Graded Aggregate (2 marks):**
A gap-graded aggregate is one in which **one or more intermediate size fractions are missing**. The grading curve shows a **horizontal step or plateau** in the missing size range, indicating a discontinuity. Such aggregates:
- Have **higher void content** than well-graded ones.
- May cause **segregation** and **honeycombing** in concrete.
- Are deliberately used in specific applications like **exposed-aggregate concrete, mass concrete (to reduce heat of hydration)**, and certain architectural finishes.

**IS 383 Grading Zones for Fine Aggregate (2 marks):**

| Sieve | Zone I (Coarse) | Zone II (Medium) | Zone III (Fine) | Zone IV (Very Fine) |
|:-----:|:---------------:|:----------------:|:---------------:|:-------------------:|
| 4.75 mm | 90 – 100 | 90 – 100 | 90 – 100 | 90 – 100 |
| 2.36 mm | 60 – 95  | 75 – 100 | 85 – 100 | 95 – 100 |
| 1.18 mm | 30 – 70  | 55 – 90  | 75 – 100 | 90 – 100 |
| 600 µm  | 15 – 34  | 35 – 59  | 60 – 79  | 80 – 100 |
| 300 µm  | 5 – 20   | 8 – 30   | 12 – 40  | 15 – 50  |
| 150 µm  | 0 – 10   | 0 – 10   | 0 – 10   | 0 – 15   |

> **Note:** Zone II is the **most preferred** for general structural concrete; Zone IV is generally unsuitable due to excessive fines.

---

### Model Solution — Q.B (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Verify total mass (½ mark):**

$$
\sum W_i = 0.0 + 1.5 + 2.5 + 3.0 + 1.8 + 1.0 + 0.2 = 10.0 \text{ kg} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 2: Calculate % Retained and Cumulative % Retained (2 marks):**

| Sieve (mm) | $W_i$ (kg) | $P_R (\%)$ | $C_R (\%)$ | $P_P = 100 - C_R (\%)$ |
|:----------:|:----------:|:----------:|:----------:|:---------------------:|
| 40   | 0.0  | 0.0  | 0.0  | 100.0 |
| 20   | 1.5  | 15.0 | 15.0 | 85.0  |
| 16   | 2.5  | 25.0 | 40.0 | 60.0  |
| 12.5 | 3.0  | 30.0 | 70.0 | 30.0  |
| 10   | 1.8  | 18.0 | 88.0 | 12.0  |
| 4.75 | 1.0  | 10.0 | 98.0 | 2.0   |
| Pan  | 0.2  | 2.0  | —    | —     |

**Step 3: Determine Nominal Maximum Size (1 mark):**

The nominal maximum size is the **smallest sieve on which more than 90% is retained** (or, equivalently, **the sieve above which less than 10% is retained**). 

From the table, the sieve **20 mm** retains only **15.0%** (i.e., passes 85%), and the next smaller sieve is **16 mm** retaining **40%**. The convention is to take the sieve where cumulative % retained first exceeds 10%. Here, $C_R(20 \text{ mm}) = 15\% > 10\%$, so:

$$
\boxed{\text{Nominal Maximum Size} = 20 \text{ mm}}
$$

(Strictly, by some definitions, it is the **sieve next above the nominal size**, so 20 mm is the nominal size.)

**Step 4: Calculate Fineness Modulus (1 mark):**

For coarse aggregate, FM uses the standard sieves 4.75 mm, 10 mm, 12.5 mm, 20 mm, 40 mm, 63 mm, 80 mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
FM &= \frac{0.0 + 15.0 + 40.0 + 70.0 + 88.0 + 98.0}{100} \\[4pt]
   &= \frac{311.0}{100} = \boxed{3.11}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this FM (3.11) seems low for coarse aggregate. **Note:** This is a **gap-graded or narrow-graded sample** where most material is in the 10–20 mm range, and very little passes 4.75 mm. A typical coarse aggregate FM ranges 6.0–8.0; this value suggests the **sample behaves more like a single-sized aggregate**, not a graded one.

**Step 5: Grading Type Identification (1 mark):**

- The sample has a **concentrated mass in the 12.5–20 mm range** (~70% between 12.5 mm and 20 mm).
- Only **2% passes the 4.75 mm sieve**, indicating very few fines.
- The grading curve will show a **steep drop between 20 mm and 4.75 mm**.

**Conclusion:** This is a **single-sized (nominally 20 mm) coarse aggregate**, also called a **"20 mm single-sized aggregate"** as per IS 383. It is **not a graded aggregate** (which would have a smoother distribution with significant mass in intermediate sizes). For concrete, this would need to be **combined with finer fractions** (e.g., 10 mm aggregate or M-sand) to achieve a well-graded coarse aggregate blend.

**Step 6: Grading Curve (1½ marks):**

Plot **% passing on Y-axis (0 to 100, linear)** versus **sieve aperture in mm on X-axis (log scale, 0.15 to 40+)**. Plot the points:
- (40 mm, 100%), (20 mm, 85%), (16 mm, 60%), (12.5 mm, 30%), (10 mm, 12%), (4.75 mm, 2%).
- Connect with a smooth curve. The shape should show a **steep descent** in the 10–20 mm range, characteristic of single-sized aggregate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't forget to sum cumulative % retained values across ALL standard sieves for FM** — some students take only a few sieves, which gives a wrong FM.
> 2. **Don't confuse % retained with % passing** — the relationship is: % passing = 100 − cumulative % retained.
> 3. **Don't include the pan cumulative in FM calculation** — the pan is NOT a standard sieve.
> 4. **Don't identify the zone based on just one sieve** — check ALL sieves. The 600 µm sieve is the most discriminating one.
> 5. **Always verify that $\sum W_i = W_1$** — failure to do this is a ½ mark deduction in KTU valuation.
> 6. **Don't use the grading curve on a linear-linear plot in exams** — it should be semi-log (X-axis log scale).
> 7. **For coarse aggregate, FM of 5–8 is normal**; values < 4 indicate a fine/narrow sample.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Exam Revision — Read this 3 times the night before the exam.**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Sieve analysis determines the **particle size distribution** of aggregates (IS 2386 Part I).
- ☐ **% Retained** = $(W_i / W_1) \times 100$.
- ☐ **Cumulative % Retained** = running total from coarsest to finest sieve.
- ☐ **% Passing** = $100 -$ Cumulative % Retained.
- ☐ **Fineness Modulus (FM)** = $\sum C_R / 100$ (across standard IS sieves only, excluding the pan).
- ☐ **Higher FM = Coarser aggregate; Lower FM = Finer aggregate.**

### Key Numbers to Memorize
- ☐ Fine aggregate: **500 g** minimum sample mass.
- ☐ Sieving duration: **minimum 10 minutes** (mechanical).
- ☐ Endpoint criterion: **<1% mass change in 1 min** of additional sieving.
- ☐ Standard IS sieves for fine aggregate: **4.75 mm, 2.36 mm, 1.18 mm, 600 µm, 300 µm, 150 µm**.
- ☐ Number of IS grading zones for fine aggregate: **4 (I, II, III, IV)**.
- ☐ Zone II is the **most preferred** for general concrete.

### Procedural Memory
- ☐ Oven-dry at **100–110 °C** before sieving.
- ☐ Sieve stack: **largest aperture on top, smallest at bottom, pan last**.
- ☐ **Lid on top, pan at bottom** — no material loss.
- ☐ Verify $\sum W_i = W_1 \pm 1\%$, else repeat.

### FM Interpretation Quick-Reference
- ☐ FM 2.2 – 2.6 → **Zone II sand** (most common, preferred).
- ☐ FM 2.6 – 2.9 → **Zone I sand** (coarse).
- ☐ FM 1.6 – 2.2 → **Zone III sand** (fine).
- ☐ FM < 1.6 → **Zone IV sand** (very fine — avoid for structural use).
- ☐ FM 6.0 – 8.0 → **Coarse aggregate** (normal range).
- ☐ FM < 5 → indicates **single-sized / gap-graded** coarse aggregate.

### Common Exam Question Types (Practice These!)
- ☐ *"Calculate the FM and grading zone from given sieve data."*
- ☐ *"Draw the grading curve and comment on the type of gradation."*
- ☐ *"Differentiate between well-graded and gap-graded aggregates."*
- ☐ *"Explain the procedure and state the IS code provisions."*
- ☐ *"What is the significance of FM in concrete mix design?"*

> **Best of luck with your KTU 2024 Board Exam — Master the formulas, the procedure, and the zone limits, and you will secure full marks!** 🎓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test on Coarse and Fine Aggregate: Water Absorption, Bulk Density, Void Ratio, Porosity and Specific Gravity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL508 - CONCRETE LAB (MT-2)
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Test on Coarse and Fine Aggregate: Water Absorption, Bulk Density, Void Ratio, Porosity and Specific Gravity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Specific Gravity of Aggregate

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
Specific gravity of an aggregate is defined as the ratio of the mass (or weight) of a given volume of aggregate solids to the mass of an equal volume of distilled water, both measured at a standard temperature of **27°C** (as per IS 2386 Part III).

$$G = \frac{\text{Density of aggregate}}{\text{Density of water}} = \frac{\gamma_{agg}}{\gamma_w}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Specific gravity is a **dimensionless** quantity (no units). It is **NOT** density. It is just a ratio. A higher value indicates a denser, stronger aggregate.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine you have a sealed, solid rock and a bottle of water of the *exact same size*. You put them on a weighing scale. The rock will weigh about 2.6–2.8 times more than the water. That magic number (≈2.65) is the **specific gravity** of the rock. It tells you "how many times heavier than water is this material, when volumes are equal?"

## 1.2 Water Absorption

**Formal Definition:**
Water absorption is the percentage of water that dry aggregate can absorb when fully immersed in water, expressed as a ratio of the weight of water absorbed to the weight of oven-dry aggregate.

$$\text{WA \%} = \frac{W_{sat} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$$

Where:
- $W_{sat}$ = Weight of aggregate in **saturated surface dry (SSD)** condition
- $W_{dry}$ = Weight of oven-dry aggregate

> [!NOTE]
> **SSD condition** is the state when the aggregate is fully saturated internally with water, but the surface is *dry*. This is a critical "imaginary" state used universally in mix design.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a dry kitchen sponge. Dip it in water for a few hours. Lift it out and gently wipe the surface until it feels *just* dry to the touch — but it is still heavy with water inside. The extra weight it has gained (compared to its dry weight) tells you how much water it soaked up. Aggregates do the exact same thing through their tiny pores.

## 1.3 Bulk Density

**Formal Definition:**
Bulk density of an aggregate is the **mass of a unit volume** of aggregate measured *in bulk* (i.e., including the voids between particles, plus the internal pores of the particles), expressed in **kg/m³** or **kg/Litre**.

$$\gamma_{bulk} = \frac{\text{Mass of aggregate in container}}{\text{Volume of container}}$$

Three sub-categories exist:
- **Loose Bulk Density (LBD):** when aggregate is poured gently into the container.
- **Roded/Compacted Bulk Density (RBD):** when aggregate is compacted by tamping/rodding in 3 layers.
- **Dry-Rodded Bulk Density (DRBD):** measured on oven-dry material in rodded condition.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Picture a jar of marbles. If you pour the marbles gently, the "loose" arrangement has lots of gaps. If you shake the jar, the marbles settle down and gaps reduce — this is "compacted" or "rodded". The *weight of marbles per litre of jar* changes. That is bulk density.

## 1.4 Void Ratio (e)

**Formal Definition:**
Void ratio is defined as the ratio of the **volume of voids** (V_v) to the **volume of solid particles** (V_s) in an aggregate mass.

$$e = \frac{V_v}{V_s}$$

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A packed lunchbox with idlis stacked neatly has smaller air gaps than one with lumpy puris. The idli box has lower void ratio because the *solids take up more space relative to the voids*.

## 1.5 Porosity (n)

**Formal Definition:**
Porosity is the ratio of the **volume of voids** (V_v) to the **total volume** (V_t = V_v + V_s) of the aggregate mass.

$$n = \frac{V_v}{V_t} = \frac{e}{1+e}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Porosity is *always less than 1*; Void ratio can be *anything ≥ 0*. They are both expressing the same fact in different denominators.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
If a glass of lemonade is 80% liquid and 20% ice, the lemonade's *fullness ratio* is 0.8 and *air/void ratio* is 0.2. Porosity here is 0.2 (voids/total), while void ratio would be 0.25 (voids/liquid).

---

## 1.6 Standards & Apparatus (KTU Expected)

| Test | IS Code | Apparatus |
|---|---|---|
| Specific Gravity | IS 2386 Part III | Pycnometer (fine agg.), Wire basket (coarse agg.) |
| Water Absorption | IS 2386 Part III | Weighing balance, oven, water tank |
| Bulk Density | IS 2386 Part III | Cylindrical metal measure (3, 5, 15 L) |
| Void Ratio & Porosity | Derived from above | Calculations only |

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Specific Gravity — Why Two Methods?

### For Coarse Aggregate (Pycnometer Not Used — Wire Basket Method)
The aggregate is weighed in water (suspended in a wire basket), and the difference between oven-dry weight and submerged weight gives the volume of solids (by Archimedes' Principle).

$$G_{ca} = \frac{W_{od}}{W_{od} - W_w}$$

Where:
- $W_{od}$ = Weight of oven-dry aggregate in air (g)
- $W_w$ = Weight of saturated aggregate immersed in water (g)

### For Fine Aggregate (Pycnometer Method)
Fine aggregate is too small to basket — so it is placed in a glass pycnometer filled with water. The displaced water reveals volume.

$$G_{fa} = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}$$

Where:
- $W_1$ = Weight of empty pycnometer
- $W_2$ = Weight of pycnometer + oven-dry fine aggregate
- $W_3$ = Weight of pycnometer + aggregate + water (full)
- $W_4$ = Weight of pycnometer filled with water only

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pycnometer formula has a *clever algebraic trick* in the denominator. (W₄ − W₁) is the weight of water that fills the *empty* pycnometer (i.e., full volume of pycnometer). (W₃ − W₂) is the weight of water added *with* the aggregate (i.e., water filling the voids between fine particles). Subtracting gives the weight of water equal in volume to the **solids only** — that's the denominator's role.

## 2.2 Water Absorption — Physical Significance

Why does it matter for concrete?
- Aggregates with high water absorption (>2% for coarse, >3% for fine) are *porous and weak*.
- They absorb mixing water → reduces workability → reduces effective w/c ratio.
- **IS 383 limits absorption** to 2% for coarse and 3% for fine aggregate for structural concrete.

## 2.3 Bulk Density — Engineering Significance

| Property | Typical Value (kg/m³) | Application |
|---|---|---|
| LBD of coarse aggregate | 1400–1500 | Used in nominal mix design (volumetric batching) |
| RBD of coarse aggregate | 1500–1700 | For high-strength concrete design |
| Sand (fine agg.) bulk density | 1500–1700 | Used in concrete mix design |

Higher bulk density = denser packing = less void content = **less cement paste needed** = economical mix.

## 2.4 Derivation of Void Ratio & Porosity

Given:
- Volume of container = V
- Mass of aggregate filled = M
- Specific gravity = G
- Density of water = $\rho_w$ = 1000 kg/m³

**Step 1:** Volume of solids
$$V_s = \frac{M}{G \cdot \rho_w}$$

**Step 2:** Volume of voids
$$V_v = V - V_s = V - \frac{M}{G \cdot \rho_w}$$

**Step 3:** Void ratio
$$e = \frac{V_v}{V_s} = \frac{V - \frac{M}{G \rho_w}}{\frac{M}{G \rho_w}} = \frac{G \rho_w V - M}{M}$$

**Step 4:** Bulk density in terms of M, V
$$\gamma_{bulk} = \frac{M}{V}$$

Substituting back, the **famous KTU relationship** emerges:
$$e = \frac{G \cdot \rho_w}{\gamma_{bulk}} - 1$$

And porosity:
$$n = \frac{e}{1+e} = 1 - \frac{\gamma_{bulk}}{G \cdot \rho_w}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> All formulas, units, and boundary conditions in one master table.

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Standard Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Gravity (coarse) | $G = \dfrac{W_{od}}{W_{od} - W_w}$ | dimensionless | 2.6 – 2.8 typical |
| 2 | Specific Gravity (fine) | $G = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}$ | dimensionless | 2.6 – 2.75 typical |
| 3 | Water Absorption | $WA = \dfrac{W_{sat} - W_{od}}{W_{od}} \times 100$ | % | ≤ 2% (coarse), ≤ 3% (fine) |
| 4 | Bulk Density (Loose) | $\gamma_{L} = \dfrac{M_{loose}}{V}$ | kg/m³ | 1400–1500 (coarse) |
| 5 | Bulk Density (Roded) | $\gamma_{R} = \dfrac{M_{roded}}{V}$ | kg/m³ | 1500–1700 (coarse) |
| 6 | Void Ratio | $e = \dfrac{V_v}{V_s}$ | dimensionless | 0.4 – 0.7 typical |
| 7 | Porosity | $n = \dfrac{V_v}{V_t} = \dfrac{e}{1+e}$ | dimensionless | 0.3 – 0.5 typical |
| 8 | Bulk density ↔ G link | $\gamma_{bulk} = \dfrac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1+e}$ | kg/m³ | — |
| 9 | Apparent specific gravity | $G_{app} = \dfrac{W_{od}}{W_{od} - W_w}$ (no SSD) | dimensionless | > G_bulk |
| 10 | SSD specific gravity | $G_{SSD} = \dfrac{W_{sat}}{W_{sat} - W_w}$ | dimensionless | Used in mix design |

**Real-world utility of these properties:**
- **G** controls **absolute volume batching** in mix design (IS 10262).
- **WA** dictates the **moisture correction** of aggregates before batching.
- **Bulk density** is needed to convert **mass batching** to **volume batching** in site mix.
- **e** and **n** are the foundation of concrete **mix proportioning** because they tell you the *cement paste demand* per unit volume of concrete.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — The "Full Stack" Problem

> This is the *exact pattern* KTU loves in 14-mark questions: a single observation table feeding **all five** properties.

### Given Observations:

A pycnometer test on fine aggregate gave the following weights:
- $W_1$ (empty pycnometer) = **640 g**
- $W_2$ (pycnometer + oven-dry fine aggregate) = **1640 g**
- $W_3$ (pycnometer + agg + water filled to top) = **2450 g**
- $W_4$ (pycnometer + water only filled to top) = **1860 g**

A bulk density test on the same fine aggregate in a 3-litre cylindrical measure (volume = 0.003 m³) gave:
- Mass of empty measure = 5.20 kg
- Mass of measure + loose aggregate = 9.65 kg
- Mass of measure + compacted (roded) aggregate = 10.40 kg

### Required: Calculate Specific Gravity, Water Absorption, Bulk Density (loose & roded), Void Ratio, and Porosity for the *roded* state. Also, given the WA test of the same material gave 1.0%, compute the SSD density.

---

### Step 1 — Specific Gravity of Fine Aggregate

$$G = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}$$

Substitute values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_2 - W_1 &= 1640 - 640 = 1000 \text{ g} \\
(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2) &= (1860 - 640) - (2450 - 1640) \\
&= 1220 - 810 \\
&= 410 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$G = \frac{1000}{410} = 2.439$$

> ✅ **G ≈ 2.44** (slightly low — suggests a porous fine aggregate, e.g., laterite sand)

---

### Step 2 — Bulk Density (Loose)

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{loose} &= 9.65 - 5.20 = 4.45 \text{ kg} \\
V &= 0.003 \text{ m}^3 \\
\gamma_{L} &= \frac{M_{loose}}{V} = \frac{4.45}{0.003} = 1483.33 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Bulk Density (Roded)

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{roded} &= 10.40 - 5.20 = 5.20 \text{ kg} \\
\gamma_{R} &= \frac{5.20}{0.003} = 1733.33 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Void Ratio (Roded condition)

Using the **master relation** derived in Section 2.4:

$$e = \frac{G \cdot \rho_w}{\gamma_R} - 1$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
e &= \frac{2.439 \times 1000}{1733.33} - 1 \\
&= 1.4072 - 1 \\
&= 0.4072
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **e ≈ 0.407** (very typical for well-graded roded sand)

---

### Step 5 — Porosity (Roded condition)

$$n = \frac{e}{1+e} = \frac{0.4072}{1 + 0.4072} = \frac{0.4072}{1.4072}$$

$$n = 0.2894$$

> ✅ **n ≈ 28.94%**

---

### Step 6 — SSD Density (using WA = 1.0%)

Water absorption is defined on oven-dry basis. So:
$$W_{sat} = W_{od} (1 + \text{WA}/100) = W_{od} \times 1.01$$

SSD specific gravity is the ratio using $W_{sat}$:

$$G_{SSD} = \frac{W_{sat}}{W_{sat} - W_w}$$

By Archimedes, the volume of solids is unchanged, so $W_{sat} - W_w$ is the same submerged weight as for the oven-dry sample. If the oven-dry specimen was 1000 g and submerged weight was $W_w$ g, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{SSD} &= \frac{1000 \times 1.01}{W_w} \\
\text{But } W_w &= W_{od}\left(1 - \frac{1}{G}\right) = 1000 \left(1 - \frac{1}{2.439}\right) = 1000 \times 0.5900 = 590.0 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$G_{SSD} = \frac{1010}{590} = 1.712$$

> ✅ **G_SSD ≈ 1.71**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Tool for KTU

> Use this matrix during the exam to identify *which* test applies to *which* form of aggregate.

| Property | Coarse Aggregate (CA) | Fine Aggregate (FA) |
|---|---|---|
| Specific Gravity | Wire basket + water tank | Pycnometer |
| Water Absorption | 24-hour soak + SSD + oven-dry | Same procedure |
| Bulk Density (LBD & RBD) | 15 L measure (large size), rodding 25 times × 3 layers | 3 L measure, rodding 25 times × 3 layers |
| Sample Size | ≥ 5 kg (for 20 mm) | ≥ 500 g (per IS 2386) |
| Typical G range | 2.6 – 2.85 | 2.55 – 2.75 |
| Typical WA range | 0.5% – 1.5% | 1.0% – 3.0% |
| IS Reference | IS 2386 Part III | IS 2386 Part III |

---

## 3.3 Lab Procedure — Step-by-Step (Coarse Aggregate)

1. Take a representative sample of coarse aggregate ≥ 2 kg.
2. Wash thoroughly to remove dust, then dry in an oven at 100–110°C for 24 hours.
3. Cool to room temperature and weigh → $W_{od}$.
4. Immerse in water at room temperature for 24 hours.
5. Take out, surface-dry with a cloth → $W_{sat}$ (SSD).
6. Place the SSD sample in a wire basket and weigh suspended in water → $W_w$.
7. Compute $G$ and $WA$ from formulas.
8. For bulk density: take a clean, dry 15-litre cylindrical measure. Fill in 3 layers; rod each layer 25 times with a 16 mm tamping rod. Strike off the top surface. Weigh.
9. Repeat without rodding (just scoop from 50 mm height) for LBD.

---

## 3.4 Lab Procedure — Step-by-Step (Fine Aggregate)

1. Take ~1 kg of representative fine aggregate sample.
2. Oven-dry at 100–110°C for 24 h. Cool. Weigh the empty pycnometer → $W_1$.
3. Add ~500 g of dry fine aggregate to the pycnometer. Weigh → $W_2$.
4. Add water to cover the sample. Stir to remove air bubbles (no boiling needed for FA, gentle rolling suffices).
5. Fill pycnometer to the top mark. Weigh → $W_3$.
6. Empty, clean, fill pycnometer with water only to the mark. Weigh → $W_4$.
7. Use the four-weight pycnometer formula.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define specific gravity of aggregate. Why is it always greater than 1?**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 3 marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Specific gravity of aggregate is the ratio of the weight of a given volume of aggregate to the weight of an equal volume of distilled water at 27°C. It is dimensionless. Since aggregates (rock, sand) are *denser than water* under normal conditions, their weight exceeds that of equal water volume, making the ratio > 1. Typical range is 2.6–2.8.

### **Q2. What is the SSD condition? Why is it preferred in mix design over oven-dry condition?**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2 | 3 marks]**

**Model Answer:**
SSD (Saturated Surface Dry) is the condition where aggregate pores are completely filled with water, but the external surface is dry. It is preferred in mix design because it represents aggregate in its *inert* state within concrete — it neither absorbs water from the mix nor contributes water to the cement paste, making the w/c ratio calculation accurate.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Specific Gravity & Bulk Density of Coarse Aggregate

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 2 | 14 marks]**

**(a) Describe with neat sketch the procedure to determine the specific gravity and water absorption of coarse aggregate. List the IS code provisions. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus:** Wire basket, water tank, weighing balance (sensitivity 0.5 g), oven, cloth for surface drying.

**IS Code:** IS 2386 (Part III) – 1963.

**Procedure:**
1. Take ~2 kg of coarse aggregate, wash to remove fines, oven-dry at 100–110°C for 24 h.
2. Cool to room temperature (1 h), weigh as $W_{od}$.
3. Immerse in water at room temperature for 24 h.
4. Remove, surface-dry with absorbent cloth to **SSD condition**, weigh as $W_{sat}$.
5. Place the SSD aggregate in the wire basket, suspend in water, weigh as $W_w$.
6. Calculate:

$$G = \frac{W_{od}}{W_{od} - W_w}$$

$$WA\% = \frac{W_{sat} - W_{od}}{W_{od}} \times 100$$

> *(Note: The question asks for "neat sketch" — students must draw the wire basket suspended in water tank connected to a balance.)*

---

**(b) In a test on coarse aggregate, the following observations were recorded: [7 marks]**
- Oven-dry weight of aggregate = **1980 g**
- Weight of SSD aggregate = **2000 g**
- Weight of SSD aggregate suspended in water = **1240 g**
- In a separate bulk density test using a 15-litre measure: weight of empty measure = 8.2 kg, weight of measure + compacted aggregate = 27.4 kg.

**Compute: (i) Specific gravity (ii) Water absorption (iii) Roded bulk density (iv) Void ratio (v) Porosity.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Specific Gravity:**
$$G = \frac{W_{od}}{W_{od} - W_w} = \frac{1980}{1980 - 1240} = \frac{1980}{740} = 2.6757$$

> ✅ **G = 2.68**

**(ii) Water Absorption:**
$$WA = \frac{W_{sat} - W_{od}}{W_{od}} \times 100 = \frac{2000 - 1980}{1980} \times 100 = 1.0101\%$$

> ✅ **WA = 1.01%** (excellent, well within IS 383 limit of 2%)

**(iii) Roded Bulk Density:**
$$M_{roded} = 27.4 - 8.2 = 19.2 \text{ kg}, \quad V = 15 \text{ L} = 0.015 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\gamma_R = \frac{19.2}{0.015} = 1280 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

> ✅ **γ_R = 1280 kg/m³** (low — suggests single-sized aggregate or gap-graded material)

**(iv) Void Ratio (using master formula):**
$$e = \frac{G \cdot \rho_w}{\gamma_R} - 1 = \frac{2.6757 \times 1000}{1280} - 1 = 2.0904 - 1 = 1.0904$$

> ✅ **e = 1.09** (very high — voids occupy more space than solids, indicating poor packing)

**(v) Porosity:**
$$n = \frac{e}{1+e} = \frac{1.0904}{2.0904} = 0.5216$$

> ✅ **n = 52.16%**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Fine Aggregate & Pycnometer Method

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Module 2 | 14 marks]**

**(a) With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the pycnometer method for determining the specific gravity of fine aggregate. State the IS code. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus:** Pycnometer (~1 L capacity), weighing balance (0.1 g sensitivity), distilled water, oven, vacuum desiccator (or rolling mechanism).

**IS Code:** IS 2386 (Part III) – 1963.

**Procedure:**
1. Take ~1 kg of fine aggregate, oven-dry at 100–110°C for 24 h; cool.
2. Weigh the **empty, clean, dry** pycnometer = $W_1$.
3. Introduce ~500 g of oven-dry fine aggregate into the pycnometer; weigh = $W_2$.
4. Add distilled water to cover the sample by ~25 mm. Agitate gently (or apply vacuum) to remove entrapped air bubbles.
5. Fill the pycnometer to its calibration mark; weigh = $W_3$.
6. Empty, clean, and refill the pycnometer with distilled water alone to the mark; weigh = $W_4$.

**Formula:**
$$G = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}$$

**Diagram:** *(Students should draw a labelled pycnometer — round-bottomed flask with a stopper containing a capillary bore, filled with FA + water up to the mark.)*

---

**(b) The following readings were obtained in a pycnometer test on fine aggregate: [7 marks]**
- $W_1$ = 645 g, $W_2$ = 1645 g, $W_3$ = 2445 g, $W_4$ = 1860 g.
- A bulk density test using a 3-litre measure gave: mass of empty measure = 5.10 kg, mass of measure + compacted sand = 10.30 kg.

**Determine: (i) Specific gravity (ii) Roded bulk density (iii) Void ratio (iv) Porosity.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Specific Gravity:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_2 - W_1 &= 1645 - 645 = 1000 \text{ g} \\
(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2) &= (1860 - 645) - (2445 - 1645) \\
&= 1215 - 800 = 415 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$G = \frac{1000}{415} = 2.4096$$

> ✅ **G = 2.41**

**(ii) Roded Bulk Density:**
$$M_{roded} = 10.30 - 5.10 = 5.20 \text{ kg}, \quad V = 3 \text{ L} = 0.003 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\gamma_R = \frac{5.20}{0.003} = 1733.33 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

> ✅ **γ_R = 1733.33 kg/m³**

**(iii) Void Ratio:**
$$e = \frac{G \cdot \rho_w}{\gamma_R} - 1 = \frac{2.4096 \times 1000}{1733.33} - 1 = 1.3901 - 1 = 0.3901$$

> ✅ **e = 0.39**

**(iv) Porosity:**
$$n = \frac{e}{1+e} = \frac{0.3901}{1.3901} = 0.2806$$

> ✅ **n = 28.06%**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the SSD step:** In water absorption, students often weigh the aggregate *while wet* instead of *after surface drying with a cloth*. SSD is non-negotiable.
> 2. **Mixing up the pycnometer formula:** The denominator is *(W₄ − W₁) − (W₃ − W₂)*, NOT *W₃ − W₄* or similar. A single sign error gives a wildly wrong G.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency in bulk density:** V must be in m³ (not litres) to get kg/m³. Marks lost in conversion.
> 4. **Reporting WA as a decimal instead of percentage:** WA is conventionally reported in %, so multiply by 100.
> 5. **Omitting the mass of the empty measure:** In bulk density, students forget to subtract the empty measure mass and end up with absurdly high values.
> 6. **Forgetting G × ρ_w = γ_solid:** Many students think the denominator in the void ratio formula is just γ_bulk, leading to e < 1 always — which is wrong for poorly packed aggregates.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> ⭐ **Must-Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- [ ] Specific Gravity for **CA**: $G = \dfrac{W_{od}}{W_{od} - W_w}$ (basket method)
- [ ] Specific Gravity for **FA**: $G = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}$ (pycnometer)
- [ ] Water Absorption: $WA\% = \dfrac{W_{sat} - W_{od}}{W_{od}} \times 100$ — always on oven-dry basis
- [ ] Bulk Density (LBD vs RBD): three layers × 25 tamping strokes with 16 mm rod
- [ ] Void Ratio formula: $e = \dfrac{G \rho_w}{\gamma_{bulk}} - 1$
- [ ] Porosity formula: $n = \dfrac{e}{1+e}$ — always a *fraction* between 0 and 1
- [ ] Standard temperature for SG = **27°C**; oven-dry temperature = **100–110°C**
- [ ] IS codes: **IS 2386 Part III** (tests), **IS 383** (specifications)
- [ ] Typical limits: WA ≤ 2% (CA), ≤ 3% (FA); G between **2.6 and 2.8**
- [ ] SSD condition is critical: surface dry, internally saturated
- [ ] Three bulk density forms: LBD < RBD < solid density (always)
- [ ] **Sampling rule:** minimum 5 kg for CA, 500 g for FA per test
- [ ] The "**1000 g**" in pycnometer means ~500 cc, ensuring 2× pycnometer volume of water can fit
- [ ] **Always report two decimal places** for G, e, and n in KTU 2024 answer scripts
- [ ] KTU may give a 14-mark question combining all five properties on a single observation set — practice the "full-stack" numerical from Section 3.1 thoroughly

> **Key Insight (often asked in viva):**
> *"Why is the apparent specific gravity always greater than the bulk specific gravity?"*
> Because apparent SG excludes the impermeable pores (only solid material is in the denominator), while bulk SG includes the volume of impermeable pores (which adds to the denominator, reducing the ratio).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test on Bulking of Sand
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL508 - CONCRETE LAB (MT-2)
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Test on Bulking of Sand

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Bulking of Sand** is defined as the *phenomenon of increase in the apparent volume of fine aggregate (sand) due to the presence of a thin film of moisture adhering around the sand particles, which causes the particles to push apart and occupy a larger bulk volume than the same mass of completely dry sand.*

In the KTU 2024 Concrete Laboratory (PCCEL508) syllabus, this test is classified under **IS 2386 (Part III) – 1963** and forms a mandatory pre-batching check before any volumetric mix design. The numerical magnitude of bulking is expressed as the **Bulking Percentage** or **Bulking Factor**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCCEL508 – Module 2):**
> The bulking test is a *qualitative + quantitative* experiment. Students are expected to (a) determine the moisture content at which maximum bulking occurs, (b) plot the characteristic **bulking curve**, and (c) explain the engineering consequence of ignoring this phenomenon during site batching.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Vital Constant:**
> For standard **river sand (M-sand)**, the **maximum bulking is approximately 20% to 30%** and it occurs at a moisture content of roughly **4% to 6%** by weight of dry sand. The KTU board expects students to remember the *approximate* peak, not exact values.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you pour a bag of dry marbles into a measuring jug — they settle tightly because nothing is forcing them apart. Now imagine the marbles are lightly greased. The same mass of marbles occupies *more* height in the jug because the thin grease film acts as a tiny cushion, keeping adjacent particles from touching. The **sand bulking phenomenon behaves identically** — water molecules wedge themselves between the sand grains, lifting them apart like invisible springs.

Think of it as the **"invisible pillow effect"**: a few percent of water does not make sand wet enough to flow like a slurry, but is just enough to keep particles separated, maximizing void space between them. Beyond a saturation point (~15–20% moisture), the water fills the voids entirely, the cushion collapses, and the sand returns to (and even drops below) its dry volume.

---

### Quick Visual Snapshot

- **Dry Sand:** Tight packing, minimum volume, particles in direct contact.
- **Moist Sand (peak bulking):** Particles separated by water films, maximum volume.
- **Saturated Sand:** Voids filled with water, volume returns close to dry state.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — The "Why" and the "How"

The physics of bulking can be broken down into four logical stages:

1. **Stage 1 – Dry State (0% moisture):**
   - Sand particles are in direct frictional contact.
   - Packing is at its densest *loose* configuration.
   - The volume recorded is the **reference dry loose volume ($V_d$)**.

2. **Stage 2 – Film Formation Stage (1% to ~5–6% moisture):**
   - Water adheres to particle surfaces, forming a thin *meniscus* film.
   - **Surface tension** of water creates an apparent repulsive force between adjacent particles (the *capillary pressure* acts like a stretched spring).
   - Volume expands rapidly — this is the **bulking zone**.

3. **Stage 3 – Peak Bulking Point (~5% to 6% moisture):**
   - The water film thickness is at its *optimal cushion* thickness.
   - The combined *inter-particle repulsion* and *void-fill* effects balance to give the **maximum bulking percentage**.
   - For most natural river sands, peak bulking = **20% to 30%**.

4. **Stage 4 – Collapse / Saturated Stage (>8% to 10% moisture):**
   - Excess water begins to fill the voids between particles.
   - Capillary pressure diminishes; particles can no longer be held apart.
   - Volume **decreases** and gradually approaches the dry volume.
   - At fully saturated state, the apparent volume is slightly **less** than the dry volume because the water itself displaces air-filled voids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does bulking matter in concrete technology?**
> If a site engineer batches concrete by *volume* (a common practice in small-scale or rural construction) using moist sand at peak bulking, the *actual mass* of sand used is significantly **less** than the design mix intended. This leads to a **sand-deficient mix** — a lean, harsh, low-strength concrete prone to segregation and bleeding. The bulking test allows the engineer to compute a **correction factor** and restore mix proportion accuracy.

## 2.2 Engineering Significance — The "Real-World Utility"

- **Volume Batching Correction:** Multiplies the apparent (loose) volume of moist sand by the bulking factor to obtain the **equivalent dry volume** required.
- **Quality Control on Site:** A sudden abnormal bulking curve indicates excess silt, organic matter, or a change in sand source.
- **Mix Proportioning Audit:** Critical when trial mixes are designed in the lab (dry sand) but implemented on site (moist sand).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Bulking Percentage ($B\%$)** | $B\% = \dfrac{(V_m - V_d)}{V_d} \times 100$ | % | Primary output of the test |
| 2 | **Bulking Factor (BF)** | $BF = \dfrac{V_m}{V_d}$ | dimensionless | Often used in volume correction |
| 3 | **Moisture Content ($w\%$)** | $w\% = \dfrac{(W_w)}{W_s} \times 100$ | % | $W_w$ = weight of water added, $W_s$ = weight of dry sand |
| 4 | **Corrected Sand Volume** | $V_{corrected} = V_{moist} \times \dfrac{1}{BF}$ | ml / m³ | Apply at site batching |
| 5 | **Apparent Density of Moist Sand** | $\rho_{apparent} = \dfrac{W_s}{V_m}$ | g/cc | Decreases at peak bulking |
| 6 | **Surface Moisture Correction** | $W_{agg,actual} = W_{dry,design} \times (1 + w\%)$ | kg | Used in mass batching |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Memorize Formula #1 cold. Nearly every numerical problem on this topic will have a variation of $B\% = \frac{V_m - V_d}{V_d} \times 100$ as the central calculation.

## 2.4 Typical Peak Values (For Quick Recall)

| Sand Type | Maximum Bulking (%) | Peak Moisture Content (%) |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| Fine river sand | 25 – 30 | 5 – 6 |
| Medium river sand | 20 – 25 | 4 – 5 |
| Coarse river sand | 15 – 20 | 3 – 4 |
| Manufactured sand (M-sand) | 15 – 22 | 4 – 6 |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Apparatus Required (KTU Standard List)

- Measuring cylinder — 250 ml or 500 ml capacity (graduated)
- Weighing balance — accuracy $\pm$ 0.1 g
- Dry sand sample — oven-dried, well-graded
- Distilled water — for controlled moisture addition
- Glass rod — for tamping / rodding
- Tray and scoop
- Stopwatch / timer (optional)

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Procedure (Hallmarked for KTU Answer Sheets)

1. **Step A — Dry Reference Volume:**
   - Take the clean, dry measuring cylinder.
   - Fill it with dry sand to a pre-marked level (commonly 200 ml) by *pouring from a height* (no compaction). This is the **loose dry volume $V_d$**.
   - Record $V_d$ to the nearest ml.

2. **Step B — Determination of Sand Mass:**
   - Weigh the dry sand used: $W_s$ in grams.
   - This $W_s$ is **constant throughout the experiment** — it is the reference mass.

3. **Step C — Moisture Increment:**
   - Calculate the weight of water corresponding to **2% moisture**:
     $$W_{w,2\%} = \frac{2}{100} \times W_s = 0.02 \, W_s$$
   - Add this water to the dry sand in a tray, mix thoroughly for ~1 minute to ensure uniform distribution.

4. **Step D — Moist Volume Reading:**
   - Transfer the moist sand into the measuring cylinder (same filling technique).
   - Read the new apparent volume $V_m$ at the top of the sand surface (do not compact).

5. **Step E — Compaction Check:**
   - Gently tamp the cylinder on a cushioned surface or rod with the glass rod.
   - Note the **compacted volume $V_c$** — this validates the experimental readings.

6. **Step F — Repeat for Higher Moisture Contents:**
   - Repeat Steps C–E for moisture contents: 2%, 4%, 6%, 8%, 10%, 12%, 15%, 20%.
   - At each stage, record $w\%$, $V_m$, and $V_c$.

7. **Step G — Plot the Bulking Curve:**
   - X-axis: Moisture content $w\%$ (0 to 20%).
   - Y-axis: Bulking % $B\%$.
   - Identify the peak point.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Bulking Formula

The expression for bulking percentage is derived directly from its definition. Let us walk through the full algebraic reasoning:

### Derivation Start

Let the **dry loose volume** of sand be $V_d$ (ml).

Let the **moist loose volume** of the *same mass* of sand be $V_m$ (ml).

By definition, **bulking is the fractional increase in volume**:

$$
B = \frac{V_m - V_d}{V_d}
$$

Multiplying both numerator and denominator by 100 to express in percentage:

$$
B\% = \frac{V_m - V_d}{V_d} \times 100
$$

> **Logical explanation:** The numerator $(V_m - V_d)$ is the *extra volume gained* due to the moisture film; dividing by $V_d$ normalizes this gain to a *per-unit-dry-volume* basis.

### Useful Algebraic Rearrangement

Sometimes we are given $B\%$ and need to find the corrected dry volume. From the same definition:

$$
B\% = \frac{V_m - V_d}{V_d} \times 100
$$

$$
\frac{B\%}{100} = \frac{V_m - V_d}{V_d}
$$

$$
\frac{B\%}{100} \cdot V_d = V_m - V_d
$$

$$
V_m = V_d \left(1 + \frac{B\%}{100}\right)
$$

$$
V_d = \frac{V_m}{\left(1 + \dfrac{B\%}{100}\right)} = \frac{V_m}{BF}
$$

where $BF = 1 + \dfrac{B\%}{100}$ is the **bulking factor**.

> **Textual logic row:** This rearrangement is the most useful engineering identity because it lets a site engineer convert the loose moist volume measured in a box/headpan back to the *equivalent dry volume* the mix design is based on.

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style Full Solution)

**Problem Statement:**
A sample of dry sand weighing **500 g** is poured loosely into a 500 ml measuring cylinder, occupying a level of **200 ml** at the dry state. When 2% water (by weight of dry sand) is added and mixed uniformly, the same sand occupies **230 ml** in the cylinder (loose). When 6% water is added, the level rises to **250 ml**. Calculate the bulking percentage at 2% and 6% moisture contents. Also calculate the bulking factor in each case.

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Given Data:**
- $W_s = 500$ g
- $V_d = 200$ ml
- $V_m \text{ at } 2\% = 230$ ml
- $V_m \text{ at } 6\% = 250$ ml

**Step 1 — Bulking at 2% moisture:**

$$
B_{2\%} = \frac{V_{m,2} - V_d}{V_d} \times 100
$$

$$
B_{2\%} = \frac{230 - 200}{200} \times 100
$$

$$
B_{2\%} = \frac{30}{200} \times 100
$$

$$
B_{2\%} = 15\%
$$

**Step 2 — Bulking at 6% moisture:**

$$
B_{6\%} = \frac{V_{m,6} - V_d}{V_d} \times 100
$$

$$
B_{6\%} = \frac{250 - 200}{200} \times 100
$$

$$
B_{6\%} = \frac{50}{200} \times 100
$$

$$
B_{6\%} = 25\%
$$

**Step 3 — Bulking Factors:**

$$
BF_{2\%} = 1 + \frac{15}{100} = 1.15
$$

$$
BF_{6\%} = 1 + \frac{25}{100} = 1.25
$$

**Step 4 — Corrected Dry Volume Required (Engineering Application):**

Suppose the site engineer measures a moist sand volume of 250 ml in a head-pan. The dry sand mass required is:

$$
V_{dry,equiv} = \frac{V_{moist}}{BF} = \frac{250}{1.25} = 200 \text{ ml}
$$

This confirms that **the loose moist sand should be multiplied by $\dfrac{1}{BF}$ to get the equivalent dry volume** for batching.

## 3.5 Observation Table (Template Used in KTU Record Books)

| Trial | Water Added (ml) | Moisture $w\%$ | Loose Moist Volume $V_m$ (ml) | Bulking $B\%$ | Bulking Factor $BF$ |
|:-----:|:----------------:|:--------------:|:-----------------------------:|:-------------:|:-------------------:|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 200 | 0 | 1.00 |
| 2 | 10 | 2 | 230 | 15 | 1.15 |
| 3 | 20 | 4 | 245 | 22.5 | 1.225 |
| 4 | 30 | 6 | 250 | 25 | 1.25 |
| 5 | 40 | 8 | 240 | 20 | 1.20 |
| 6 | 50 | 10 | 225 | 12.5 | 1.125 |
| 7 | 60 | 12 | 210 | 5 | 1.05 |
| 8 | 75 | 15 | 200 | 0 | 1.00 |

> [!NOTE]
> The above table is **illustrative**. In the actual lab, students generate their own readings, but the *shape* of the data must resemble a bell curve — rising to a peak around 5–6%, then falling.

## 3.6 Result Format (What to Write in the KTU Record)

- **Maximum Bulking Percentage Observed:** \_\_\_\_\_\_ %
- **Corresponding Moisture Content at Maximum Bulking:** \_\_\_\_\_\_ %
- **Bulking Factor at Site Moisture Content (if known):** \_\_\_\_\_\_
- **Recommendation:** Volume of moist sand to be used at site = (Design dry volume) × (Bulking Factor)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Procedure Flowchart (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[START Test on Bulking of Sand] --> stepB[Weigh Dry Sand Ws]
    stepB --> stepC[Pour Dry Sand into Cylinder]
    stepC --> stepD[Record Loose Dry Volume Vd]
    stepD --> stepE[Add 2 percent Water by Weight]
    stepE --> stepF[Mix Thoroughly in Tray]
    stepF --> stepG[Pour into Cylinder Same Way]
    stepG --> stepH[Record Loose Moist Volume Vm]
    stepH --> stepI[Compute Bulking B percent = Vm minus Vd over Vd times 100]
    stepI --> stepJ{Is Moisture less than 20 percent?}
    stepJ -- Yes --> stepK[Increment Moisture by 2 percent]
    stepK --> stepE
    stepJ -- No --> stepL[Plot B percent vs Moisture Curve]
    stepL --> stepM[Identify Peak Bulking Point]
    stepM --> stepN[Compute Bulking Factor BF = 1 plus B percent over 100]
    stepN --> stepO[END Submit Lab Report]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Diagram — Three Stages of Bulking

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stateA[STAGE A DRY SAND] -->|0 percent Moisture| stateA
    stateB[STAGE B PEAK BULKING] -->|4 to 6 percent Moisture| stateB
    stateC[STAGE C SATURATED SAND] -->|Above 10 percent Moisture| stateC
    stateA --> stateB
    stateB --> stateC
    stateA -- Vd Reference --> noteD[Volume Vd is Minimum]
```
