```mermaid
    stateB -- Vm Maximum --> noteE[Volume Vm is Maximum]
    stateC -- Vm Approaches Vd --> noteF[Volume Returns toward Vd]
```

## 4.3 Apparatus Block Architecture (Functional Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[INPUT SIDE]
        drySand[Oven Dried Sand Sample]
        distWater[Distilled Water in Beaker]
        weighingBal[Weighing Balance plus 0.1 g Accuracy]
    end
    subgraph B[MEASUREMENT SIDE]
        cyl[Graduated Measuring Cylinder 500 ml]
        glassRod[Glass Tamping Rod]
        tray[Mixing Tray and Scoop]
    end
    subgraph C[OUTPUT SIDE]
        obsTable[Observation Table of Vm versus w percent]
        bulkingCurve[Bulking Curve Graph]
        bfValue[Computed Bulking Factor]
    end
    drySand --> weighingBal
    distWater --> tray
    weighingBal --> tray
    tray --> cyl
    glassRod --> cyl
    cyl --> obsTable
    obsTable --> bulkingCurve
    bulkingCurve --> bfValue
```

## 4.4 Verbal Description of the Bulking Curve (For Lab Records)

- **X-axis:** Moisture content $w\%$ ranging from 0% to 20%.
- **Y-axis:** Bulking percentage $B\%$ ranging from 0% to ~30%.
- **Shape:** Bell-shaped (unimodal) curve.
- **Origin:** Starts at (0, 0) — zero moisture gives zero bulking.
- **Rising limb:** Steep rise from 0% to ~5–6% moisture.
- **Peak:** Maximum bulking at 4–6% moisture, value 20–30%.
- **Falling limb:** Gradual fall from peak to ~12% moisture, then plateau.
- **Tail:** Approaches zero (or slightly negative) at saturation (~20%+).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "bulking of sand". State the approximate range of maximum bulking for ordinary river sand and the moisture content at which it occurs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Bulking of sand is the increase in the apparent volume of sand caused by the formation of a thin moisture film around the sand grains, which pushes the particles apart and increases the bulk volume of the same mass of sand.
- **Range of maximum bulking (1 mark):** Approximately **20% to 30%** for ordinary river sand.
- **Moisture content at peak (0.5 mark):** Approximately **4% to 6%** by weight of dry sand.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`
**Why is the bulking test conducted before batching concrete by volume? What correction is applied at the site?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Reason (1.5 marks):** When sand is moist, it occupies a larger volume than dry sand for the same mass. If volume batching is done using this increased (apparent) volume, the actual mass of sand used is **less** than the design mix, leading to a sand-deficient (harsh and weak) concrete.
- **Correction (1.5 marks):** The volume of moist sand is divided by the **bulking factor** ($BF = 1 + B\%/100$) to obtain the equivalent dry volume, or alternatively the design dry volume is multiplied by the bulking factor to obtain the required apparent volume of moist sand to be batched.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`
**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the laboratory procedure to determine the **bulking of sand**. State the apparatus, observations, and the formula used. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** In a bulking test, dry sand weighing 600 g occupied a loose volume of 250 ml. After adding 4% water, the volume increased to 290 ml. After adding 8% water, the volume was 270 ml. Calculate the bulking percentage and bulking factor at 4% and 8% moisture. At which moisture content is the bulking maximum in this case? **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution — (a) Procedure

1. **Apparatus (2 marks):** Measuring cylinder (250/500 ml), weighing balance, dry sand, distilled water, glass rod, mixing tray, scoop.
2. **Procedure (4 marks):**
   - Determine the dry loose volume $V_d$ of a known mass of dry sand in the measuring cylinder.
   - Add 2% water (by weight of dry sand), mix uniformly in a tray.
   - Transfer the moist sand to the cylinder, read the new loose volume $V_m$.
   - Tamp gently with the glass rod to record the compacted volume $V_c$.
   - Repeat for 4%, 6%, 8%, 10%, 12%, 15%, 20% moisture contents.
3. **Formula (1 mark):**
   $$B\% = \frac{V_m - V_d}{V_d} \times 100$$

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical

**Given:** $V_d = 250$ ml, $V_m$ at 4% = 290 ml, $V_m$ at 8% = 270 ml.

**At 4% moisture:**

$$
B_{4\%} = \frac{290 - 250}{250} \times 100 = \frac{40}{250} \times 100 = 16\%
$$

$$
BF_{4\%} = 1 + \frac{16}{100} = 1.16
$$

**At 8% moisture:**

$$
B_{8\%} = \frac{270 - 250}{250} \times 100 = \frac{20}{250} \times 100 = 8\%
$$

$$
BF_{8\%} = 1 + \frac{8}{100} = 1.08
$$

**Conclusion:** Bulking is **higher at 4% (16%)** than at 8% (8%) in this sand. Hence the maximum bulking is **at least 16%** (likely at moisture content between 4% and 6%, since the curve is past its peak at 8%).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (a):**
> Students often forget to mention that the **filling technique must be uniform** (same drop height, no compaction during pouring). Examiners award zero marks if you state "fill the cylinder" without clarifying that loose pouring is essential — otherwise the volume reading is not reproducible.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (b):**
> A common error is computing $B\% = \dfrac{V_m}{V_d} \times 100$ (treating it as a direct ratio). The correct formula is $\dfrac{V_m - V_d}{V_d} \times 100$. This single mistake will cost **3 to 4 marks** in a 7-mark question. Always subtract the dry volume first.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative). `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**(a)** What is the **bulking factor**? How is the bulking of sand experimentally determined in the laboratory? Discuss the **engineering significance** of bulking with respect to volume batching of concrete. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** The design mix of M25 concrete requires 450 kg of dry sand per cubic metre of concrete. At the site, the sand has a moisture content of 5% and the corresponding bulking is 22%. Determine:
- (i) The actual mass of moist sand to be batched per cubic metre of concrete. **\[3.5 Marks\]**
- (ii) The apparent (loose) volume occupied by this moist sand if the bulk density of moist sand is 1.45 g/cc. **\[3.5 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution — (a) Theory

1. **Bulking Factor (2 marks):** It is the ratio of the loose volume of moist sand to the loose volume of the same mass of dry sand:
   $$BF = \frac{V_m}{V_d} = 1 + \frac{B\%}{100}$$
2. **Experimental Determination (3 marks):** (As detailed in Section 3.2 above — measure $V_d$ for dry sand, then incrementally add water and measure $V_m$, compute $B\%$, plot the bulking curve.)
3. **Engineering Significance (2 marks):** Bulking causes volume batching to under-dose the sand. Correction via $BF$ restores mix proportions and prevents a sand-deficient, weak concrete.

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical

**Given:** $W_{dry,design} = 450$ kg, $w\% = 5\%$, $B\% = 22\%$, $\rho_{moist,bulk} = 1.45$ g/cc.

**Part (i) — Actual mass of moist sand to batch:**

$$
W_{moist} = W_{dry,design} \times \left(1 + \frac{w\%}{100}\right)
$$

$$
W_{moist} = 450 \times \left(1 + \frac{5}{100}\right)
$$

$$
W_{moist} = 450 \times 1.05
$$

$$
W_{moist} = 472.5 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Part (ii) — Apparent volume of moist sand:**

$$
V_{apparent} = \frac{W_{moist}}{\rho_{moist,bulk}}
$$

Convert mass to grams and density to g/cc consistently:

$$
V_{apparent} = \frac{472.5 \times 1000 \text{ g}}{1.45 \text{ g/cc}} = 325.86 \text{ litres/m}^3 = 0.326 \text{ m}^3
$$

Equivalently, using the bulking factor approach:

$$
BF = 1 + \frac{22}{100} = 1.22
$$

$$
V_{apparent} = \frac{V_{dry,equiv}}{1} \times BF = \text{(equivalent dry volume)} \times 1.22
$$

Both methods give the same engineering conclusion: **the engineer must measure ~1.22 times the design dry volume of moist sand to obtain the correct mass.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B (b):**
> Students often forget to convert **kg/m³ to g/cc consistently**. Density = 1.45 g/cc and mass = 472.5 kg = 472500 g. The volume is then in **cc**, which converts to **litres** (÷1000) or m³. Losing this unit conversion typically costs 1 full mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition:** Bulking = increase in apparent volume of sand due to moisture films.
- [x] **Peak Bulking:** ~20% to 30% for river sand, at 4%–6% moisture.
- [x] **Core Formula:** $B\% = \dfrac{V_m - V_d}{V_d} \times 100$.
- [x] **Bulking Factor:** $BF = 1 + \dfrac{B\%}{100}$.
- [x] **Site Correction:** Use $V_{moist} \times \dfrac{1}{BF}$ to get equivalent dry volume.
- [x] **Apparatus:** Measuring cylinder, weighing balance, dry sand, water, glass rod, tray.
- [x] **Procedure Mantra:** "Same mass, same pouring height, no compaction during loose-volume readings."
- [x] **IS Code:** IS 2386 (Part III) – 1963 governs this test.
- [x] **Curve Shape:** Bell-shaped, peaks at ~5% moisture.
- [x] **Practical Risk:** Ignoring bulking → sand-deficient concrete → low strength, harsh mix.
- [x] **Common Mistake:** Using $V_m / V_d$ instead of $(V_m - V_d) / V_d$ for bulking %.
- [x] **Memory Hook:** "**Moisture is a pillow, not a filler.**" Pillow effect peaks early; once voids fill, the volume collapses.
- [x] **Volume vs Mass Batching:** Bulking correction is irrelevant in *mass* batching; critical in *volume* batching.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test on Coarse Aggregate Crushing Value
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL508 - CONCRETE LAB (MT-2)
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Test on Coarse Aggregate Crushing Value

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV)** test is a standardized mechanical test conducted on coarse aggregates to determine their resistance to crushing under a gradually applied compressive load. As per the Bureau of Indian Standard **IS 2386 (Part IV) – 1963**, the ACV of an aggregate is defined as the percentage of the crushed (finer) material obtained from the test sample in relation to the total weight of the original test sample, when subjected to a standardized load of **400 kN applied over a period of 10 minutes**.

$$ACV = \frac{M_2}{M_1} \times 100 \quad (\%)$$

Where:
- $M_1$ = Total mass of the dry aggregate sample taken for the test (typically around **3400 g** as per IS standard).
- $M_2$ = Mass of the aggregate fraction passing through the **2.36 mm IS Sieve** after the crushing load is applied.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The ACV test is a **destructive mechanical test** that gives an indirect measure of the **compressive strength** and **toughness** of coarse aggregates used in the production of concrete and road construction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you have a handful of small rocks (coarse aggregate) used to make concrete. When heavy construction vehicles drive over a road made of these aggregates, the rocks must not crumble into dust. The **Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV) test** is essentially a controlled laboratory simulation of this real-world crushing action.

**Real-World Analogy — The Apple Press Test:**

- Think of an **apple press** used in a kitchen. When pressure is applied slowly and steadily, a *strong* apple resists and only a small amount of juice is squeezed out. A *rotten* or *soft* apple, however, will collapse into pulp, yielding a much larger quantity of juice.
- In the ACV test, the **aggregate is the apple**, the **hydraulic press is the load applicator**, and the **crushed material passing the 2.36 mm sieve is the "juice" (crushed fines)**.
- A **low ACV** = Strong aggregate (like a firm apple — very little crushed material).
- A **high ACV** = Weak aggregate (like a rotten apple — lot of fines produced).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights):**
> - **Standard Load:** 400 kN (applied at a uniform rate over 10 minutes).
> - **Sieve Used for Fines:** 2.36 mm IS Sieve.
> - **Typical Test Sample Weight:** ~3400 g (exact weight $M_1$ to be recorded).
> - **Recommended Maximum ACV for Concrete:** **30%** (as per IS 383).
> - **Recommended Maximum ACV for Road Sub-base (WBM):** Up to **45%** permissible.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of the Test

The test exploits the fundamental principle that under a uniaxial compressive load, weaker aggregate particles will fracture, split, and disintegrate, while stronger particles will resist and remain largely intact. By measuring the proportion of broken-down material, engineers can quantify aggregate quality.

### Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic:

1. **Sample Selection & Preparation:**
   - Aggregate passing **12.5 mm** and retained on **10 mm** IS Sieve is selected.
   - This standardized grading ensures uniform particle size and minimizes variability in test results.

2. **Filling the Cylindrical Mould:**
   - The aggregate is placed in a **standard cylindrical mould** of internal diameter **152 mm** (6 inches) and height **115 mm** (4.5 inches).
   - The mould is filled in **3 equal layers**, with each layer tamped **25 times** using a standard tamping rod.

3. **Application of Load:**
   - The plunger (piston) is placed on top of the aggregate, and the entire assembly is placed in a **compression testing machine (CTM)**.
   - A load of **400 kN** is applied **uniformly at a rate of 400 kN/min** (i.e., over 10 minutes).

4. **Sieving & Weighing:**
   - After load release, the crushed aggregate is removed, sieved through the **2.36 mm IS Sieve**, and the weight of fines ($M_2$) is recorded.

5. **Calculation:**
   - The ratio $M_2 / M_1$ multiplied by 100 gives the ACV.

## 2.2 Why ACV Matters in Engineering

- **Concrete Durability:** Aggregates with high ACV will produce concrete prone to cracking under load.
- **Road Construction:** Used to determine suitability of aggregate for flexible and rigid pavement layers.
- **Quality Control:** A low ACV indicates an aggregate with high resistance to crushing → safe for use in high-strength concrete.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Value / Unit | KTU Quick Memory Tip |
|-----------|--------|--------------|----------------------|
| Aggregate Crushing Value | $ACV$ | Percentage (%) | Low = Strong, High = Weak |
| Total dry weight of sample | $M_1$ | grams (g) | Record **before** the test |
| Weight of fines (passing 2.36 mm) | $M_2$ | grams (g) | Record **after** the test |
| ACV Formula | $ACV = (M_2 / M_1) \times 100$ | % | Always compute ratio FIRST, then ×100 |
| Standard Test Load | $P$ | **400 kN** | Applied uniformly over 10 minutes |
| Internal mould diameter | $D$ | 152 mm (6 in) | Standard IS 2386 (Part IV) |
| Mould height | $H$ | 115 mm (4.5 in) | Fill in 3 layers, 25 tamps each |
| Aggregate size for test | - | Passing 12.5 mm & Retained 10 mm | Single-sized graded sample |
| Sieve for fines | - | **2.36 mm IS Sieve** | Critical for measuring $M_2$ |
| Max ACV (concrete, IS 383) | $ACV_{max}$ | **30%** | Higher → reject aggregate |
| Max ACV (WBM, IS 2386) | $ACV_{max,WBM}$ | **45%** | Water Bound Macadam roads |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Trick Question:** "Define the Aggregate Impact Value (AIV) and ACV. Why are they both used together?" — AIV tests resistance to **sudden impact**, while ACV tests resistance to **gradual compression**. They are complementary.

## 2.4 Apparatus Required (Mandatory for KTU Lab Records)

1. **Compression Testing Machine (CTM)** – Capacity 2000 kN minimum.
2. **Cylindrical Mould** – Internal Ø = 152 mm, Height = 115 mm with detachable base plate.
3. **Tamping Rod** – 16 mm diameter, 45–60 cm long with rounded end.
4. **Plunger (Piston)** – Fits inside the cylindrical mould, transmits load uniformly.
5. **IS Sieves** – 12.5 mm, 10 mm, 2.36 mm, plus a receiver pan.
6. **Weighing Balance** – Accuracy ± 1 g.
7. **Drying Oven** – For drying the aggregate to a constant weight.
8. **Stopwatch** – For timing the 10-minute load application.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example

> **Sample Problem (KTU Exam Standard — 14 Mark Question):**
> *In an Aggregate Crushing Value test, the initial dry weight of the aggregate sample was found to be 3420 g. After applying the standard load of 400 kN for 10 minutes, the crushed aggregate was sieved, and the mass passing the 2.36 mm IS sieve was 720 g. Calculate the Aggregate Crushing Value and comment on the suitability of the aggregate for use in concrete construction as per IS 383.*

### Step 1: Identify the Given Data

From the problem statement, we extract the values:
- Initial dry weight of aggregate sample: $M_1 = 3420 \text{ g}$
- Weight of crushed material passing 2.36 mm sieve: $M_2 = 720 \text{ g}$
- Test load: $P = 400 \text{ kN}$
- Sieve size: 2.36 mm (IS)

### Step 2: Apply the ACV Formula

The fundamental formula defined by IS 2386 (Part IV) is:

$$ACV = \frac{M_2}{M_1} \times 100 \quad (\%)$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Values

Substituting the given numerical values into the formula:

$$ACV = \frac{720 \text{ g}}{3420 \text{ g}} \times 100 \quad (\%)$$

### Step 4: Perform the Division

We first compute the dimensionless ratio $M_2 / M_1$:

$$\frac{720}{3420} = 0.21052...$$

### Step 5: Multiply by 100 to Convert to Percentage

$$ACV = 0.21052 \times 100 = 21.05\%$$

### Step 6: Interpretation as per IS 383

The IS 383 specification mandates that for coarse aggregates used in normal concrete, the ACV shall **not exceed 30%**.

Since our computed value $ACV = 21.05\% < 30\%$, the aggregate is **suitable for use in concrete**.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix (ACV Interpretation)

| Computed ACV Range | Aggregate Classification | Recommended Use |
|--------------------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| **< 20%** | Very Strong Aggregate | High-strength concrete, heavy-duty pavements |
| **20% – 30%** | Strong Aggregate | Normal concrete (acceptable per IS 383) |
| **30% – 45%** | Moderately Strong | Sub-base in WBM roads, lean concrete |
| **> 45%** | Weak Aggregate | Generally rejected; unsuitable for structural use |

## 3.3 Detailed Comparison: ACV vs. AIV vs. 10% Fines Value

| Property | ACV | AIV | 10% Fines Value |
|----------|-----|-----|-----------------|
| Test type | Gradual compression | Sudden impact | Gradual compression |
| Load | 400 kN | 14 kg hammer drop | Load to produce 10% fines |
| Sieve | 2.36 mm | 2.36 mm | 2.36 mm |
| Indicates | Compressive resistance | Impact resistance | Toughness |
| IS Code | IS 2386 (Part IV) | IS 2386 (Part IV) | IS 2386 (Part IV) |
| Typical limit (concrete) | ≤ 30% | ≤ 30% | ≥ 100 kN (minimum) |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Coarse Aggregate Sample"] --> B["Sieve: Pass 12.5 mm and Retain 10 mm"]
    B --> C["Dry in Oven at 100-110 deg C for 24 hrs"]
    C --> D["Weigh and Record M1 (~3400 g)"]
    D --> E["Fill Cylindrical Mould in 3 Layers"]
    E --> F["Tamp Each Layer 25 Times with 16 mm Rod"]
    F --> G["Level Surface and Place Plunger"]
    G --> H["Transfer to Compression Testing Machine"]
    H --> I["Apply Load 400 kN Uniformly in 10 minutes"]
    I --> J["Release Load and Remove Crushed Aggregate"]
    J --> K["Sieved Through 2.36 mm IS Sieve"]
    K --> L["Weigh and Record M2 (Fines)"]
    L --> M["Calculate ACV = M2 divided by M1 times 100"]
    M --> N{"Is ACV less than 30 percent?"}
    N -->|Yes| O["Acceptable for Concrete Per IS 383"]
    N -->|No| P["Rejected: Too Weak for Concrete"]
    O --> Q["End: Aggregate Approved"]
    P --> R["End: Aggregate Rejected"]
```

## 4.2 Cylindrical Mould Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M["STANDARD CYLINDRICAL TEST MOULD — IS 2386 Part IV"]
        P1["Plunger / Piston (Top)"] --> P2["Aggregate Bed (3 Layers, Tamped 25x)"]
        P2 --> P3["Base Plate (Detachable)"]
    end
    P1 --> P4["Load Applied: 400 kN"]
    P4 --> P5["Compression Testing Machine"]
    P5 --> P6["Crushed Fines Fall Through 2.36 mm Sieve"]
```

## 4.3 Precautions & Sources of Error (Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X["Common Errors Affecting ACV Result"] --> Y1["Non-uniform tamping leads to density variation"]
    X --> Y2["Moisture in sample changes M1 and M2 readings"]
    X --> Y3["Loading rate too fast or too slow"]
    X --> Y4["Plunger not seated properly on aggregate surface"]
    Y1 --> Z["Result: Inaccurate ACV Calculation"]
    Y2 --> Z
    Y3 --> Z
    Y4 --> Z
    Z --> W["Mitigation: Strict Adherence to IS 2386 Part IV Procedure"]
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the term "Aggregate Crushing Value" as per IS 2386 (Part IV). State its standard formula. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

> The Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV) of a coarse aggregate is defined, as per **IS 2386 (Part IV) – 1963**, as the ratio of the weight of the crushed material passing through the **2.36 mm IS Sieve** to the total weight of the test sample, expressed as a percentage, when a standard load of **400 kN** is applied over **10 minutes** through a plunger.
>
> **Formula:** $ACV = (M_2 / M_1) \times 100$, where $M_1$ = initial weight, $M_2$ = weight of fines.

---

### Q2. List the apparatus required for conducting the Aggregate Crushing Value test. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> The apparatus required are:
> 1. Compression Testing Machine (CTM) of capacity ≥ 2000 kN.
> 2. Cylindrical mould (152 mm dia, 115 mm height) with detachable base plate.
> 3. Tamping rod (16 mm dia, rounded end).
> 4. Plunger/piston fitting the mould.
> 5. IS Sieves: 12.5 mm, 10 mm, 2.36 mm, receiver pan, and lid.
> 6. Weighing balance with accuracy ± 1 g.
> 7. Drying oven (100–110 °C).
> 8. Stopwatch.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> **(a)** Explain the procedure for determining the **Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV)** of coarse aggregate as per IS 2386 (Part IV). Mention all precautions to be taken. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** In an ACV test, the weight of the aggregate sample was **3325 g**. After applying the standard load, the weight of fines passing the 2.36 mm sieve was **760 g**. Calculate the ACV and comment on the suitability of the aggregate for concrete. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:

The procedure to determine the Aggregate Crushing Value is as follows:

1. **Sample Preparation:** Take about **6500 g** of coarse aggregate sample. Sieve it through **12.5 mm and 10 mm IS Sieves**. Use the fraction passing 12.5 mm and retained on 10 mm. Place in an oven at **100–110 °C** for 24 hours, then cool to room temperature.

2. **Filling the Mould:** Place the cylindrical mould on a flat surface. Fill in **3 equal layers**, with each layer tamped **25 times** using a 16 mm tamping rod. Strike off the surface level.

3. **Placing the Plunger:** Place the plunger gently on top of the aggregate, ensuring even contact.

4. **Loading:** Place the assembly in the **Compression Testing Machine (CTM)**. Apply a load of **400 kN uniformly over 10 minutes** (40 kN/min).

5. **Discharge and Sieving:** After load release, carefully remove the crushed aggregate. Sieve it through the **2.36 mm IS Sieve** until no further material passes. Weigh the material passing the sieve ($M_2$).

6. **Calculation:** $ACV = (M_2 / M_1) \times 100$.

**Precautions:**
- Aggregate must be **oven-dry** before testing.
- Tamping must be done uniformly **25 strokes per layer**.
- Load must be applied at a **uniform rate** (40 kN/min).
- Sieving must be continued until **no more fines pass**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:

**Given Data:**
- Initial weight of sample: $M_1 = 3325 \text{ g}$
- Weight of fines (passing 2.36 mm): $M_2 = 760 \text{ g}$

**Applying the ACV formula:**

$$ACV = \frac{M_2}{M_1} \times 100 = \frac{760}{3325} \times 100$$

$$\frac{760}{3325} = 0.2285$$

$$ACV = 0.2285 \times 100 = 22.85\%$$

**Comment:** Since the ACV is **22.85%**, which is **less than the 30% maximum limit** prescribed by IS 383 for concrete aggregates, the aggregate is **suitable for use in concrete construction**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> **(a)** Differentiate between **Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV)** and **Aggregate Impact Value (AIV)** tests. Mention typical permissible limits for both as per IS standards. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** A coarse aggregate sample weighing **3500 g** was tested for ACV. After loading, the crushed material retained on the 2.36 mm sieve weighed **2860 g**. Calculate the ACV and state whether the aggregate is acceptable for concrete as per IS 383. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:

| Feature | ACV Test | AIV Test |
|---------|----------|----------|
| Type of loading | Gradual compressive load | Sudden impact load |
| Apparatus | Compression Testing Machine (400 kN) | Impact Testing Machine (13.6 kg hammer, 380 mm drop) |
| Load / Impact | 400 kN over 10 min | 15 blows of hammer |
| Aggregate size | 12.5 mm – 10 mm | 12.5 mm – 10 mm |
| Sieve | 2.36 mm | 2.36 mm |
| Formula | $ACV = (M_2 / M_1) \times 100$ | $AIV = (M_2 / M_1) \times 100$ |
| Indicates | Resistance to compression | Resistance to impact / shock |
| Permissible limit (concrete) | ≤ **30%** (IS 383) | ≤ **30%** (IS 383) |
| Permissible limit (WBM roads) | ≤ **45%** | ≤ **35%** |

**Engineering Significance:** ACV gives an idea about the **compressive strength** of the aggregate, while AIV measures the **toughness** against sudden shock. Both are essential for quality control in concrete and pavement construction.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:

**Given Data:**
- Total weight of sample: $M_1 = 3500 \text{ g}$
- Weight of material **retained on 2.36 mm sieve** (i.e., *uncrushed* fraction) = $2860 \text{ g}$

**Step 1: Calculate weight of fines (passing 2.36 mm):**

$$M_2 = M_1 - \text{Weight retained on 2.36 mm} = 3500 - 2860 = 640 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2: Apply ACV formula:**

$$ACV = \frac{M_2}{M_1} \times 100 = \frac{640}{3500} \times 100$$

$$\frac{640}{3500} = 0.1829$$

$$ACV = 0.1829 \times 100 = 18.29\%$$

**Step 3: Comment:**

Since $ACV = 18.29\% < 30\%$ (IS 383 limit), the aggregate is **acceptable for use in concrete**. In fact, with such a low ACV, this is a **strong aggregate** suitable for high-strength concrete applications.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $M_2$:** Many students mistakenly take the weight **retained on** the 2.36 mm sieve as $M_2$. **$M_2$ is the weight PASSING the 2.36 mm sieve** (the crushed fines). This single error can cost 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Always state ACV in **%** (percentage). Writing a bare ratio (e.g., 0.21) is a mark deduction.
> 3. **Skipping the IS Code reference:** KTU board examiners expect "as per **IS 2386 (Part IV) – 1963**". A missing IS code reference is a 0.5–1 mark loss.
> 4. **Not stating the load:** The standard load is **400 kN applied over 10 minutes**, not 400 kg or 40 tonnes. Check units carefully.
> 5. **Skipping the interpretation/comment:** Always end your numerical answer with a comment on acceptability as per **IS 383** (limit ≤ 30%).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **ACV** = Aggregate Crushing Value, a measure of resistance of coarse aggregate to **gradual compressive load**.
- ✅ **IS Code:** IS 2386 (Part IV) – 1963.
- ✅ **Aggregate size for test:** Passing **12.5 mm**, retained on **10 mm** IS Sieve.
- ✅ **Standard Load:** **400 kN** applied **uniformly over 10 minutes**.
- ✅ **Cylindrical Mould:** Internal diameter **152 mm**, height **115 mm**.
- ✅ **Filling in 3 layers**, each tamped **25 times** with a **16 mm tamping rod**.
- ✅ **Sieve for fines:** **2.36 mm IS Sieve**.
- ✅ **Formula:** $ACV = (M_2 / M_1) \times 100$, where $M_2$ is **weight of fines (passing 2.36 mm)**.
- ✅ **$M_1$** = Initial weight of dry sample (~3400 g).
- ✅ **$M_2$** = Weight of crushed material passing 2.36 mm sieve.
- ✅ **Permissible Limit (Concrete, IS 383):** ACV ≤ **30%**.
- ✅ **Permissible Limit (WBM Roads):** ACV ≤ **45%**.
- ✅ **Low ACV = Strong aggregate; High ACV = Weak aggregate.**
- ✅ **Always dry the sample** in an oven at 100–110 °C for 24 hours before testing.
- ✅ **ACV vs. AIV:** ACV → gradual compression; AIV → sudden impact. Both have a typical limit of 30% for concrete.
- ✅ **For 10% Fines Value test:** Minimum acceptable value is **100 kN** for concrete.
- ✅ **Common examiner traps:** Wrong sieve for $M_2$ calculation, missing IS code, no interpretation comment.

---

**End of KTU Module Experiment 2 Notes — Aggregate Crushing Value Test (PCCEL508).**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (CIVIL) Outcome-Based Education Standards.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tests on Fresh Concrete: Measurement of Workability of Concrete by Slump Cone Test and Compacting Factor Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCEL508 - CONCRETE LAB (MT-2) > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Tests on Fresh Concrete: Measurement of Workability of Concrete by Slump Cone Test and Compacting Factor Test # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Fresh Concrete & Workability — The Foundational Definitions > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (IS 6461-Part VII & IS 1199)** > **Workability** of fresh concrete is defined as the property of freshly mixed concrete that determines the ease and homogeneity with which it can be mixed, transported, placed, compacted, and finished without segregation. **Workability** is not a single measurable property but a composite characteristic encompassing: - **Consistency** — the wetness or fluidity of the mix - **Mobility** — the ability to flow around reinforcement - **Compactability** — the ease with which air voids can be removed - **Finishability** — the ease of achieving a smooth surface > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Definition (Exact IS Code Wording)** > *The slump test and compacting factor test are both categorized as "Workability Tests" under IS 1199:1959 (Methods of Sampling and Analysis of Concrete). They are classified as empirical/physical tests that measure different facets of the same property.* ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Honey vs. Peanut Butter" Model Imagine you are trying to spread a substance on a slice of bread: - **Honey** (very high workability) flows on its own, fills every corner, requires zero effort — but if you tilt the bread, it will run off and segregate. - **Peanut Butter** (medium workability) needs gentle pushing but stays where you place it — it does not segregate easily. - **Cold Clay** (low workability) needs vigorous effort to spread; air pockets remain trapped inside, leading to honeycomb in concrete. **Workability is the engineering "Goldilocks Zone"** — too fluid causes segregation (honey problem), too stiff causes honeycombing (clay problem). The two tests we study today measure this property in two fundamentally different ways: - **Slump Cone Test** → measures how much the concrete "sinks" under its own weight (gravitational flow). - **Compacting Factor Test** → measures how much the concrete compacts under a standard amount of drop energy (dynamic compaction). > [!TIP] > **Real-World Engineering Significance** > In a heavily reinforced beam-column junction at a Kochi metro station, concrete must flow through 25 mm gaps between rebar. If workability is too low → honeycomb voids → structural failure. If too high → segregation → weak cover → corrosion. This is why *every* batch of ready-mix concrete is tested for workability at the site before placement. ## 1.3 IS Code Standards Referenced (KTU Mandatory) | Standard Code | Year | Title | |---|---|---| | **IS 1199** | 1959 | Methods of Sampling & Analysis of Concrete | | **IS 6461 (Part VII)** | 1973 | Glossary of Terms Relating to Cement Concrete | | **IS 7320** | 1974 | Specification for Concrete Slump Test Apparatus | | **IS 5515** | 1983 | Specification for Compacting Factor Apparatus | > [!WARNING] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Trap** > Many students write "IS 1199:2018" — the *correct* IS code for the slump cone test apparatus itself is **IS 7320:1974**. Always cite both when defining the apparatus. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 TEST 1 — SLUMP CONE TEST (IS 1199:1959, IS 7320:1974) ### 2.1.1 Conceptual Logic of the Test The slump cone test measures the **vertical subsidence (slump)** of a frustum-shaped concrete sample when the mould is lifted. The logic chain is: 1. Place a fixed volume of concrete (in a standardized cone) under standard conditions. 2. Remove the lateral support (the mould) by lifting it vertically. 3. The concrete deforms under its **own self-weight** (gravitational shear). 4. The amount of vertical settlement is a proxy for workability. > A *more workable* mix collapses more → **higher slump**. > A *stiffer* mix retains the cone shape → **lower slump**. ### 2.1.2 Apparatus Specification (Memory Anchor) | Component | Specification (Strict IS 7320) | |---|---| | **Mould Shape** | Hollow frustum (truncated cone) | | **Bottom Diameter** | **200 mm** | | **Top Diameter** | **100 mm** | | **Height** | **300 mm** | | **Wall Thickness** | **1.5 mm (minimum)** | | **Foot Pedals / Handles** | Two foot pieces at bottom + Two lifting handles at top | | **Tamping Rod** | **16 mm** diameter, **600 mm** long, **bullet-nosed** (rounded tip) | | **Base Plate** | Smooth, flat, non-absorbent steel plate | ### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Procedure 1. **Clean & Dampen** the inner surface of the mould and the base plate (no free water should be visible). 2. Place the mould on a **flat, level, non-absorbent** surface and hold it firmly by standing on the foot pieces. 3. **Layer 1**: Fill the mould to **1/3 height (~100 mm)**. Tamp **25 times** uniformly across the cross-section with the rounded tip of the rod. 4. **Layer 2**: Fill to **2/3 height (~200 mm)**. Tamp **25 times**, with the rod just penetrating into Layer 1. 5. **Layer 3**: Heap above the top rim. Tamp **25 times**. Strike off the surface flush with a trowel. 6. **Lift the mould** vertically upward in **5 to 10 seconds** with a steady, smooth motion (no twisting or lateral movement). 7. Place the empty mould **beside** the slumped concrete. 8. Measure the **vertical difference** between the top of the mould and the **highest point of the subsided concrete** using the tamping rod as a straight edge. 9. **Record** the slump in **mm** to the nearest **5 mm**. 10. **Tap the side** of the concrete gently with the rod — observe the type of failure (true/shear/collapse). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why 3 layers and 25 strokes each?** > IS 1199 prescribes this exact protocol because the *degree of compaction* within the mould must be standardized; otherwise, two labs testing the "same" concrete could get different slumps. This is also why the test is called an **empirical** test (results valid only when the *exact* procedure is followed). ### 2.1.4 Three Types of Slump (Visual Memory Aid) | Type | Visual Description | Interpretation | Validity | |---|---|---|---| | **True Slump** | Concrete subsides uniformly, retains cone shape | Indicates a **cohesive, well-proportioned** mix | ✅ **Valid test** — record the value | | **Shear Slump** | Top portion shears off and slides sideways | Indicates a **harsh or deficient mix** | ⚠️ **Re-do** the test; if again shear → report as shear slump | | **Collapse Slump** | Concrete collapses completely | Indicates a **very wet, over-sanded, or segregated** mix | ❌ **Invalid** — repeat with lower w/c ratio | ## 2.2 TEST 2 — COMPACTING FACTOR TEST (IS 1199:1959, IS 5515:1983) ### 2.2.1 Conceptual Logic of the Test The slump cone fails to give reliable results for **stiff, low-workability, or dry mixes** (e.g., road pavements, mass concrete dams). The Compacting Factor Test was developed by **Glanville, Collins & Matthews (1947)** at the UK Road Research Laboratory to address this gap. The principle: Concrete is allowed to fall through **two hoppers** under gravity, then the cylinder is vibrated. The ratio of *partially compacted* to *fully compacted* weight gives the **Compacting Factor (CF)** — a direct measure of how easily the mix consolidates. ### 2.2.2 Apparatus Specification | Component | Geometry & Dimension | |---|---| | **Upper Hopper** | Top: rectangular (254 mm × 254 mm) / Cylindrical top: **254 mm** dia. Bottom opening with **130 mm × 130 mm** rectangular hatch | | **Lower Hopper** | Top opening **230 mm × 230 mm** rectangular / Cylindrical top: **229 mm** dia. Bottom opening: **130 mm × 130 mm** rectangular hatch | | **Cylinder** | **152.5 mm** internal diameter × **305 mm** height | | **Distance between hoppers** | **200 mm** (between bottom of upper hopper and top of lower hopper) | | **Distance between lower hopper and cylinder** | **200 mm** | | **Hatches** | Quick-release, fitted with hinges and rubber seals to prevent leakage | | **Cylinder base** | Removable, has handles for easy weighing | ### 2.2.3 Step-by-Step Procedure 1. **Weigh the empty cylinder** with base plate: Let this be $W_1$. 2. Place the cylinder directly below the lower hopper. 3. **Close the lower hopper hatch**. Fill the upper hopper gently with a representative sample of fresh concrete (about **6 to 7 kg**). 4. **Open the upper hopper hatch** — concrete falls into the lower hopper. 5. Wait **2 minutes** to allow air to escape. 6. **Open the lower hopper hatch** — concrete falls into the cylinder, with the **top heaped and overflowing** the rim. 7. **Cut off the excess** concrete flush with the cylinder rim using a trowel (no compaction). 8. **Weigh the cylinder + partially compacted concrete** in the same balance: Let this be $W_2$. 9. **Partially compacted weight** = $W_2 - W_1$ 10. **Refill the cylinder** (or use a fresh sample) in **three layers**, tamping each layer **25 times** with the standard 16 mm rod. 11. **Weigh the cylinder + fully compacted concrete**: Let this be $W_3$. 12. **Fully compacted weight** = $W_3 - W_1$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Procedure Insight** > Unlike the slump test, this test requires **two separate sub-samples** from the same batch — one for the *gravity-only* (uncompacted) state and one for the *fully compacted* state. This is because once concrete is vibrated, its internal air void structure is destroyed and cannot be reversed. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | S.No. | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Standard / Boundary | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Slump Value** | $S = h_i - h_f$ | **mm** | IS 1199:1959 | | 2 | **Compacting Factor (CF)** | $CF = \dfrac{W_{p}}{W_{f}} = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{W_3 - W_1}$ | **Dimensionless** | IS 1199:1959 | | 3 | **Volume of Slump Cone Mould** | $V = \dfrac{1}{3}\pi h(R^2 + Rr + r^2) = 5.51 \times 10^6$ | **mm³** (≈ 5.51 L) | IS 7320 | | 4 | **Mass of Sample Required (CF Test)** | $m \geq 6 \text{ to } 7 \text{ kg}$ | **kg** | IS 5515 | > Where: > - $h_i$ = Initial height of mould = **300 mm** > - $h_f$ = Height of subsided concrete after lifting = mm > - $W_1$ = Weight of empty cylinder (kg) > - $W_2$ = Weight of cylinder + partially compacted concrete (kg) > - $W_3$ = Weight of cylinder + fully compacted concrete (kg) > - $R$ = Bottom radius = **100 mm**; $r$ = Top radius = **50 mm**; $h$ = **300 mm** ## 2.4 Workability Classification Tables (KTU Frequently Tested) ### 2.4.1 Slump-Based Classification (For Medium/Low Workability Mixes) | Degree of Workability | Slump (mm) | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Very Low** | 0 – 25 | Mass concrete dams, road foundations (roller-compacted) | | **Low** | 25 – 75 | Foundations, lightly reinforced sections, pavements | | **Medium** | 75 – 100 | Beams, slabs, columns with normal reinforcement | | **High** | 100 – 150 | Heavily reinforced sections, pumped concrete | | **Very High** | $\gt 150$ | Tremie concrete, self-compacting concrete (SCC) | ### 2.4.2 Compacting Factor-Based Classification (For Dry/Stiff Mixes) | Degree of Workability | Compacting Factor | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Very Low** | 0.78 – 0.80 | Very dry mixes (earth-moist) | | **Low** | 0.80 – 0.85 | Road pavements, mass concrete | | **Medium** | 0.85 – 0.90 | Normal reinforced concrete | | **High** | 0.90 – 0.95 | Pumped concrete, heavily reinforced | | **Very High** | $\gt 0.95$ | Self-compacting concrete (SCC) | ## 2.5 Comparative Engineering Utility — When to Use Which Test? | Property | Slump Cone Test | Compacting Factor Test | |---|---|---| | **Range of validity** | Medium to high workability (slump $\gt 25$ mm) | Low to medium workability (CF 0.78 – 0.95) | | **Best suited for** | Normal mixes, pumped concrete | Dry / harsh mixes (e.g., pavements) | | **Reliability for dry mixes** | ❌ Poor (always reads "0 slump") | ✅ Excellent | | **Reliability for fluid mixes** | ⚠️ Subject to collapse slump | ⚠️ Less sensitive (CF saturates near 1) | | **Equipment cost** | Low (~₹1500) | Moderate (~₹8000) | | **Test duration** | ~3 minutes | ~10 minutes | | **Field vs. Lab** | Both (most common field test) | Lab preferred (delicate hatch alignment) | > [!TIP] > **KTU Real-World Decision Rule** > If w/c ratio is **< 0.45** (dry mix) → use **Compacting Factor Test** instead of slump. > If the concrete is **pumped** → use **Slump Test** (industry standard for QC). > For **research or laboratory mix design** → use **both** for cross-validation. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volume of the Slump Cone Mould The slump cone is a **truncated cone (frustum)**. The volume of a frustum of a cone is given by the standard geometry formula. We apply it to verify the IS-specified capacity. Let: - $h = 300 \text{ mm}$ (height of cone) - $R = 100 \text{ mm}$ (radius of base, since bottom diameter = 200 mm) - $r = 50 \text{ mm}$ (radius of top, since top diameter = 100 mm) The standard frustum volume formula is: $$ V = \frac{1}{3} \pi h (R^2 + Rr + r^2) $$ **Step 1:** Substitute the values $$ V = \frac{1}{3} \times \pi \times 300 \times (100^2 + 100 \times 50 + 50^2) $$ **Step 2:** Evaluate the bracket term $$ R^2 = 100 \times 100 = 10000 \text{ mm}^2 $$ $$ Rr = 100 \times 50 = 5000 \text{ mm}^2 $$ $$ r^2 = 50 \times 50 = 2500 \text{ mm}^2 $$ $$ \text{Sum} = 10000 + 5000 + 2500 = 17500 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Step 3:** Combine $$ V = \frac{1}{3} \times \pi \times 300 \times 17500 $$ $$ V = 100 \times \pi \times 17500 $$ $$ V = 1750000 \times \pi $$ **Step 4:** Final numerical value $$ V \approx 1750000 \times 3.14159 $$ $$ V \approx 5497787 \text{ mm}^3 $$ $$ V \approx 5.498 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3 \approx 5.50 \text{ L} $$ > This is the **exact volume of the slump cone mould** as per IS 7320. The test result of 0 slump (i.e., the concrete remains at the full 300 mm height) is a check of the apparatus; if the volume is wrong, the standardisation is invalid. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Compacting Factor — Worked Numerical Example **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** In a Compacting Factor Test conducted in the concrete lab, the following weights were recorded: - Empty cylinder weight: $W_1 = 12.40 \text{ kg}$ - Cylinder + partially compacted concrete: $W_2 = 23.85 \text{ kg}$ - Cylinder + fully compacted concrete: $W_3 = 25.20 \text{ kg}$ Calculate the Compacting Factor and classify the workability. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Compute the weight of partially compacted concrete $$ W_p = W_2 - W_1 = 23.85 - 12.40 $$ $$ W_p = 11.45 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 2:** Compute the weight of fully compacted concrete $$ W_f = W_3 - W_1 = 25.20 - 12.40 $$ $$ W_f = 12.80 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 3:** Apply the Compacting Factor formula $$ CF = \frac{W_p}{W_f} = \frac{11.45}{12.80} $$ **Step 4:** Evaluate the ratio $$ CF = 0.8945 \approx 0.89 $$ **Step 5:** Classify the workability > [!IMPORTANT] > **Classification Result:** > A CF of **0.89** falls in the range **0.85 – 0.90**, which corresponds to **MEDIUM workability** — suitable for normal reinforced concrete (beams, slabs, columns with standard rebar spacing). **Step 6:** Quality check — verify the partial compaction makes physical sense $$ \text{Density reduction} = \frac{W_f - W_p}{W_f} \times 100\% $$ $$ = \frac{12.80 - 11.45}{12.80} \times 100\% $$ $$ = \frac{1.35}{12.80} \times 100\% \approx 10.55\% $$ This means **~10.55% air voids** remain in the partially compacted state — a typical value for medium-workability concrete. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cross-Verification Between Slump and Compacting Factor **Problem Statement:** A concrete mix yields a slump of **65 mm** and a compacting factor of **0.86**. The batch is to be used for a heavily reinforced column section. Is this concrete acceptable? Justify using both classifications. **Analysis:** **From Slump data** (65 mm): > Falls in range **25 – 75 mm** → classified as **LOW workability**. **From CF data** (0.86): > Falls in range **0.85 – 0.90** → classified as **MEDIUM workability**. **Conflict Resolution:** > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** > The two tests measure *different* aspects. Slump 65 mm classifies as low because the concrete is on the boundary. CF 0.86 = medium means it compacts reasonably well. For a **heavily reinforced** section, the engineer should **re-design the mix** to push the slump to **100 – 125 mm** and the CF to **0.92 – 0.95**, by: > - Adding **plasticizer (superplasticizer)** at **0.8% – 1.2%** by weight of cement > - **NOT** adding more water (would reduce strength) > - Or, **NOT** increasing fine aggregate content beyond 35% (would cause segregation) --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Limiting Cases and Physical Meaning | Test Result | Physical Interpretation | Likely Cause | Engineering Correction | |---|---|---|---| | Slump = 0 mm | Concrete retains full cone shape | **Very dry mix** (low w/c) | Add water or plasticizer; **switch to CF test** for quantification | | Slump = 200 mm, Collapse | Concrete collapsed completely | **Excess water** OR **high fines** OR **segregation** | Reduce water, re-check sand grading | | Slump = 100 mm, Shear | Top portion sheared off | **Harsh, undersanded mix** | Increase sand fraction or use finer sand | | CF = 0.99 | Nearly equal to fully compacted | **Very fluid mix** (saturated) | Re-check mix design; risk of segregation | | CF = 0.75 | Well below 0.78 lower bound | **Severe compaction issue** | Re-check for contamination, low fines, or aggregate shape | | CF > 1.00 | Physically impossible | **Measurement / calibration error** | Re-weigh balance, check for concrete sticking to cylinder walls | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Slump Cone Test Apparatus Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Fresh Concrete Sample] --> nodeB[Slump Cone Mould] nodeB[Slump Cone Mould
Top Dia 100mm
Bottom Dia 200mm
Height 300mm] --> nodeC[Fill Layer 1: 1/3 Height] nodeC --> nodeD[Tamp 25 strokes
16mm dia bullet-nosed rod] nodeD --> nodeE[Fill Layer 2: 2/3 Height] nodeE --> nodeF[Tamp 25 strokes
penetrate into Layer 1] nodeF --> nodeG[Fill Layer 3: Heap Above Rim] nodeG --> nodeH[Tamp 25 strokes
Strike off flush with trowel] nodeH --> nodeI[Lift Mould Vertically
in 5 to 10 seconds] nodeI --> nodeJ[Measure Slump
Vertical Settlement in mm] nodeJ --> nodeK{Identify Slump Type} nodeK --> nodeK1[True Slump
Valid Test] nodeK --> nodeK2[Shear Slump
Repeat Test] nodeK --> nodeK3[Collapse Slump
Invalid Test] nodeJ --> nodeL[Classify Workability
from IS Slump Table] nodeL --> nodeM[Record Result
to nearest 5 mm] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Compacting Factor Test Apparatus Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Fresh Concrete Sample
6 to 7 kg] --> nodeB[Fill Upper Hopper] nodeB --> nodeC[Close Lower Hopper Hatch] nodeC --> nodeD[Open Upper Hopper Hatch
Concrete falls 200mm] nodeD --> nodeE[Wait 2 minutes
Air escape] nodeE --> nodeF[Open Lower Hopper Hatch
Concrete falls 200mm into cylinder] nodeF --> nodeG[Cut off excess concrete
flush with cylinder rim] nodeG --> nodeH[Weigh Cylinder + Partially Compacted Concrete
W2] nodeH --> nodeI[Refill Cylinder in 3 Layers
Tamp 25 strokes per layer] nodeI --> nodeJ[Weigh Cylinder + Fully Compacted Concrete
W3] nodeJ --> nodeK[Compute Wp and Wf] nodeK --> nodeL[CF equals Wp divided by Wf] nodeL --> nodeM[Classify Workability
from IS CF Table] nodeM --> nodeN[Report CF to two decimal places] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Both Tests ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A1[Slump Cone Test Domain] n1[Empirical Apparatus] --> n2[Gravitational Flow Measurement] n2 --> n3[Slump Value in mm] n3 --> n4[IS 1199 Slump Classification] end subgraph A2[Compacting Factor Test Domain] n5[Empirical Apparatus] --> n6[Dynamic Drop Energy + Vibration] n6 --> n7[Dimensionless CF Ratio] n7 --> n8[IS 1199 CF Classification] end A1 --> A3[Workability Evaluation Matrix] A2 --> A3 A3 --> A4[Decision Matrix for Mix Adjustment] A4 --> A5[Plasticizer or Water or Aggregate Correction] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Decision Flow: Which Test to Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD node0[Start: Fresh Concrete Sample Available] --> node1{Visual Consistency Check} node1 --> node2[Wet Fluid Mix] node1 --> node3[Plastic Mix] node1 --> node4[Stiff Dry Mix] node2 --> node5[Use Slump Cone Test
primary] node3 --> node6[Both Tests Valid
use Slump first] node4 --> node7[Use Compacting Factor Test
primary] node5 --> node8[Slump 100 to 200 mm
Classify as High to Very High] node6 --> node9[Slump 50 to 100 mm
CF 0.85 to 0.92] node7 --> node10[CF 0.78 to 0.85
Classify as Low to Very Low] node8 --> node11[Record and Report] node9 --> node11 node10 --> node11 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 regulations, Part A carries 2 marks for content and 1 mark for clarity/terminology. Always cite the relevant IS code. ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]` **Q: Define workability of fresh concrete. List the four characteristics that determine workability.** **Model Answer:** **Workability** (as per IS 6461 Part VII) is the property of freshly mixed concrete that determines the ease and homogeneity with which it can be mixed, transported, placed, compacted, and finished without segregation. The **four sub-properties** of workability are: 1. **Consistency** — degree of wetness/fluidity of the mix 2. **Mobility** — ability to flow around reinforcement and into corners 3. **Compactability** — ease with which entrapped air can be removed 4. **Finishability** — ease of obtaining a smooth, uniform surface *Note: A concrete is workable when it satisfies all four simultaneously; high mobility without compactability would result in segregation.* --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model]` **Q: Differentiate between the Slump Cone Test and the Compacting Factor Test in 5 key points.** **Model Answer:** | S.No. | Parameter | Slump Cone Test | Compacting Factor Test | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Property measured** | Consistency (under self-weight) | Compactability (under drop energy) | | 2 | **IS Code** | IS 1199:1959, IS 7320:1974 | IS 1199:1959, IS 5515:1983 | | 3 | **Best suited for** | Medium to high workability | Low to medium workability (dry mixes) | | 4 | **Result** | Slump in mm (dimensional) | Compacting factor (dimensionless, 0 to 1) | | 5 | **Test duration** | ~3 minutes | ~10 minutes (plus 2 min wait) | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks. Model structure is **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. You are expected to draw labelled diagrams (2 marks) and present the step-by-step procedure (3 marks), with the remaining 2 marks for application/correctness. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **(a)** Describe the **Slump Cone Test** as per IS 1199:1959, with a neatly labelled diagram of the apparatus. List the three types of slump observed and explain when each type is reported as valid or invalid. (7 Marks) **(b)** A concrete mix is tested using the slump cone apparatus. The original height of the mould is 300 mm. After lifting the mould, the highest point of the subsided concrete is measured. The heights of the subsided concrete measured at four points on the top surface are: 245 mm, 240 mm, 248 mm, and 235 mm. Determine the slump value and classify the workability as per IS standards. (7 Marks) --- #### **Model Solution (a):** **Apparatus Description (as per IS 7320:1974):** - **Mould**: Hollow truncated cone, bottom diameter **200 mm**, top diameter **100 mm**, height **300 mm**, minimum wall thickness **1.5 mm** - **Foot pedals**: Two pieces welded at the bottom rim to hold the mould against the base plate - **Lifting handles**: Two handles at the top rim for vertical removal - **Tamping rod**: Steel rod, **16 mm** diameter, **600 mm** long, **bullet-nosed** (rounded tip) - **Base plate**: Smooth, flat, non-absorbent metal plate **Procedure (concise):** 1. Dampen the mould interior and place on a level non-absorbent plate 2. Fill the mould in **three equal layers** (each 1/3 of the height) 3. Tamp each layer with **25 strokes** of the tamping rod, distributed uniformly 4. Strike off the top surface flush with a trowel 5. Lift the mould **vertically upward in 5 to 10 seconds** with a steady motion 6. Measure the **difference between the mould height and the highest point of the slumped concrete** using the tamping rod as a straight edge 7. Record the slump to the **nearest 5 mm** **Three Types of Slump:** | Type | Description | Validity | |---|---|---| | **True Slump** | Concrete subsides uniformly, retains the cone shape | ✅ **Valid** — record the value | | **Shear Slump** | Top half shears off and slides sideways | ⚠️ **Repeat the test**; if again shear slump → record as shear slump and reject the mix | | **Collapse Slump** | Concrete collapses completely into a heap | ❌ **Invalid** — repeat with a stiffer mix (lower w/c ratio) | **Diagrammatic Representation (in exam):** > Draw a labelled cone with the three dimensions (200, 100, 300 mm), the tamping rod, and the base plate. --- #### **Model Solution (b):** **Given:** - Original mould height: $h_i = 300 \text{ mm}$ - Heights of subsided concrete at 4 points: 245, 240, 248, 235 mm **Step 1:** Calculate the **average height** of the subsided concrete $$ h_f = \frac{245 + 240 + 248 + 235}{4} $$ $$ h_f = \frac{968}{4} = 242 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 2:** Calculate the **slump value** $$ S = h_i - h_f $$ $$ S = 300 - 242 = 58 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 3:** Classify the workability > A slump of **58 mm** lies in the range **25 – 75 mm**, which corresponds to **LOW workability** as per IS 1199:1959. **Step 4:** Interpretation > This mix is suitable for **lightly reinforced sections, foundations, and simple slabs**. It is **not** suitable for heavily reinforced members without adding a plasticizer. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **(a)** Describe the **Compacting Factor Test** in detail, with a labelled diagram of the apparatus. State the relevant IS codes and explain why this test is preferred over the slump cone test for dry/stiff concrete mixes. (7 Marks) **(b)** In a Compacting Factor Test, the following observations were recorded: - Mass of empty cylinder: $12.50 \text{ kg}$ - Mass of cylinder + partially compacted concrete: $24.10 \text{ kg}$ - Mass of cylinder + fully compacted concrete: $25.60 \text{ kg}$ Compute the **Compacting Factor**, classify the workability, and estimate the **percentage of entrapped air voids** in the partially compacted state. (7 Marks) --- #### **Model Solution (a):** **Principle:** The Compacting Factor Test (developed by Glanville, Collins & Matthews, 1947) measures the **degree of compaction** achieved by standard gravitational drop energy alone, relative to the degree achieved by full compaction. It is the most reliable test for **stiff, dry, or harsh mixes** where the slump cone reads zero. **Apparatus (as per IS 5515:1983):** - **Upper Hopper**: Cylindrical body, top rectangular opening **254 mm × 254 mm**, with a bottom rectangular hatch **130 mm × 130 mm** - **Lower Hopper**: Similar geometry, top opening **230 mm × 230 mm**, bottom hatch **130 mm × 130 mm** - **Cylinder**: Internal diameter **152.5 mm**, height **305 mm**, with a removable base plate - **Free-fall distance**: **200 mm** between hoppers; **200 mm** between lower hopper and cylinder top - **Hatches**: Quick-release, hinged, with rubber seals **Procedure (concise):** 1. Weigh the empty cylinder with base plate → $W_1$ 2. Place the cylinder directly below the lower hopper; close the lower hopper hatch 3. Fill the upper hopper gently with the fresh concrete sample (~6–7 kg) 4. Open the upper hopper hatch — concrete falls into the lower hopper 5. Wait **2 minutes** for trapped air to escape 6. Open the lower hopper hatch — concrete falls into the cylinder 7. Cut off the excess concrete flush with the rim using a trowel 8. Weigh the cylinder + partially compacted concrete → $W_2$ 9. Refill (or use a fresh sample) the cylinder in 3 layers, tamping 25 times per layer 10. Weigh the cylinder + fully compacted concrete → $W_3$ **Why Preferred for Dry Mixes:** - A dry mix (slump = 0) gives **no useful information** in the slump test. - The CF test still gives a meaningful ratio (typically 0.78 to 0.85) for such mixes. - The two-hopper drop system simulates the field placement energy in pavements and mass concrete. --- #### **Model Solution (b):** **Given:** - $W_1 = 12.50 \text{ kg}$ (empty cylinder) - $W_2 = 24.10 \text{ kg}$ (cylinder + partially compacted) - $W_3 = 25.60 \text{ kg}$ (cylinder + fully compacted) **Step 1:** Compute the weight of partially compacted concrete $$ W_p = W_2 - W_1 = 24.10 - 12.50 $$ $$ W_p = 11.60 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 2:** Compute the weight of fully compacted concrete $$ W_f = W_3 - W_1 = 25.60 - 12.50 $$ $$ W_f = 13.10 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 3:** Apply the Compacting Factor formula $$ CF = \frac{W_p}{W_f} = \frac{11.60}{13.10} $$ $$ CF = 0.8855 \approx 0.89 $$ **Step 4:** Classify the workability > A CF of **0.89** falls in the range **0.85 – 0.90**, classified as **MEDIUM workability**. This is suitable for **normal reinforced concrete construction**. **Step 5:** Estimate the percentage of entrapped air voids $$ \text{Air voids (\%)} = \frac{W_f - W_p}{W_f} \times 100\% $$ $$ = \frac{13.10 - 11.60}{13.10} \times 100\% $$ $$ = \frac{1.50}{13.10} \times 100\% $$ $$ \approx 11.45\% $$ > This means **~11.45%** of the partially compacted concrete volume consists of entrapped air. After full compaction (vibration/tamping), these voids are eliminated. **Step 6:** Engineering interpretation > A 11.45% air void content is **moderately high** for structural concrete. If the structure is exposed to marine or sulphate-rich environments, an **air-entraining admixture** may be added intentionally to achieve a controlled air void system (typically 4%–6%) for freeze-thaw resistance — but this is a deliberate design choice, distinct from compaction-induced voids. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to subtract the empty cylinder weight ($W_1$)**: Many students write $CF = W_2 / W_3$ — **wrong by 30%!** The correct ratio is $(W_2 - W_1) / (W_3 - W_1)$. **Deduct 2 marks.** > 2. **Reporting CF > 1.00**: Indicates measurement error (concrete stuck to walls, or balance not zeroed). Examiners treat this as a **"no answer"** situation. > 3. **Confusing "shear slump" with "true slump"**: A shear slump **must be repeated**, not averaged. Reporting it directly leads to **3-mark deduction**. > 4. **Writing "IS 1199:2018"**: The correct year is **1959** for the test method. Apparatus code for slump is **IS 7320:1974**. Examiners check IS code year. > 5. **Lifting the mould with a twisting or jerky motion**: Causes the concrete to "tear" — gives an artificially high slump. Examiner expects procedural mention. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 (Module Experiment 2)** ### **Slump Cone Test — Key Anchors** - ✅ **Mould dimensions:** 200 mm base × 100 mm top × 300 mm height (memorize the "trumpet shape") - ✅ **Mould volume:** ≈ **5.50 litres** (frustum formula) - ✅ **Tamping rod:** 16 mm diameter, 600 mm long, **bullet-nosed** - ✅ **Filling:** 3 equal layers, **25 strokes per layer**, with rod penetrating into the layer below - ✅ **Lifting time:** 5 to 10 seconds, **no twisting** - ✅ **Result reporting:** Slump = $h_i - h_f$, nearest 5 mm - ✅ **IS Code for test:** IS 1199:1959; **IS Code for apparatus:** IS 7320:1974 - ✅ **Three slump types:** True (valid), Shear (repeat), Collapse (invalid — redo) ### **Compacting Factor Test — Key Anchors** - ✅ **Apparatus:** 2 hoppers + 1 cylinder, each free-fall distance **200 mm** - ✅ **Cylinder:** 152.5 mm internal diameter × 305 mm height - ✅ **Sample mass:** Minimum 6–7 kg - ✅ **Air escape pause:** **2 minutes** between opening the two hoppers - ✅ **Formula:** $CF = (W_2 - W_1) / (W_3 - W_1)$ - ✅ **IS Code for test:** IS 1199:1959; **IS Code for apparatus:** IS 5515:1983 - ✅ **Range of validity:** 0.78 to 0.95 (CF > 1 is physically impossible — error) ### **Workability Classification — Memorize Both Tables** - ✅ **Slump-based:** 0–25 (very low), 25–75 (low), 75–100 (medium), 100–150 (high), >150 (very high) - ✅ **CF-based:** 0.78–0.80 (very low), 0.80–0.85 (low), 0.85–0.90 (medium), 0.90–0.95 (high), >0.95 (very high) ### **Strategic Selection Rule (for KTU viva & field practice)** - ✅ **Slump test** → wet to medium mixes (slump > 25 mm); field test of choice - ✅ **Compacting factor test** → dry/stiff mixes (slump ≤ 25 mm); lab test of choice - ✅ **Flow test** → very fluid mixes (slump > 150 mm) - ✅ **Vee-Bee test** → highly stiff mixes (alternative to CF test) ### **Common Exam Traps to Avoid** - ❌ Don't write **CF = $W_2 / W_3$** — must subtract $W_1$ - ❌ Don't say **"IS 1199:2018"** — the correct year is **1959** - ❌ Don't say **"slump = $h_f$"** — slump is the **difference**, not the residual height - ❌ Don't forget the **2-minute wait** in the CF test for air escape - ❌ Don't use the **slump test for dry mixes** — switch to CF or Vee-Bee - ❌ Don't apply the **CF test to SCC (self-compacting concrete)** — CF will saturate near 1.0 and give no information > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Memory Mnemonics:** > - **S**lump = **S**ubsidence (under self-weight) > - **C**ompacting **F**actor = **C**ompare **F**ractions > - **"3 × 25 = 75"** — the magic numbers of layer × strokes for **both** tests > - **"200, 100, 300"** — the **trumpet dimensions** of the slump cone > - **"152 × 305"** — the **cylinder dimensions** of the CF apparatus (in mm)

topics.title: Study on Workability of Concrete by Vee-Bee Test and Flow Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL508 - CONCRETE LAB (MT-2)
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study on Workability of Concrete by Vee-Bee Test and Flow Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Workability" in Concrete?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Per IS 6461 Part 8 & IS 1199):**
> *Workability of concrete is defined as the property of freshly mixed concrete or mortar which determines the ease and homogeneity with which it can be mixed, transported, placed, compacted, and finished without any segregation.* — **Bureau of Indian Standards, IS 6461 (Part 8): 1973, Reaffirmed 2018.**

Workability is a **composite property** — it is not measured directly by a single number. It depends on:
- **Water Content** (free water, not absorbed by aggregates)
- **Mix Proportions** (cement-to-aggregate ratio)
- **Aggregate Shape & Texture** (rounded vs. crushed, smooth vs. rough)
- **Admixtures** (plasticizers, superplasticizers)
- **Time & Temperature** (workability decreases with time)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Honey vs. Butter Comparison:**
> Imagine spreading **honey** on a toast vs. spreading **cold butter**.
> - **Honey** flows easily, fills the toast surface, requires no effort → *High workability, high flow*
> - **Cold butter** resists, requires force to spread, doesn't fill corners easily → *Low workability, low flow*
>
> Concrete behaves the same way. The **Vee-Bee Test** measures how "butter-like" (stiff) the mix is, while the **Flow Test** measures how "honey-like" (fluid) the mix is. Different construction scenarios (slabs, columns, mass concrete) need different "spreads."

---

### 1.2 The Vee-Bee Consistometer Test — Core Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vee-Bee Consistometer Test Definition (IS 1199: Part 2 — 2018):**
> A dynamic workability test that measures the **time (in seconds)** required for a standard mass of fresh concrete, placed in a slump cone and subjected to standard vibration, to change its shape from a truncated cone to a **compacted horizontal cylinder (Vee shape)**. The result is expressed as **Vee-Bee Degrees (V-B sec)**.

**Key Insight:** It is the **only standard workability test that uses vibration** to determine consistency. It is specifically used for **stiff, low-workability concrete** mixes (e.g., road pavements, mass concrete, roller-compacted concrete).

**Quantitative Standard:** Lower Vee-Bee seconds = more workable concrete.

| Vee-Bee Time (sec) | Workability Class | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **0 to 5** | Very high | Not used in practice (too fluid) |
| **5 to 10** | High | Heavily reinforced sections |
| **10 to 15** | Medium | Normal reinforced concrete |
| **15 to 20** | Low | Road slabs, mass concrete |
| **> 20** | Very low | Stiff mixes, pavement concrete |

> **Note:** Most practical concrete mixes for KTU laboratory assessment fall in the **5 to 15 seconds** range.

---

### 1.3 The Flow Table Test — Core Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flow Table Test Definition (IS 1199: Part 1 — 2018, reaffirmed 2024):**
> A static workability test that measures the **spread (flow)** of freshly mixed concrete or mortar when subjected to a **standard number of drops (15 or 25)** on a **standard flow table (brass plate, 70 cm × 70 cm)**. The result is expressed as a **percentage flow (%)** relative to the original base diameter.

**Quantitative Standard:** Higher flow % = more workable concrete.

| Flow (%) | Consistency | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **0 to 20%** | Stiff / Dry | Paving mixes |
| **20 to 50%** | Plastic | General RCC work |
| **50 to 75%** | Wet / Fluid | Heavily reinforced, thin sections |
| **> 75%** | Very wet | Self-compacting concrete (SCC zone) |

> **Note:** For KTU Lab MT-2 assessment, the **standard 15 drops** is most commonly adopted (per IS 1199 Part 1 for concrete). For **mortar**, **25 drops** is used (per IS 2250 / IS 4031 Part 7).

---

### 1.4 Why Do We Need TWO Different Tests?

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Logic — The Range of Workability:**
> No single test can accurately measure the **full spectrum** of workability from "rock-hard" to "soup-like."
> - **Vee-Bee Test** is best for **stiff-to-medium** consistency.
> - **Slump Test** is best for **medium-to-plastic** consistency.
> - **Flow Test** is best for **plastic-to-fluid** consistency.
> - **Compaction Factor Test** is best for **medium** consistency (low water content).
>
> Together, Vee-Bee and Flow Tests cover approximately **80%** of practical concrete workability scenarios encountered in civil engineering practice.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Vee-Bee Test — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Mechanics)

The Vee-Bee test apparatus is a **mechanical, vibration-based system**. The operational logic is built on **four physical principles**:

1. **Geometric Standardization:** The fresh concrete is shaped into a standard truncated cone (slump cone: 20 cm bottom Ø, 10 cm top Ø, 30 cm height). This provides a known initial volume and surface area.

2. **Gravitational Reference:** A **transparent disc** (called a **"swing plate"** or **"glass disc"**) is placed on top of the concrete inside the cone. This disc has a **graduated rod** attached vertically.

3. **Vibration Input:** A **vibrating table** at **50 Hz** (3000 vibrations per minute) is energized. The concrete starts to flow, the cone collapses, and the disc sinks as the concrete compacts.

4. **Vee-Shape Endpoint:** The test ends when the disc is **fully covered with concrete** AND the concrete surface is **level / flat** — i.e., the cone has "veed" (re-shaped) into a **flat pancake**. The elapsed time = **Vee-Bee Degrees**.

**The "Why" Behind Vee-Bee Degrees:**
- Stiff concrete → takes long time to deform → **HIGH Vee-Bee value**
- Fluid concrete → deforms quickly → **LOW Vee-Bee value**
- Hence Vee-Bee is an **inverse indicator** of workability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Pancake Analogy:**
> Take a raw idli batter poured into a steel tumbler, then vibrate the tumbler.
> - **Thick batter** → takes 30 seconds to flatten into a pancake → **High Vee-Bee (stiff)**
> - **Thin batter** → takes 3 seconds → **Low Vee-Bee (fluid)**
>
> The Vee-Bee test is exactly this physical principle applied to concrete.

---

### 2.2 Flow Test — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Mechanics)

The Flow table test uses **impact (drop) energy** rather than vibration:

1. **Cone Placement:** A standard slump cone (10 cm top, 20 cm bottom, 30 cm height) is placed at the **center of a brass-topped flow table** (70 cm × 70 cm).

2. **Layered Filling:** Concrete is filled in **2 equal layers**, each rodded 10 times with a tamping rod (16 mm Ø, 60 cm long).

3. **Cone Removal:** After 30 seconds, the cone is lifted **vertically upward** (NOT tilted) in 3 to 6 seconds. The concrete subsides by its own weight.

4. **Dropping Action:** The table is **raised and dropped 15 times** from a height of **12.5 mm** (5/8 inch) at a rate of **one drop per second**.

5. **Spread Measurement:** The **diameter of the spread** is measured in **two perpendicular directions** (D₁ and D₂). The mean diameter is used for flow calculation.

**The "Why" Behind Flow %:**
- Flowing concrete → spreads widely → **HIGH flow %**
- Stiff concrete → minimal spread → **LOW flow %**
- Hence Flow % is a **direct indicator** of workability.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Code)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Vee-Bee & Flow Test**

| Parameter | Formula | Units | IS Code Reference | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Vee-Bee Degrees** | $V_B = t_{\text{end}} - t_{\text{start}}$ | **seconds (sec)** | IS 1199 Part 2: 2018 | Inverse to workability |
| **Flow Diameter (Mean)** | $D_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{D_1 + D_2}{2}$ | **cm** | IS 1199 Part 1: 2018 | Direct |
| **Flow Percentage** | $F = \dfrac{D_{\text{mean}} - D_0}{D_0} \times 100$ | **%** | IS 1199 Part 1: 2018 | Direct |
| **Original Base Diameter** | $D_0 = 25$ cm | **cm** | IS 1199 Part 1: 2018 | Constant |
| **Number of Drops (Concrete)** | $n = 15$ | — | IS 1199 Part 1: 2018 | Constant |
| **Number of Drops (Mortar)** | $n = 25$ | — | IS 4031 Part 7: 2017 | Constant |
| **Drop Height** | $h = 12.5$ mm | **mm (5/8 inch)** | IS 1199 Part 1: 2018 | Constant |
| **Vibration Frequency** | $f = 50$ Hz | **Hz (3000 vpm)** | IS 1199 Part 2: 2018 | Constant |
| **Vibration Amplitude** | $a = 0.5$ mm | **mm** | IS 1199 Part 2: 2018 | Constant |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Memorization Fact:** For the **Flow Test**, the **base diameter of the slump cone is 25 cm** — this is the standard reference value $D_0$ used in **every** flow % calculation. Do NOT use the top diameter (10 cm) or height (30 cm).

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Test | Industry Application | Why Preferred |
|---|---|---|
| **Vee-Bee** | Road & runway paving, mass dams, dry-lean concrete (DLC) | Stiff mixes that wouldn't show meaningful slump |
| **Flow Test** | Self-compacting concrete (SCC) screening, grout, mortar | Measures high-end workability beyond slump range |
| **Both Combined** | Mix design verification in R&D labs | Cross-validates workability for medium-range mixes |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Flow Percentage Formula

The flow percentage is fundamentally a **geometric strain** measurement — i.e., how much the concrete has *deformed* relative to its original size.

**Step 1:** Let the original base diameter of the concrete mass (when cone is lifted off) be:
$$D_0 = 25 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2:** After 15 drops, the concrete spreads into an approximate circular pancake. Measure its diameter in two perpendicular directions:
$$D_1 = \text{spread along X-axis (cm)}$$
$$D_2 = \text{spread along Y-axis (cm)}$$

**Step 3:** The mean spread diameter is the arithmetic average:
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{mean}} &= \frac{D_1 + D_2}{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \left( D_1 + D_2 \right) \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic: Averaging two perpendicular measurements cancels out any asymmetric (non-circular) flow, giving a representative diameter.*

**Step 4:** The **absolute increase in diameter** (spread) is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta D &= D_{\text{mean}} - D_0 \\
&= D_{\text{mean}} - 25 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** The **relative flow (dimensionless ratio)** is the absolute spread divided by the original diameter:
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{ratio}} &= \frac{\Delta D}{D_0} \\
&= \frac{D_{\text{mean}} - 25}{25}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** To express as a percentage, multiply by 100:
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\%} &= F_{\text{ratio}} \times 100 \\
F_{\%} &= \frac{D_{\text{mean}} - 25}{25} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FINAL FLOW FORMULA (KTU Board Exam Standard):**
> 
> $$F_{\%} = \frac{D_{\text{mean}} - 25}{25} \times 100$$

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Flow Test Calculation

**Problem Statement (Model KTU Question):**
A flow table test is performed on a freshly mixed concrete sample. After 15 drops, the spread is measured as $D_1 = 38.5$ cm and $D_2 = 39.0$ cm. Calculate the **flow percentage**.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Identify given values
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= 38.5 \text{ cm} \\
D_2 &= 39.0 \text{ cm} \\
D_0 &= 25 \text{ cm (standard, from IS 1199 Part 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate mean spread diameter
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{mean}} &= \frac{D_1 + D_2}{2} \\
&= \frac{38.5 + 39.0}{2} \\
&= \frac{77.5}{2} \\
&= 38.75 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic: We add the two perpendicular measurements and divide by 2 to obtain the average spread.*

**Step 3:** Substitute into the flow % formula
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\%} &= \frac{D_{\text{mean}} - D_0}{D_0} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{38.75 - 25}{25} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{13.75}{25} \times 100 \\
&= 0.55 \times 100 \\
&= 55\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $F = 55\%$

**Interpretation:** This concrete has a **medium-to-wet consistency** (per the KTU classification table in Section 1.3). It is suitable for **heavily reinforced sections** with congested reinforcement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** "38 - 25 = 13, 13/25 = 0.52, × 100 = 52." The arithmetic is straightforward. **Marks are lost in unit handling and formula selection, not in the calculation itself.**

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Vee-Bee Test

**Problem Statement (Model KTU Question):**
In a Vee-Bee consistometer test, the vibration timer is started at $t_{\text{start}} = 0$ sec. The transparent disc becomes fully covered with concrete at $t_{\text{end}} = 9$ sec. The slump (initial) is measured as **2 cm**. Determine: (a) Vee-Bee Degrees, (b) workability classification.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Vee-Bee degrees (direct reading from apparatus)
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_B &= t_{\text{end}} - t_{\text{start}} \\
&= 9 - 0 \\
&= 9 \text{ sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Classify using the standard KTU table (Section 1.2)
- $V_B = 9$ sec → falls in the **5 to 10 sec** range → **HIGH WORKABILITY** class
- Suitable for **heavily reinforced sections** and thin, congested members.

**Step 3:** Cross-validate with slump (slump = 2 cm, very low slump; the Vee-Bee value of 9 sec indicates *high* workability — this is an interesting case where the slump test under-reports workability for this mix, and the Vee-Bee test provides better discrimination).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** This example highlights why Vee-Bee is **superior to slump** for stiff mixes. A concrete with slump = 2 cm would be classified as "low workability" by the slump test alone — but the Vee-Bee value of 9 sec reclassifies it as **high workability**. The vibration input reveals the true rheological behavior.

---

### 3.4 KTU Calculation Matrix — Common Pitfalls

| Common Mistake | Correct Approach | Mark Loss Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Using **top diameter (10 cm)** in flow % | Always use base diameter = **25 cm** | **-2 to -3 marks** |
| Using **single direction spread** $D_1$ only | Always use **mean** of two perpendicular readings | **-1 mark** |
| Reporting Vee-Bee in **minutes** | Report in **seconds (sec)** | **-1 mark** |
| Forgetting to **subtract $D_0$** in flow formula | Numerator is $D_{\text{mean}} - D_0$, not $D_{\text{mean}}$ alone | **-2 marks** |
| Using **25 drops** for concrete | Concrete = **15 drops**; Mortar = **25 drops** | **-1 mark** |
| Not mentioning the **IS Code** in procedure | Always cite **IS 1199 (Part 1 or Part 2): 2018** | **-1 to -2 marks** |

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module 2 Pattern:** Two choices are offered. Solve ANY ONE. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Vee-Bee Focus**

**[KTU Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe the **Vee-Bee Consistometer Test** in detail. Include its principle, apparatus, procedure, IS code reference, and at least **two advantages and two limitations** of the test. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a Vee-Bee test, the timer reads **0 sec** when vibration starts. After **8 seconds**, the glass disc becomes fully covered and the concrete surface is flat. Classify the workability and state **two suitable field applications** of this concrete. **(7 Marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks — Detailed Procedure**

> **Principle (1 Mark):**
> The Vee-Bee test measures workability by determining the **time taken** for a fresh concrete sample, under **standard vibration**, to remould from a cone shape to a **uniform Vee (pancake) shape**. It is a **dynamic test** that simulates field compaction.
>
> **Apparatus (1 Mark):**
> 1. Vee-Bee consistometer with vibrating table (50 Hz, 0.5 mm amplitude).
> 2. Cylindrical container (240 mm Ø, 200 mm height).
> 3. Standard slump cone.
