> 4. Transparent glass disc with vertical graduated rod.
> 5. Tamping rod (16 mm Ø).
> 6. Stopwatch.
> 7. Trowel and scoop.
>
> **IS Code (1 Mark):** **IS 1199 (Part 2): 2018**
>
> **Procedure (3 Marks):**
> 1. Place the **slump cone** centrally inside the cylindrical container.
> 2. Fill the cone with fresh concrete in **4 equal layers**, tamping each layer **25 times** with the tamping rod.
> 3. Strike off the top surface flush with the cone rim using a trowel.
> 4. Place the **transparent glass disc** on top of the concrete inside the cone.
> 5. Position the **graduated rod** vertically and note its initial reading.
> 6. Switch on the **vibrating table** and simultaneously **start the stopwatch**.
> 7. Observe the disc through the inspection window.
> 8. Stop the stopwatch when the **disc is fully covered with concrete** and the surface is **uniformly flat** (Vee shape achieved).
> 9. Record the **elapsed time** as the **Vee-Bee Degrees** in seconds.
>
> **Advantages (0.5 Marks):**
> 1. Most suitable for **stiff concrete mixes** where slump test gives negligible values.
> 2. Provides a **quantitative, time-based** measurement with high repeatability.
>
> **Limitations (0.5 Marks):**
> 1. Not suitable for **very fluid** or **self-compacting concrete** (Vee-Bee time too short to measure accurately).
> 2. Apparatus is **bulky and expensive** compared to slump test.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks — Numerical Classification**

**Given:**
$$
V_B = 8 \text{ sec}
$$

**Step 1 — Vee-Bee Classification (3 Marks):**
From the standard KTU table:
- $V_B = 8$ sec falls in the **5 to 10 sec** range.
- **Classification:** **HIGH WORKABILITY** concrete.

**Step 2 — Field Applications (2 Marks):**
1. **Heavily reinforced concrete sections** (e.g., beam-column junctions with congested reinforcement) where concrete must flow easily around bars.
2. **Thin structural members** (e.g., thin slabs, shell roofs) requiring good flow into formwork.

**Step 3 — Cross-Validation Note (2 Marks):**
The Vee-Bee test of 8 sec suggests **good workability** even though the **initial slump** (not given) might be misleading. This demonstrates the **superiority of the Vee-Bee test** for discriminating between stiff-to-medium workability mixes, and is the **primary reason it is preferred for road and pavement concrete** quality control.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Flow Test Focus**

**[KTU Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Flow Table Test** for concrete with a neat labeled diagram. State the IS code, formula, and standard base diameter used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a flow table test on fresh concrete, the spread after 15 drops is measured as **$D_1 = 42$ cm** and **$D_2 = 44$ cm**. Calculate the **flow percentage** and classify the workability. **(7 Marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks — Flow Table Test Theory**

> **Principle (1 Mark):**
> The flow table test measures workability by determining the **spread (flow)** of fresh concrete, when subjected to a **standard number of drops**, on a **standard flow table**. It is a **static-impact test** that simulates lateral flow under impact energy.
>
> **Apparatus (1 Mark):**
> 1. Flow table (70 cm × 70 cm brass top) with cam mechanism (12.5 mm lift).
> 2. Standard slump cone (20 cm base, 10 cm top, 30 cm height).
> 3. Tamping rod (16 mm Ø, 60 cm long).
> 4. Measuring scale / Vernier calipers.
> 5. Stopwatch and trowel.
>
> **IS Code (1 Mark):** **IS 1199 (Part 1): 2018** (for concrete) and **IS 4031 (Part 7): 2017** (for mortar).
>
> **Procedure (2 Marks):**
> 1. Clean and dry the flow table top.
> 2. Place the **slump cone** at the **center** of the flow table.
> 3. Fill the cone with fresh concrete in **2 equal layers**.
> 4. Tamping each layer **10 times** uniformly with the tamping rod.
> 5. Strike off the top surface flush with the cone rim.
> 6. Wait for **30 seconds** to allow partial subsidence.
> 7. Lift the cone **vertically** in 3 to 6 seconds (do NOT tilt).
> 8. Raise the table and let it **drop 15 times** from a height of **12.5 mm**, at the rate of **1 drop per second**.
> 9. Measure the **spread diameter in two perpendicular directions** ($D_1$ and $D_2$).
>
> **Formula and Constant (2 Marks):**
> 
> $$F_{\%} = \frac{D_{\text{mean}} - 25}{25} \times 100$$
> 
> where $D_0 = 25$ cm (base diameter of slump cone).
> 
> $D_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{D_1 + D_2}{2}$

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks — Numerical Calculation**

**Given:**
$$
D_1 = 42 \text{ cm}, \quad D_2 = 44 \text{ cm}, \quad D_0 = 25 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 1 — Mean Diameter (2 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{mean}} &= \frac{D_1 + D_2}{2} \\
&= \frac{42 + 44}{2} \\
&= \frac{86}{2} \\
&= 43 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic: The two perpendicular readings are averaged to obtain a representative spread diameter that cancels out directional asymmetry.*

**Step 2 — Apply Flow Formula (3 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\%} &= \frac{D_{\text{mean}} - D_0}{D_0} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{43 - 25}{25} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{18}{25} \times 100 \\
&= 0.72 \times 100 \\
&= 72\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Classification (2 Marks):**
From the KTU classification table (Section 1.3):
- $F = 72\%$ falls in the **50% to 75%** range → **WET / FLUID consistency**.
- **Suitable for:** Self-compacting concrete (SCC) candidates, heavily reinforced thin sections, and tremie concreting.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 CRITICAL VALUATION PITFALLS — MARK-LOSS ZONES:**
>
> 1. **Formula Misuse Trap (-2 Marks):** Students often write the flow formula as
> $F = \frac{D_{\text{mean}}}{D_0} \times 100$ (missing the $-1$ term in the numerator). The correct form has $D_{\text{mean}} - D_0$ in the numerator. This single error costs **2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Diameter Confusion Trap (-2 Marks):** Using the **top diameter (10 cm)** of the cone or the **height (30 cm)** in flow % calculation. Always use the **base diameter (25 cm)**.
>
> 3. **Drops Confusion Trap (-1 Mark):** Using **25 drops** for concrete (this is for mortar). Concrete = **15 drops**.
>
> 4. **Unit Error Trap (-1 Mark):** Reporting Vee-Bee in **minutes** instead of **seconds**, or flow % as a decimal (0.55) instead of percentage (55%).
>
> 5. **No IS Code Trap (-1 to -2 Marks):** Failing to cite **IS 1199 (Part 1 or Part 2): 2018** in any procedure description. Always cite the IS code at least once.
>
> 6. **Single-Direction Spread Trap (-1 Mark):** Measuring spread in **only one direction** instead of two perpendicular directions. Always take $D_1$ AND $D_2$.
>
> 7. **Vee-Bee Direction Trap (-1 Mark):** Forgetting that Vee-Bee time is **inversely** proportional to workability. Higher seconds = LOWER workability.
>
> **Total potential mark loss from these traps: 8 to 10 marks out of 14** — i.e., they can fail a 14-mark question entirely.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 KTU MT-2 — Module 2 — Vee-Bee & Flow Test: Rapid Revision Flashcard**

### **A. Vee-Bee Test — Key Takeaways**

- [x] **IS Code:** IS 1199 (Part 2): 2018
- [x] **Vibration:** 50 Hz, 0.5 mm amplitude, 3000 vpm
- [x] **Number of Layers in Cone:** 4 layers, 25 tamps each
- [x] **Measurement:** Time in **seconds** for cone → Vee shape
- [x] **Result:** Vee-Bee Degrees (V-B sec) — **inverse to workability**
- [x] **Ideal Range for RCC:** 5 to 15 sec
- [x] **Best for:** Stiff mixes, pavements, mass concrete
- [x] **Limitation:** Not for very fluid or SCC mixes

### **B. Flow Test — Key Takeaways**

- [x] **IS Code:** IS 1199 (Part 1): 2018 (concrete) / IS 4031 Part 7: 2017 (mortar)
- [x] **Table Size:** 70 cm × 70 cm brass top
- [x] **Drop Height:** 12.5 mm (5/8 inch)
- [x] **Drops for Concrete:** **15 drops**
- [x] **Drops for Mortar:** **25 drops**
- [x] **Number of Layers in Cone:** 2 layers, 10 tamps each
- [x] **Original Base Diameter:** $D_0 = 25$ cm (CONSTANT — memorize!)
- [x] **Measurement:** Two perpendicular spread diameters $D_1$ and $D_2$
- [x] **Formula:** $F_{\%} = \frac{D_{\text{mean}} - 25}{25} \times 100$
- [x] **Result:** Flow Percentage — **direct relation to workability**
- [x] **Best for:** Plastic to fluid mixes, SCC screening, mortar

### **C. Cross-Test Comparison (Exam-Ready Points)**

| Feature | Vee-Bee | Flow Test |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Type** | Vibration | Impact (Drop) |
| **Output** | Seconds | Percentage |
| **Direction** | Inverse | Direct |
| **Concrete Layers** | 4 × 25 tamps | 2 × 10 tamps |
| **Drops / Frequency** | 3000 vpm, 50 Hz | 15 drops, 12.5 mm |
| **Base Ø $D_0$** | 20 cm (cone base) | 25 cm (cone base) |
| **Best Suited For** | Stiff mixes | Plastic to fluid mixes |
| **IS Code** | IS 1199 Part 2: 2018 | IS 1199 Part 1: 2018 |

### **D. Formula Quick-Recall (Flash Memory)**

- **Vee-Bee Degrees:** $V_B = t_{\text{end}} - t_{\text{start}}$ (in **seconds**)
- **Mean Spread:** $D_{\text{mean}} = \frac{D_1 + D_2}{2}$ (in **cm**)
- **Flow %:** $F_{\%} = \frac{D_{\text{mean}} - 25}{25} \times 100$ (in **%**)

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always **state the IS code**, **show the formula with units**, **substitute values with units**, and **interpret the result with a workability class** in any KTU lab exam answer. This 4-step structure guarantees full marks in the calculation portion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concrete Mix Design by IS Code Method and Casting of Cubes, Cylinders with Designed Concrete Mixes
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL508 - CONCRETE LAB (MT-2)
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Concrete Mix Design by IS Code Method and Casting of Cubes, Cylinders with Designed Concrete Mixes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Concrete Mix Design** is the scientific process of selecting suitable proportions of cement, water, fine aggregate (sand), and coarse aggregate (gravel) to produce concrete of desired **workability**, **strength**, **durability**, and **economy**.

According to **IS 10262:2019** ("Concrete Mix Proportioning — Guidelines"), mix design is a procedure for determining the **proportions by mass or volume** of the constituents of concrete, where the performance of the concrete is specified by the designer. The designed mix is also referred to as a **"Design Mix Concrete"** as opposed to the conventional **"Nominal Mix Concrete"** prescribed in **IS 456:2000**.

**Key Distinctions:**

| Term | Definition | Governing Code |
|---|---|---|
| **Nominal Mix** | Fixed ratio by volume (e.g., M20 → 1:1.5:3) | IS 456:2000, Table 9 |
| **Design Mix** | Proportions determined by laboratory testing of materials | IS 10262:2019, IS 456:2000 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For all grades of concrete ≥ **M20**, IS 456 mandates the use of **Design Mix Concrete**. Nominal mixes are permitted only for small-scale works (volume ≤ 5 m³) and for grades ≤ M20.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Concrete Recipe"

Think of concrete mix design as **baking a cake**:
- **Cement** is the *flour* — the binding backbone.
- **Water** is the *milk/eggs* — activates the binder and provides workability.
- **Fine aggregate (sand)** is the *sugar* — fills small voids.
- **Coarse aggregate (gravel)** is the *chocolate chips/nuts* — provides bulk and strength.
- **Admixtures** (optional) are the *flavoring agents* — improve specific properties.

Just as a chef adjusts ingredients based on the humidity, flour brand, and desired cake texture, a civil engineer adjusts concrete proportions based on aggregate moisture, cement strength class, and required structural performance.

### 1.3 Test Specimens (KTU Lab Focus)

| Specimen | Standard Size | Purpose | Curing Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cube** | 150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm | Compressive strength ($f_{ck}$) | 7 days & 28 days |
| **Cylinder** | 150 mm ⌀ × 300 mm height | Compressive strength & modulus of elasticity | 28 days |
| **Beam** | 100 mm × 100 mm × 500 mm | Flexural strength | 28 days |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Standards:** Cube compressive strength ($f_{ck,\text{cube}}$) ≈ **1.25 ×** cylinder compressive strength ($f_{ck,\text{cyl}}$) for normal-weight concrete.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Philosophy of IS 10262:2019 Method

The IS code method is built on three cornerstones:

1. **Target Mean Strength ($f_{ck,t}$)** — must exceed the characteristic strength to account for variability.
2. **Water-Cement (w/c) Ratio** — the most influential parameter governing strength and durability.
3. **Aggregate Grading & Packing** — determines the paste requirement and workability.

### 2.2 The Ten Logical Steps of IS 10262:2019 Mix Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize these 10 steps in sequence — they are the standard valuation rubric for any 14-mark mix design problem.

1. **Step 1 — Target Strength Calculation**
   The target mean compressive strength at 28 days is given by:

   $$f_{ck,t} = f_{ck} + 1.65 \times s$$

   where $s$ = standard deviation (N/mm²) based on the degree of quality control.

2. **Step 2 — Selection of w/c Ratio**
   Based on:
   - Target strength ($f_{ck,t}$)
   - Durability criteria (Table 5 of IS 456:2000)
   
   *Adopt the **lower** of the two values for safety.*

3. **Step 3 — Water Content Selection**
   Based on:
   - Nominal max aggregate size
   - Slump/workability requirement
   - Use of admixtures (reduces water by 5–20%)

4. **Step 4 — Cement Content Calculation**

   $$\text{Cement Content} = \frac{\text{Water Content}}{\text{w/c ratio}}$$

   *Check against minimum cement content from IS 456 durability Table 5.*

5. **Step 5 — Coarse Aggregate Proportion**
   Based on nominal max size and **fine aggregate grading zone** (I, II, III, IV).

6. **Step 6 — Mix Proportion by Mass**
   Compute fine and coarse aggregate masses using absolute volume method.

7. **Step 7 — Mix Proportion by Volume**
   Convert mass proportions to volume using bulk densities.

8. **Step 8 — Trial Mix Verification**
   Cast 3 cubes, test at 28 days, compare with target.

9. **Step 9 — Adjustment for Moisture Content**
   Account for aggregate absorption and surface moisture in field conditions.

10. **Step 10 — Final Mix Proportions**
    Document final proportions for site use.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_{ck,t} = f_{ck} + 1.65\,s$ | Target mean strength | N/mm² |
| 2 | $\text{w/c} = \dfrac{0.45\,f_{ck,t} + 11}{f_{ck,t} + 23}$ (approx. from IS curves) | w/c ratio from strength | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{Cement} = \dfrac{\text{Water}}{\text{w/c}}$ | Cement content | kg/m³ |
| 4 | $V_{\text{total}} = 1\,\text{m}^3$ | Absolute volume method basis | m³ |
| 5 | $V_c = \dfrac{M_c}{G_c \times \rho_w}$ | Absolute volume of cement | m³ |
| 6 | $V_w = \dfrac{M_w}{\rho_w}$ | Absolute volume of water | m³ |
| 7 | $V_{\text{void}} = 1 - (V_c + V_w + V_a + V_{fa})$ | Air content check | m³ |
| 8 | $\rho_c = \dfrac{M_c + M_{fa} + M_{ca} + M_w}{V_{\text{total}}}$ | Fresh concrete density | kg/m³ |
| 9 | $f_{cu} = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Cube compressive strength | N/mm² |
| 10 | $f_{\text{cyl}} \approx 0.8\,f_{\text{cube}}$ | Cylinder strength relation | N/mm² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Density Values Used in KTU Problems:**
> - Specific gravity of cement (OPC): **$G_c = 3.15$**
> - Specific gravity of water: $\rho_w = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3 = 1\,\text{g/cc}$
> - Specific gravity of sand (FA): $G_{fa} = 2.60$ to **$2.65$**
> - Specific gravity of coarse aggregate: $G_{ca} = 2.65$ to **$2.75$**

### 2.4 Standard Deviation Values (IS 10262:2019, Table 2)

| Degree of Quality Control | Standard Deviation $s$ (N/mm²) |
|---|---|
| Excellent (Large RMC plant) | **3.5** |
| Good (Site-controlled) | **4.0** |
| Fair / Average (Site laboratory) | **5.0** |
| Poor (No field control) | **6.0** |

> **Engineering Utility:** Mix design is the **backbone of modern construction** — every RMC (Ready-Mix Concrete) plant, every high-rise, every dam, every bridge pier relies on a properly designed concrete mix. Without it, structural safety and economy cannot be guaranteed.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Sample Problem (Full Mix Design Walk-through)

> **Sample Problem (KTU Model — 14 Mark):**
> Design a concrete mix of grade **M30** using IS 10262:2019 for the following data:
> - **Cement:** OPC 43 grade, specific gravity = 3.15
> - **Fine Aggregate (FA):** River sand, Zone II, specific gravity = 2.65, water absorption = 1.0%
> - **Coarse Aggregate (CA):** Crushed angular, 20 mm nominal max size, specific gravity = 2.74, water absorption = 0.5%
> - **Workability:** Slump = 75 mm (medium)
> - **Exposure:** Mild
> - **Quality Control:** Good
> - **Admixture:** Superplasticizer (1% by weight of cement)

---

### Step 1 — Target Strength Calculation

$$f_{ck,t} = f_{ck} + 1.65 \times s$$

For M30, $f_{ck} = 30\,\text{N/mm}^2$.
For "Good" quality control, $s = 4.0\,\text{N/mm}^2$.

$$f_{ck,t} = 30 + 1.65 \times 4.0$$

$$f_{ck,t} = 30 + 6.6 = 36.6\,\text{N/mm}^2$$

---

### Step 2 — Selection of w/c Ratio

**From strength curve (Fig. 1 of IS 10262):**
For $f_{ck,t} = 36.6\,\text{N/mm}^2$ and OPC 43 grade, the corresponding w/c ratio ≈ **0.42** (by graphical interpolation).

**From durability (IS 456, Table 5, Mild exposure):**
Maximum w/c ratio = **0.55** (for RCC), minimum cement = 300 kg/m³.

**Adopt the lower of the two:**

$$\left(\frac{w}{c}\right)_{\text{adopted}} = 0.42$$

---

### Step 3 — Water Content Selection

For 20 mm nominal max aggregate + 75 mm slump + crushed angular aggregate (Table 4, IS 10262):

$$W_{\text{water}} = 186\,\text{kg/m}^3 \quad (\text{baseline})$$

Adjustment for angular aggregate: **+10%** (since angular aggregates need more water than rounded).

$$W_{\text{water,adjusted}} = 186 \times 1.10 = 204.6\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

Adjustment for superplasticizer (1% dose → 20% water reduction):

$$W_{\text{water,final}} = 204.6 \times (1 - 0.20) = 163.7\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

> Let's round to $W_w = 164\,\text{kg/m}^3$.

---

### Step 4 — Cement Content Calculation

$$M_c = \frac{W_w}{(w/c)_{\text{adopted}}}$$

$$M_c = \frac{164}{0.42} = 390.5\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

Rounding:

$$M_c = 391\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

> **Durability check:** For mild exposure, minimum cement = 300 kg/m³. Adopted 391 > 300 ✓. Also, w/c = 0.42 < 0.55 ✓. **Durability satisfied.**

---

### Step 5 — Coarse Aggregate Proportion (Table 5, IS 10262)

For 20 mm aggregate, Zone II FA, w/c = 0.42:

$$\frac{V_{ca}}{V_{ca} + V_{fa}} = 0.64$$

This means CA occupies 64% of total aggregate volume.

---

### Step 6 — Mix Proportions by Mass (Absolute Volume Method)

**Step 6a — Compute absolute volumes of known materials:**

$$V_c = \frac{M_c}{G_c \times \rho_w} = \frac{391}{3.15 \times 1000} = 0.1241\,\text{m}^3$$

$$V_w = \frac{M_w}{\rho_w} = \frac{164}{1000} = 0.1640\,\text{m}^3$$

$$V_{\text{air}} = 0.01\,\text{m}^3 \quad (\text{assumed 1% entrapped air for 20 mm aggregate})$$

**Step 6b — Total aggregate volume:**

$$V_{fa} + V_{ca} = 1 - (V_c + V_w + V_{\text{air}})$$

$$V_{fa} + V_{ca} = 1 - (0.1241 + 0.1640 + 0.01) = 0.7019\,\text{m}^3$$

**Step 6c — Apply the 0.64 ratio:**

$$V_{ca} = 0.64 \times 0.7019 = 0.4492\,\text{m}^3$$

$$V_{fa} = 0.7019 - 0.4492 = 0.2527\,\text{m}^3$$

**Step 6d — Convert volumes to masses:**

$$M_{ca} = V_{ca} \times G_{ca} \times \rho_w = 0.4492 \times 2.74 \times 1000 = 1230.8\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

$$M_{fa} = V_{fa} \times G_{fa} \times \rho_w = 0.2527 \times 2.65 \times 1000 = 669.7\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

---

### Step 7 — Mix Proportion Summary

| Material | Mass (kg/m³) | Ratio by Mass |
|---|---|---|
| Cement | 391 | 1.00 |
| Water | 164 | 0.42 |
| Fine Aggregate | 670 | 1.71 |
| Coarse Aggregate | 1231 | 3.15 |
| Admixture (1% of cement) | 3.91 | 0.01 |

**Final Mix Ratio by Mass:**

$$\boxed{\text{Cement} : \text{FA} : \text{CA} = 1 : 1.71 : 3.15 \quad @\; w/c = 0.42}$$

---

### 3.2 Casting of Cubes (IS 516:1959 / IS 516:2018)

**Standard Cube Mold:** 150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm (steel, machined faces).

**Procedure:**
1. **Apply mould release agent** (oil/grease) on inner faces.
2. **Place concrete in 3 layers of ~50 mm each.**
3. **Compact each layer** with a tamping rod (16 mm dia, 600 mm long, bullet-nosed) using **35 strokes** distributed uniformly.
4. For vibrated concrete: use **vibrating table/needle vibrator** until surface becomes glossy.
5. **Strike off top surface** flush with trowel; smooth with float.
6. **Cure** for 24 hours at laboratory temperature (27 ± 2°C), then **demould** and **submerge in water** at 27 ± 2°C until testing.

> **Number of strokes = 35 (cube) and 25 per layer (cylinder).** This is a frequently asked **3-mark question**.

### 3.3 Casting of Cylinders (ASTM C192 / IS 516)

**Standard Cylinder Mold:** 150 mm diameter × 300 mm height.

**Procedure:**
1. Coat mould with release agent.
2. **Fill in 3 equal layers** (each ≈ 100 mm).
3. **Rod each layer 25 times** with 16 mm rod.
4. Tap sides gently to release air pockets.
5. Strike off top, cap with **sulphur mortar** or **neoprene cap** for testing.
6. Demould after 24 h, water-cure at 27 ± 2°C.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — IS 10262:2019 Mix Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Target Grade and Materials Data] --> B[Step 1: Compute Target Strength fck,t = fck + 1.65s]
    B --> C[Step 2: Select w/c Ratio from Strength Curve and Durability]
    C --> D[Step 3: Decide Water Content from Slump and Aggregate Size]
    D --> E[Step 4: Calculate Cement Content Cement = Water / w/c]
    E --> F{Durability Check: Min Cement Satisfied?}
    F -- No --> G[Increase Cement to Meet Minimum]
    F -- Yes --> H[Step 5: Decide CA Proportion from Table 5]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Step 6: Compute FA and CA Mass by Absolute Volume]
    I --> J[Step 7: Express Final Mix Ratio by Mass]
    J --> K[Step 8: Cast Trial Cubes and Cylinders]
    K --> L[Step 9: Test at 7 and 28 days for Strength]
    L --> M{28-day Strength Equals or Exceeds Target?}
    M -- No --> N[Adjust w/c or Cement Content and Redo]
    M -- Yes --> O[Step 10: Adjust for Field Moisture and Approve Mix]
    O --> P[End: Approved Design Mix Ready for Site Use]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Cube and Cylinder Casting Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prepare Cube 150mm and Cylinder 150x300mm Moulds] --> B[Apply Release Agent on Inner Surfaces]
    B --> C[Place Fresh Concrete in 3 Layers]
    C --> D[Compact Each Layer with Tamping Rod]
    D --> D1[35 strokes for cube]
    D --> D2[25 strokes for cylinder]
    D1 --> E[Strike Off Top Surface Flush]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Cover and Rest for 24 Hours at 27C]
    F --> G[Demould Specimens]
    G --> H[Submerge in Clean Water Tank for Curing]
    H --> I[Test at 7 days and 28 days]
    I --> J[Compute Compressive Strength f = P over A]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Material Interaction in Concrete

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph BINDERS
        C1[OPC Cement 43 or 53 Grade]
    end
    subgraph LIQUIDS
        W1[Potable Water]
        AD1[Chemical Admixtures]
    end
    subgraph AGGREGATES
        FA1[Fine Aggregate Sand Zone I to IV]
        CA1[Coarse Aggregate 10mm 20mm Nominal]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        CC1[Hardened Concrete with fck strength]
    end
    C1 --> MIX[Mixing and Hydration]
    W1 --> MIX
    AD1 --> MIX
    FA1 --> MIX
    CA1 --> MIX
    MIX --> CC1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define the term "Target Mean Strength" in concrete mix design. How is it calculated?*

**Model Answer:**
Target mean strength ($f_{ck,t}$) is the compressive strength that the concrete mix must achieve on average, accounting for the variability in production. It ensures that the characteristic strength ($f_{ck}$) is met with adequate reliability.

$$f_{ck,t} = f_{ck} + 1.65\,s$$

where $s$ is the standard deviation based on the degree of quality control. The factor **1.65** corresponds to a 95% probability of strength compliance under normal distribution.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*List any six key steps involved in concrete mix design as per IS 10262:2019.*

**Model Answer (any six):**
1. Determination of target mean strength.
2. Selection of water-cement ratio from strength and durability.
3. Estimation of water content for required workability.
4. Calculation of cement content.
5. Selection of coarse aggregate fraction (Table 5).
6. Calculation of fine and coarse aggregate by absolute volume method.
7. Conducting trial mixes and adjustment.
8. Conversion of mass proportions to volume ratios for field use.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Design a concrete mix of grade **M25** using IS 10262:2019 with the following data:
- Cement OPC 43 grade, $G_c = 3.15$
- Fine aggregate: Zone III, $G_{fa} = 2.60$, absorption = 1.5%
- Coarse aggregate: 20 mm nominal, $G_{ca} = 2.70$, absorption = 0.5%
- Workability: 50 mm slump
- Exposure: Moderate
- Quality control: Good ($s = 4.0$)
- No admixture

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** $f_{ck,t} = 25 + 1.65 \times 4 = 31.6\,\text{N/mm}^2$

**Step 2:** w/c from strength curve (interpolated for 31.6 N/mm²) ≈ **0.50**
w/c from durability (moderate, RCC) = **0.50** (IS 456, Table 5)
Adopt w/c = **0.50** (lower of the two, equal here).

**Step 3:** Water content for 20 mm aggregate + 50 mm slump (Table 4) = 186 kg/m³. No angular adjustment needed (already angular). No admixture. $W_w = 186\,\text{kg/m}^3$.

**Step 4:** $M_c = 186 / 0.50 = 372\,\text{kg/m}^3$
Min cement for moderate exposure = 300 kg/m³ ✓. w/c = 0.50 ✓.

**Step 5:** CA ratio for 20 mm + Zone III + w/c = 0.50 → 0.63 (Table 5)

**Step 6 — Absolute volumes:**
- $V_c = 372 / (3.15 \times 1000) = 0.1181\,\text{m}^3$
- $V_w = 186 / 1000 = 0.1860\,\text{m}^3$
- $V_{\text{air}} = 0.0100\,\text{m}^3$ (1% for 20 mm)
- $V_{fa} + V_{ca} = 1 - (0.1181 + 0.1860 + 0.0100) = 0.6859\,\text{m}^3$

- $V_{ca} = 0.63 \times 0.6859 = 0.4321\,\text{m}^3$
- $V_{fa} = 0.6859 - 0.4321 = 0.2538\,\text{m}^3$

- $M_{ca} = 0.4321 \times 2.70 \times 1000 = 1166.7\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- $M_{fa} = 0.2538 \times 2.60 \times 1000 = 659.9\,\text{kg/m}^3$

**Final Mix Ratio:**

$$\text{Cement : FA : CA} = 1 : 1.77 : 3.13 \quad @\; w/c = 0.50$$

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Describe the **step-by-step procedure for casting and curing of concrete cubes** as per IS 516. State the standard size and number of compaction strokes.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Mold Preparation:** Use standard 150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm steel cube molds; clean, oil inside surfaces.
2. **Filling in Layers:** Place concrete in **3 equal layers** of approximately 50 mm each.
3. **Compaction:** Rod each layer uniformly with a 16 mm diameter, 600 mm long bullet-nosed tamping rod using **35 strokes** per layer.
4. **Tapping/Vibration:** For vibrated concrete, use vibrating table or internal vibrator until the surface becomes glossy and air bubbles cease.
5. **Finishing:** Strike off the top surface flush with a trowel and smooth the top.
6. **Initial Curing:** Keep the specimen undisturbed at laboratory temperature (27 ± 2°C) for 24 ± 0.5 hours.
7. **Demoulding:** Remove the cube carefully and label.
8. **Water Curing:** Submerge the cubes in clean, fresh water at 27 ± 2°C until testing at 7 and 28 days.
9. **Testing:** Apply axial load at 14 N/mm²/min until failure; record peak load and compute $f_c = P/A$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Differentiate between **Design Mix** and **Nominal Mix** concrete. When is each type recommended per IS 456:2000?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Nominal Mix | Design Mix |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Fixed volumetric ratio (e.g., 1:2:4) | Proportions determined by lab testing |
| Basis | Empirical | Scientific (IS 10262:2019) |
| Strength control | Limited | Precise and economical |
| Application | Small works, M20 max | All major works, all grades |
| Cement control | Wastage possible | Optimized usage |
| Cost | Often uneconomical | Economical and quality-assured |

**IS 456:2000 Recommendation:**
- Nominal mix permitted only for grades ≤ M20 and volume of concrete ≤ 5 m³.
- For grades > M20, **design mix is mandatory.**

**(b)** *(7 marks)* With a neat flow diagram, explain the procedure for **casting of concrete cylinders** as per IS 516. Compare the compressive strength values of cubes and cylinders.

**Model Solution:**

**Cylinder Casting Procedure (IS 516):**
1. Mould: 150 mm diameter × 300 mm height cylindrical steel mould.
2. Apply mould oil; place on a firm level base.
3. Fill concrete in **3 equal layers of 100 mm each.**
4. Compact each layer with **25 strokes** of a 16 mm tamping rod.
5. Tap the mould sides 10–15 times with a mallet to release entrapped air.
6. Strike off top, smooth with trowel; cover with wet burlap.
7. After 24 ± 0.5 hours, demould and immerse in water at 27 ± 2°C for curing.
8. Before testing, cap the cylinder ends with **sulphur mortar (capping compound)** or use **neoprene pads** in the compression testing machine.
9. Apply load at **14 N/mm²/min** rate and record peak load.

**Comparison of Cube and Cylinder Strength:**

$$f_{ck,\text{cube}} \approx 1.25 \times f_{ck,\text{cylinder}}$$

Equivalently:

$$f_{ck,\text{cylinder}} \approx 0.80 \times f_{ck,\text{cube}}$$

This is because the cylinder specimen has a more uniform lateral confinement pattern; the cube is restrained by platen friction, which artificially increases the measured strength.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take the lower w/c** between strength and durability is a 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Mixing up specific gravity values** ($G_c$ vs $G_{fa}$ vs $G_{ca}$) is a common 1-mark error.
> 3. **Omitting air content** (0.5–2% based on aggregate size) in the absolute volume equation causes the answer to be off by 5–10%.
> 4. **Confusing tamping strokes:** 35 for cube, **25** for cylinder — a frequently asked difference.
> 5. **Not verifying** minimum cement for durability — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 6. Always **carry 3 decimal places** in absolute volume calculations to avoid cumulative rounding errors.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2**

- [x] **IS 10262:2019** governs **Design Mix**; IS 456:2000 covers **Nominal Mix** and durability.
- [x] **Target strength** $f_{ck,t} = f_{ck} + 1.65\,s$ — memorize the 1.65 factor.
- [x] **w/c ratio** is the **single most important** parameter for strength.
- [x] **Adopt the LOWER w/c** of strength and durability (IS 456).
- [x] **Cement content** = Water content ÷ w/c; must exceed **IS 456 minimum** (300–400 kg/m³ depending on exposure).
- [x] **Standard deviations** $s$: Excellent = 3.5, Good = 4.0, Fair = 5.0, Poor = 6.0.
- [x] **Specific gravity of OPC** = 3.15; water = 1.0; sand ≈ 2.60–2.65; CA ≈ 2.65–2.75.
- [x] **Cube** = 150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm; **35 tamping strokes per layer**; **3 layers**.
- [x] **Cylinder** = 150 mm ⌀ × 300 mm; **25 tamping strokes per layer**; **3 layers**.
- [x] **Curing temperature** = 27 ± 2°C in clean water; test at **7 and 28 days**.
- [x] **Loading rate** in compression test = **14 N/mm²/min** (IS 516).
- [x] **Cube strength ≈ 1.25 × Cylinder strength** (for normal-weight concrete).
- [x] **Absolute volume method:** $V_c + V_w + V_{fa} + V_{ca} + V_{\text{air}} = 1\,\text{m}^3$.
- [x] **Trial mix is mandatory** — every 14-mark question should end with a verification step.
- [x] **Admixture** reduces water by 5–20% (1% dose → ~20% water reduction with superplasticizers).
- [x] **Air content assumption:** 0.5% for 40 mm CA, **1% for 20 mm**, 1.5% for 12.5 mm, 2% for 10 mm.

> **Mnemonic for the 10 Steps — "T.W.W.C.C.A.A.V.M.A":**
> **T**arget strength → **W**/c ratio → **W**ater content → **C**ement content → **C**A fraction → **A**bsolute volumes → **A**ggregate mass → **V**erify trial → **M**oisture adjust → **A**pprove mix.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tests on Hardened Properties of Concrete: Compressive, Split and Flexural Strength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL508 - CONCRETE LAB (MT-2)
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Tests on Hardened Properties of Concrete: Compressive, Split and Flexural Strength

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — TESTS ON HARDENED PROPERTIES OF CONCRETE

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardened Concrete** is the concrete that has attained its design strength through the process of cement hydration and is capable of carrying the intended structural loads. The mechanical properties of hardened concrete are evaluated through standardized destructive tests as per **Indian Standard codes (IS 516, IS 5816, IS 14858)** and equivalent **ASTM C39, C78, C496** standards.

The three primary destructive strength tests performed on hardened concrete specimens in this experiment are:

| Test | Property Evaluated | Primary IS Code |
|------|--------------------|------------------|
| Compression Test | Compressive Strength (f_c) | IS 516 (Part 1/Sec 1) |
| Split Cylinder Test | Split Tensile Strength (f_ct) | IS 5816 |
| Flexure Test | Modulus of Rupture (f_b) | IS 516 (Part 1/Sec 4) |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Concrete Sandwich Analogy**
> Imagine a chocolate bar (the concrete cube) being slowly crushed between your palms. The maximum force your palms can press before the bar cracks into crumbs is its **compressive strength**. Now try to pull the bar apart from the top and bottom in the opposite direction (like tearing a sandwich) — the force needed is the **tensile strength**. Finally, hold the bar at both ends and bend it like a diving board — the force needed to snap it is the **flexural strength**. Concrete is strong in the first (pushing), but weak in the last two (pulling and bending) — that's why steel reinforcement is needed.

### 1.3 Specimen Geometry (Standard Sizes)

| Test | Specimen Shape | Standard Size (mm) |
|------|----------------|--------------------|
| Compression | Cube | **150 × 150 × 150** |
| Compression | Cylinder | **150 ⌀ × 300 height** |
| Split Tensile | Cylinder | **150 ⌀ × 300 height** |
| Flexure (Beam) | Prismatic | **150 × 150 × 700** span **600** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Test 1 — Compressive Strength of Concrete

### 2.1.1 Theory
Concrete is classified by its characteristic compressive strength at **28 days** of curing. The compressive strength test is the most universally accepted measure of concrete quality. The specimen is subjected to a uniaxial compressive load until failure.

**Key Principles:**
- Specimens are cast, demoulded after **24 ± 0.5 hours**, and cured in clean fresh water at **27 ± 2 °C**.
- The bearing surfaces of the testing machine are cleaned, and the load is applied without shock.
- The load is increased continuously at a constant rate of **140 kg/cm²/min** (≈ 13.73 N/mm²/min) for cubes.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Procedure
1. Prepare moulds, cast concrete in **3 layers**, each compacted with **35 strokes** of a tamping rod (16 mm ⌀, 600 mm long).
2. Cover specimens with wet gunny bags for **24 ± 0.5 hours**.
3. Demould and label the specimens, then submerge in curing tank.
4. At **7 days / 28 days**, remove specimen, wipe surface water, and place in CTM.
5. Align the specimen centrally between the platens.
6. Apply load at the specified rate until failure; record the **maximum load (P)**.
7. Calculate compressive strength: $f_c = \dfrac{P}{A}$

> [!TIP]
> **Why a 150 mm cube?**
> Smaller cubes (100 mm) over-estimate strength; larger ones (200 mm) under-estimate. The 150 mm cube provides a representative value, and the cylinder strength is roughly **0.8 × cube strength** for normal-weight concrete.

---

## 2.2 Test 2 — Split Tensile Strength (Brazilian Test)

### 2.2.1 Theory
Concrete is **weak in tension** — its direct tensile strength is only about **8 % to 12 %** of its compressive strength. Direct tension tests are difficult to perform due to gripping problems. Hence, the **indirect split cylinder test (IS 5816:1999)** is used. A standard cylinder is loaded diametrically in compression, producing a nearly uniform tensile stress acting horizontally across the loaded diameter. The specimen fails by splitting along the vertical diameter.

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Procedure
1. Cast cylinders of **150 mm ⌀ × 300 mm** height.
2. Cure for **7 / 28 days**.
3. Draw diametrical lines on both end faces to ensure proper alignment.
4. Place the cylinder on its side in the CTM with **packing strips** (3 mm plywood, 25 mm wide) on top and bottom (along the loaded diameter).
5. Apply load continuously at a rate of **1.2 to 2.4 N/mm²/min** until failure.
6. Record the maximum load **P** and note the failure pattern (should be a clean vertical split).

### 2.2.3 Theoretical Background (Why it Works)
The Brazilian test produces a horizontal tensile stress on the vertical diametral plane:

$$\sigma_t = \dfrac{2P}{\pi L D}$$

This formula is derived from **Frocht's 2D elasticity solution** for a cylinder loaded across a diameter. The maximum horizontal tensile stress at the centre equals the maximum vertical compressive stress at the contact points, making this a valid measure of tensile strength.

---

## 2.3 Test 3 — Flexural Strength of Concrete (Modulus of Rupture)

### 2.3.1 Theory
Flexural strength represents the ability of concrete to resist bending. It is critical in the design of **beams, slabs, and pavements**. Two loading configurations are permitted under IS 516:

- **Central Point Loading (3-point bending)** — produces a constant maximum bending moment only at the central section.
- **Two-Point (Third-Point) Loading (4-point bending)** — preferred because it produces a **constant bending moment** over the middle third of the span, ensuring the failure occurs at the weakest section in that region.

### 2.3.2 Step-by-Step Procedure
1. Cast beam specimens of size **150 × 150 × 700 mm** (tested on a **600 mm** span).
2. Cure for **7 / 28 days** in fresh water.
3. Place the beam on two steel rollers, **20 to 40 mm** diameter, at the third-points.
4. Apply load through a similar roller at the top.
5. Load rate ≈ **1.8 kN/min** (or producing a stress rate of **1.2 to 2.4 N/mm²/min**).
6. Record the **maximum load P** at failure; note the location of failure (within middle third = valid).
7. Calculate modulus of rupture using the appropriate formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb for flexural strength:**
> $f_b \approx 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (in N/mm²) — empirical relation widely used for plain concrete.

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

| Test | Specimen | Standard | Formula | Units |
|------|----------|----------|---------|-------|
| Compressive | Cube 150 mm | IS 516 | $f_c = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | N/mm² |
| Compressive | Cylinder 150 ⌀ | IS 516 | $f_c = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{4P}{\pi D^{2}}$ | N/mm² |
| Split Tensile | Cylinder 150 ⌀ | IS 5816 | $f_{ct} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi L D}$ | N/mm² |
| Flexural (Center) | Beam 150×150×700 | IS 516 | $f_b = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b d^{2}}$ | N/mm² |
| Flexural (Third-Point) | Beam 150×150×700 | IS 516 | $f_b = \dfrac{P L}{b d^{2}}$ | N/mm² |
| Load Rate (Cube) | Cube | IS 516 | $140 \text{ kg/cm}^{2}\text{/min}$ | ≈ 13.73 N/mm²/min |
| Load Rate (Split) | Cylinder | IS 5816 | $1.2$ to $2.4$ | N/mm²/min |
| Load Rate (Flexure) | Beam | IS 516 | $1.8$ kN/min or $1.2$–$2.4$ N/mm²/min | — |
| Curing Temp | All | — | $27 \pm 2$ | °C |
| Demoulding Time | All | — | $24 \pm 0.5$ | hours |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Compressive strength** governs the **characteristic strength (f_ck)** used in **IS 456:2000** for structural design.
- **Split tensile strength** is used in **ACI 318** and **IS 456** to estimate shear capacity, crack-width, and deflection.
- **Flexural strength** governs the design of **rigid pavements (IRC 44)**, **pavement-quality concrete**, and **plain concrete beams**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS, CALCULATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Compressive Strength — Detailed Calculation

**Given:** A 150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm cube fails under a maximum load of **815 kN** at 28 days.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$A = 150 \text{ mm} \times 150 \text{ mm} = 22{,}500 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum load conversion:**

$$P = 815 \text{ kN} = 815{,}000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Compressive strength:**

$$f_c = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{815{,}000}{22{,}500} = 36.22 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The compressive strength should be reported to the nearest **0.5 N/mm²**, per IS 516.

---

## 3.2 Split Tensile Strength — Detailed Derivation & Calculation

### 3.2.1 Derivation of the Brazilian Formula
For a 2D disc of diameter **D** and length **L** loaded diametrically with **P**, the horizontal tensile stress along the loaded diameter is given by the **Lamé / Frocht equation**:

$$\sigma_t = \dfrac{2P}{\pi L D}$$

**Derivation outline:**
1. The applied compressive force P over a thin strip of width $a$ (where $a \to 0$) produces contact stresses.
2. Using **Airy stress function** $\phi = A r^{2} \ln r + B r^{2} + C \ln r + D$, with boundary conditions, the resulting principal stresses at the centre of the disc are:
3. $\sigma_x$ (horizontal) = $\dfrac{2P}{\pi L D}$ (tensile)
4. $\sigma_y$ (vertical) = $\dfrac{-6P}{\pi L D}$ (compressive, 3× tensile)
5. Hence, the **horizontal tensile stress** is $\sigma_t = \dfrac{2P}{\pi L D}$ and failure occurs by splitting along the vertical plane.

### 3.2.2 Worked Numerical Problem
**Given:** A cylinder of **150 mm** diameter and **300 mm** length fails at a load of **185 kN** in the split-cylinder test.

**Step 1 — Convert load:**

$$P = 185 \text{ kN} = 185{,}000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Geometry:**

$$D = 150 \text{ mm}, \quad L = 300 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply formula:**

$$f_{ct} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi L D} = \dfrac{2 \times 185{,}000}{\pi \times 300 \times 150}$$

$$f_{ct} = \dfrac{370{,}000}{141{,}371.67} = 2.617 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check:** If cube strength is **30 N/mm²**, expected split tensile strength ≈ **0.35–0.5 × 30 = 10.5–15 MPa**? No — actually the relation is:

$$f_{ct} \approx 0.56 \sqrt{f_{ck}} = 0.56 \times \sqrt{30} \approx 3.07 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

Our answer (**2.62 N/mm²**) is reasonable and falls within **±15 %** of the empirical estimate. ✔

---

## 3.3 Flexural Strength — Detailed Derivation & Calculation

### 3.3.1 Theory of Pure Bending
Under bending, the **extreme fibre stress** in a rectangular section is given by:

$$f_b = \dfrac{M}{Z}$$

where $M$ = maximum bending moment, and $Z$ = section modulus $= \dfrac{b d^{2}}{6}$ (rectangular).

### 3.3.2 Derivation for Centre-Point Loading
For a simply supported beam of span **L** with a central load **P**:
1. Maximum bending moment at centre: $M = \dfrac{P L}{4}$
2. Section modulus: $Z = \dfrac{b d^{2}}{6}$
3. Substituting:

$$f_b = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{P L/4}{b d^{2}/6} = \dfrac{6 P L}{4 b d^{2}} = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b d^{2}}$$

**Final formula (centre-point):** $\boxed{f_b = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b d^{2}}}$

### 3.3.3 Derivation for Third-Point Loading
For two equal loads **P/2** placed at the third-points of a simply supported beam:
1. Reactions at supports: $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{P}{2}$
2. Shear force is zero between the two loads → constant moment in the middle third.
3. Maximum bending moment: $M = \dfrac{P L}{6}$ (at any point in middle third)
4. Section modulus: $Z = \dfrac{b d^{2}}{6}$
5. Substituting:

$$f_b = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{P L/6}{b d^{2}/6} = \dfrac{P L}{b d^{2}}$$

**Final formula (third-point):** $\boxed{f_b = \dfrac{P L}{b d^{2}}}$

> [!NOTE]
> The third-point method gives a **lower strength value** than the centre-point method (roughly **15 % lower**) because the maximum moment acts over the entire middle third, ensuring the failure occurs at the weakest section in that region.

### 3.3.4 Worked Numerical Problem — Third-Point Loading
**Given:** Beam **150 mm × 150 mm** cross-section, span **600 mm**, fails at **P = 16.2 kN** in the third-point flexure test.

**Step 1 — Maximum load:**

$$P = 16.2 \text{ kN} = 16{,}200 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Geometry:**

$$b = d = 150 \text{ mm}, \quad L = 600 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply third-point formula:**

$$f_b = \dfrac{P L}{b d^{2}} = \dfrac{16{,}200 \times 600}{150 \times 150^{2}} = \dfrac{9{,}720{,}000}{3{,}375{,}000}$$

$$f_b = 2.88 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Empirical cross-check:**
For $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm²: $f_b \approx 0.7 \sqrt{25} = 3.5$ N/mm². Our result (2.88) is plausible. ✔

---

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison

| Parameter | Compressive | Split Tensile | Flexural |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|----------|
| Specimen | Cube/Cylinder | Cylinder | Beam |
| Loading | Uniaxial compression | Diametral compression | Bending |
| Failure type | Crushing | Vertical split | Tensile crack at bottom |
| Result range (% of f_ck) | **100 %** | **8–12 %** | **10–20 %** |
| Sensitive to | Curing, compaction | Aggregate type, curing | Surface defects, fibres |
| Approximate inter-relation | $f_{ct} \approx 0.56 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | — | $f_b \approx 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Compressive Strength Test — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: Prepare 150 mm cube mould] --> B[StepB: Mix and cast concrete in 3 layers]
    B --> C[StepC: Compact each layer with 35 tamping strokes]
    C --> D[StepD: Cover with wet gunny bag 24 hours]
    D --> E[StepE: Demould and label specimen]
    E --> F[StepF: Submerge in curing tank 27 C]
    F --> G[StepG: Remove at 7 or 28 days]
    G --> H[StepH: Wipe surface water and place in CTM]
    H --> I[StepI: Apply load at 13.73 N per mm sq per min]
    I --> J[StepJ: Record peak load P]
    J --> K[StepK: Calculate fc equals P by A]
    K --> L[StepL: Report fc to nearest 0.5 N per mm sq]
```

## 4.2 Split Tensile Strength Test — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: Cast cylinder 150 mm dia 300 mm] --> B[StepB: Cure for 7 or 28 days]
    B --> C[StepC: Mark diametral lines on end faces]
    C --> D[StepD: Place cylinder horizontally on CTM platen]
    D --> E[StepE: Insert 3 mm plywood packing strips top and bottom]
    E --> F[StepF: Align cylinder to marked diametral line]
    F --> G[StepG: Apply load at 1.2 to 2.4 N per mm sq per min]
    G --> H[StepH: Specimen splits along vertical diameter]
    H --> I[StepI: Record maximum load P]
    I --> J[StepJ: Calculate fct equals 2P over pi LD]
    J --> K[StepK: Report as split tensile strength]
```

## 4.3 Flexural Strength Test — Third-Point Loading Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: Cast beam 150 by 150 by 700 mm] --> B[StepB: Cure for 7 or 28 days]
    B --> C[StepC: Place beam on two bottom rollers span 600 mm]
    C --> D[StepD: Position top loading rollers at third points]
    D --> E[StepE: Apply load at 1.8 kN per min rate]
    E --> F[StepF: Maximum moment in middle third region]
    F --> G[StepG: Tensile crack at bottom extreme fibre]
    G --> H[StepH: Record peak load P and crack location]
    H --> I{I: Crack in middle third?}
    I -->|Yes valid| J[StepJ: Calculate fb equals PL over bd squared]
    I -->|No invalid| K[StepK: Discard and rerun test]
    J --> L[StepL: Report modulus of rupture]
```

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Stress State in Brazilian Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[StepA: Vertical compressive force P] --> B[StepB: Plywood strip contact]
    B --> C[StepC: Vertical compressive stress 6P over pi LD]
    C --> D[StepD: Horizontal tensile stress 2P over pi LD]
    D --> E[StepE: Maximum at vertical diametral plane centre]
    E --> F[StepF: Tensile crack propagates vertically]
    F --> G[StepG: Cylinder splits into two halves]
```

## 4.5 Specimen Geometry Summary Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hardened Concrete Test Specimens] --> B[Cube 150 by 150 by 150 mm]
    A --> C[Cylinder 150 mm dia 300 mm length]
    A --> D[Beam 150 by 150 by 700 mm]
    B --> B1[Used for Compressive Strength per IS 516]
    C --> C1[Used for Compressive Strength per IS 516]
    C --> C2[Used for Split Tensile per IS 5816]
    D --> D1[Used for Flexural Strength per IS 516]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 model]
**Define compressive strength of concrete and state the standard specimen size and loading rate as per IS 516.**

**Model Answer:**
The compressive strength of concrete is defined as the maximum compressive load a hardened concrete specimen can sustain per unit cross-sectional area before failure. As per **IS 516**, the standard specimen is a **150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm cube** (or 150 mm ⌀ × 300 mm cylinder). The load is applied continuously at a rate of **140 kg/cm²/min** (≈ **13.73 N/mm²/min**) without shock. The compressive strength is calculated as $f_c = P/A$, where $P$ is the failure load and $A$ is the cross-sectional area. Specimens are cured in water at **27 ± 2 °C** and tested at **7 and 28 days**.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]
**State the IS code and formula used to determine the split tensile strength of concrete.**

**Model Answer:**
The **split tensile strength** of concrete is determined by the **Brazilian test** as per **IS 5816:1999**, using a standard cylinder of **150 mm diameter and 300 mm length**. The cylinder is placed horizontally and loaded diametrically in compression using plywood packing strips. The formula is:

$$f_{ct} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi L D}$$

where $P$ = maximum load (N), $L$ = length of cylinder (mm), $D$ = diameter (mm). The load is applied at a rate of **1.2 to 2.4 N/mm²/min**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ➤ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the procedure to determine the compressive strength of concrete as per IS 516, with a neat sketch of the test setup. State the factors affecting compressive strength.

**Model Solution:**

**Procedure (IS 516):**
1. Cast concrete in **150 mm cube moulds** in **three layers**, each compacted with **35 strokes** of a 16 mm tamping rod.
2. Cover specimens with wet gunny bags; demould after **24 ± 0.5 hours**.
3. Submerge specimens in clean fresh water at **27 ± 2 °C** for curing until the test age (**7 or 28 days**).
4. Remove the specimen; wipe surface water with a cloth.
5. Place the cube centrally in the **Compression Testing Machine (CTM)** between the platens.
6. Apply load **continuously and uniformly** at a rate of **140 kg/cm²/min (13.73 N/mm²/min)** until failure.
7. Note the **maximum load (P)** at failure.
8. Calculate: $f_c = P/A = P / 22{,}500$ (for 150 mm cube).
9. Take the **average of three specimens**; report to the nearest **0.5 N/mm²**.

**Sketch of test setup:** A cube placed between two rigid platens of CTM, load applied vertically.

**Factors affecting compressive strength:**
- Water-cement ratio (most significant)
- Cement content and type
- Aggregate type, size, and grading
- Curing conditions (temperature, duration)
- Compaction quality
- Age of concrete
- Admixtures and additives

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a compression test, three 150 mm cubes of M25 concrete failed at loads of **715 kN, 740 kN, and 720 kN** at 28 days. Calculate the average compressive strength and verify whether it meets the target mean strength as per IS 456.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Area of cube:**

$$A = 150 \times 150 = 22{,}500 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Individual strengths:**

$$f_{c1} = \dfrac{715{,}000}{22{,}500} = 31.78 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

$$f_{c2} = \dfrac{740{,}000}{22{,}500} = 32.89 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

$$f_{c3} = \dfrac{720{,}000}{22{,}500} = 32.00 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Average compressive strength:**

$$f_{cm} = \dfrac{31.78 + 32.89 + 32.00}{3} = \dfrac{96.67}{3} = 32.22 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Target mean strength as per IS 456:**

$$f_{cm,target} = f_{ck} + 1.65 s$$

Assuming standard deviation $s = 4$ N/mm² (good site control):

$$f_{cm,target} = 25 + 1.65 \times 4 = 25 + 6.6 = 31.6 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$$f_{cm} = 32.22 \text{ N/mm}^{2} > 31.6 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The mix satisfies the target mean strength criterion. ✔

---

### ➤ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the split tensile strength test for concrete with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for split tensile strength.

**Model Solution:**

**Test Description (IS 5816:1999):**
The split tensile test is an **indirect** method of determining the tensile strength of concrete. A standard cylinder (**150 mm ⌀ × 300 mm** long) is placed **horizontally** in a CTM and loaded **diametrically** in compression using thin plywood strips.

**Diagram description (text-based):**
A horizontal cylinder rests on the lower platen. Two plywood packing strips (3 mm thick, 25 mm wide) are placed on the top and bottom along the diametral line. The load is applied through these strips, creating a horizontal tensile stress across the vertical diametral plane.

**Derivation:**
For a 2D disc of diameter $D$ and thickness $L$ loaded with force $P$ along its diameter, the principal stresses at the centre of the disc (Frocht's elasticity solution) are:

$$\sigma_x = \dfrac{2P}{\pi L D} \quad \text{(tensile, horizontal)}$$

$$\sigma_y = \dfrac{-6P}{\pi L D} \quad \text{(compressive, vertical)}$$

The failure occurs by splitting along the vertical plane, since concrete is weak in tension. Hence the split tensile strength is:

$$f_{ct} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi L D}$$

**Procedure:**
1. Cast cylinder; cure for 7/28 days.
2. Draw diametrical lines on both end faces.
3. Place cylinder on side; align diametrical line with packing strip.
4. Apply load at **1.2 to 2.4 N/mm²/min**.
5. Record peak load $P$.
6. Calculate $f_{ct} = 2P/(\pi L D)$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 150 mm × 300 mm concrete cylinder failed in the split tensile test at a load of **190 kN**. Calculate the split tensile strength and estimate the corresponding cube compressive strength using the empirical relation $f_{ct} = 0.56 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert load:**

$$P = 190 \text{ kN} = 190{,}000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Apply formula:**

$$f_{ct} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi L D} = \dfrac{2 \times 190{,}000}{\pi \times 300 \times 150} = \dfrac{380{,}000}{141{,}371.67} = 2.688 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Estimate cube strength from $f_{ct} = 0.56 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$:**

$$2.688 = 0.56 \sqrt{f_{ck}} \;\Rightarrow\; \sqrt{f_{ck}} = \dfrac{2.688}{0.56} = 4.80$$

$$f_{ck} = 4.80^{2} = 23.04 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Conclusion:** The estimated cube compressive strength is **≈ 23 N/mm²**, suggesting the mix is close to **M20 grade** (characteristic strength 20 N/mm²).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** — load is given in kN but formula requires **N**. Always multiply kN by 1000.
> 2. **Wrong formula in flexure** — students confuse the **centre-point** ($3PL/2bd^2$) and **third-point** ($PL/bd^2$) formulas. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Mixing up span length** — for a beam of length 700 mm, the test span is **600 mm**, not 700 mm. Loading rollers are placed at the **third points** (i.e., 200 mm and 400 mm from one support).
> 4. **Reporting in wrong units** — $f_c$ should be in **N/mm²** (= MPa), not in kg/cm².
> 5. **Ignoring failure location validity** — in flexure test, if the crack occurs **outside the middle third**, the result is **invalid** as per IS 516.
> 6. **Using the wrong cylinder dimensions** — split tensile uses **D = 150 mm, L = 300 mm**, not cube dimensions.
> 7. **Not averaging three specimens** — IS 516 mandates reporting the **average of three** test results.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Exam Ready**

### Specimen Sizes (Must Memorize)
- **Cube:** **150 × 150 × 150 mm** (IS 516)
- **Cylinder:** **150 mm ⌀ × 300 mm** (IS 516 / IS 5816)
- **Beam:** **150 × 150 × 700 mm** (tested on **600 mm span**)

### Curing & Test Age
- Demoulding: **24 ± 0.5 hours**
- Curing temperature: **27 ± 2 °C**
- Test ages: **7 days** and **28 days** (characteristic)

### Loading Rates (Critical for KTU Lab)
- **Cube compression:** 140 kg/cm²/min ≈ **13.73 N/mm²/min**
- **Cylinder compression:** **0.6 ± 0.4 N/mm²/min** (ASTM)
- **Split tensile:** **1.2 to 2.4 N/mm²/min**
- **Flexure:** **1.8 kN/min** OR stress rate of **1.2 to 2.4 N/mm²/min**

### Key Formulas (One-Line Recall)
- $f_c = P/A$  *(cube & cylinder compression)*
- $f_{ct} = 2P/(\pi L D)$  *(split cylinder)*
- $f_b = 3PL/(2bd^2)$  *(centre-point flexure)*
- $f_b = PL/(bd^2)$  *(third-point flexure)*

### Empirical Inter-Relations
- $f_{ct} \approx 0.56 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$
- $f_b \approx 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$
- $f_{ct} \approx 0.1 \text{ to } 0.2 \times f_{ck}$
- Cylinder strength $\approx 0.8 \times$ cube strength

### IS Codes to Quote in Answers
- **IS 516 (Part 1/Sec 1):2021** — Compressive strength
- **IS 516 (Part 1/Sec 4):2021** — Flexural strength
- **IS 5816:1999** — Split tensile strength
- **IS 14858:2000** — Compression testing machine requirements
- **IS 456:2000** — Plain and reinforced concrete (for $f_{ck}$ definition)

### Exam-Day Golden Rules
1. Always convert kN → N before substitution.
2. Average **three** specimens and report to **0.5 N/mm²**.
3. State the **IS code** in every answer.
4. Mention **loading rate** when describing a test.
5. Always draw a **neat labeled sketch** in descriptive answers (carries 1–2 marks).
6. Verify the **validity of failure** location in flexure test.

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme | PCCEL508 Concrete Lab**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tests on Hardened Properties of Concrete: Modulus of Elasticity of Concrete
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL508 - CONCRETE LAB (MT-2)
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Tests on Hardened Properties of Concrete: Modulus of Elasticity of Concrete

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Modulus of Elasticity of Concrete** (denoted as $E_c$) is defined as the ratio of the applied compressive stress ($\sigma$) to the corresponding longitudinal compressive strain ($\varepsilon$) within the **proportional elastic limit** of the concrete. It quantifies the inherent stiffness and the resistance of hardened concrete against elastic deformation when subjected to an axial compressive load.

In strict accordance with **IS 516:1959 (Methods of Tests for Strength of Concrete)** and **IS 456:2000 (Plain and Reinforced Concrete – Code of Practice)**, the modulus of elasticity is experimentally determined by subjecting a standard cylindrical concrete specimen to a **gradual, monotonic uniaxial compressive load** and measuring the corresponding shortening.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCEL508 – Module 2):**
> The student is expected to determine the **Static Modulus of Elasticity in Compression** using standard cylindrical specimens, plot the **Stress–Strain Curve**, and compute the **Secant Modulus** at one-third of the ultimate compressive strength, as per IS 516 and IS 1343 provisions.

## 1.2 The Three Key Variants of Elastic Modulus

Concrete being a **quasi-brittle, heterogeneous, and non-linear material** does not possess a single, unique value of Young's Modulus. Therefore, four distinct values are recognized in civil engineering practice:

| Variant | Symbol | Description |
|---------|--------|-------------|
| **Initial Tangent Modulus** | $E_i$ | Slope of the tangent drawn to the stress–strain curve at the **origin** (point of zero stress). |
| **Tangent Modulus** | $E_t$ | Slope of the tangent drawn to the curve at **any specific point** (usually at 25–50% of $f_c$). |
| **Secant Modulus** | $E_s$ | Slope of the secant line joining the **origin** to a designated point on the curve (typically at $\frac{1}{3} f_c$ as per IS). |
| **Chord Modulus (ASTM)** | – | Slope of the chord joining two pre-defined points on the stress–strain curve. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Practice:** IS 456:2000 and IS 516:1959 both endorse the **Secant Modulus at $\frac{1}{3} f_c$** as the design value of modulus of elasticity for structural concrete, where $f_c$ is the **ultimate cylinder compressive strength**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are sitting on a newly built concrete bench in a park. You will notice that it bends — or rather, *deforms* — *just a tiny bit* under your weight. Now, if you replace that bench with one made of solid steel, it will deform *much less* under the same weight.

This intrinsic property of a material to **resist deformation** under load is what we call **stiffness**. The Modulus of Elasticity is the **mathematical score** of this stiffness.

- A **high** $E_c$ → concrete is **stiff** (deforms less).
- A **low** $E_c$ → concrete is **flexible** (deforms more).

Think of the concrete cylinder in the lab as a **compressed spring**. The Modulus of Elasticity tells you how *stiff* that spring is — the higher the value, the harder it is to compress.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values (as per IS 456:2000, Clause 6.2.3.1):**
> - Empirical formula: $E_c = 5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ **N/mm²** (or MPa)
> - Where $f_{ck}$ = characteristic cube compressive strength at 28 days (in MPa)
> - **Standard Cylinder Size:** 150 mm diameter × 300 mm height
> - **Loading Rate:** 14 MPa/min (≈ 140 kg/cm²/min) as per IS 516
> - **Design Modulus Reference Point:** Secant modulus at $\frac{1}{3} f_c$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations – Why and How

### A. The Stress–Strain Behaviour of Concrete

Concrete is a **composite material** made of cement paste, aggregates, and interfacial transition zones (ITZ). Because of this microstructural heterogeneity:

- Concrete **does not follow Hooke's Law** strictly across its full stress range.
- The stress–strain response is **curvilinear from the very beginning** of loading.
- The slope (i.e., $E$) **decreases progressively** as stress increases.
- This non-linearity arises due to **progressive micro-cracking** at the aggregate–paste interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the curve bends:** Even at low stress levels (~30% of $f_c$), internal micro-cracks initiate at weak ITZ regions, causing the effective stiffness to drop continuously.

### B. The Logical Steps to Determine $E_c$ (Lab Procedure)

1. **Cast & Cure:** Prepare a standard cylindrical specimen (150 mm Ø × 300 mm height) and cure for **28 days** in clean potable water at 27 ± 2 °C.
2. **Surface Preparation:** Cap the specimen with **sulphur capping or capping compound** to ensure uniform load distribution.
3. **Mounting:** Place the specimen centrally on the lower platen of a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** of capacity ≥ 2000 kN.
4. **Pre-Loading (Seating Load):** Apply a small initial load (~50 kN) to seat the specimen properly.
5. **Dial Gauge Attachment:** Fix a **compressometer** (with dial gauge of least count 0.002 mm) or two dial gauges mounted diametrically opposite on a compressometer frame to measure longitudinal deformation.
6. **Loading Protocol:** Apply the load **incrementally** in equal stages of approximately **1/10 of the expected ultimate load** at a uniform rate of **14 MPa/min**.
7. **Recording:** At each load increment, note the **load (P)** and the corresponding **deformation (δ)**.
8. **Continue** until failure and record the **ultimate load ($P_{max}$)**.
9. **Plot** the **Stress–Strain Curve** ($\sigma$ on Y-axis, $\varepsilon$ on X-axis).
10. **Compute** the **Secant Modulus** as the slope of the line joining the origin to the point corresponding to **$\frac{1}{3} f_c$**.

### C. Why Secant Modulus at $\frac{1}{3} f_c$?

- At $\frac{1}{3} f_c$, the concrete is still in the **stable, largely elastic range** (micro-cracking is minimal).
- It represents a **realistic service-load condition** since design working stresses are typically a fraction of $f_c$.
- The IS code adopted this convention to ensure **consistency and safety** in structural design.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Compressive stress on the specimen | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\delta}{L}$ | Longitudinal strain (deformation ÷ gauge length) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $E_c = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Modulus of Elasticity (definition) | N/mm² (MPa) or GPa |
| 4 | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Cross-sectional area of cylinder (D = 150 mm) | mm² |
| 5 | $f_c = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A}$ | Ultimate cylinder compressive strength | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 6 | $E_c = 5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | **IS 456:2000 Empirical Relation** for design modulus | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 7 | $\sigma_{1/3} = \dfrac{f_c}{3}$ | Stress at the secant reference point | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 8 | $E_{secant} = \dfrac{\sigma_{1/3}}{\varepsilon_{1/3}}$ | Secant modulus at $\frac{1}{3} f_c$ | N/mm² (MPa) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Never use the vertical pipe `\|` symbol inside a markdown table cell. Always use LaTeX commands like `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking table formatting.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Deflection Control:** Used in calculating **short-term and long-term deflections** of beams, slabs, and columns in RCC structures.
- **Buckling Analysis:** Critical in the design of **slender columns** where second-order effects depend directly on $E_c$.
- **Prestressed Concrete:** The **loss of prestress** due to elastic shortening of concrete is computed using $E_c$.
- **Dynamic Analysis:** The **natural frequency** of a structure ($\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, where $k \propto E_c \cdot I$) depends on modulus of elasticity.
- **Stiffness Compatibility:** Ensures **compatibility of strains** between concrete and reinforcing steel in serviceability limit state (SLS) design.
- **Finite Element Modelling (FEM):** $E_c$ is a primary input parameter for any **numerical simulation** of concrete structures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Secant Modulus from First Principles

The **secant modulus** is the slope of a straight line drawn from the **origin (0,0)** of the stress–strain plot to a specific data point corresponding to a stress of $\frac{1}{3} f_c$.

**Step 1 — Express Stress in Terms of Load:**

For a cylindrical specimen of diameter $D$, the cross-sectional area is:

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}
$$

The applied compressive stress corresponding to a load $P$ is:

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{4P}{\pi D^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express Strain in Terms of Deformation:**

If the gauge length over which deformation is measured is $L$ (typically 200 mm for compressometer) and the recorded shortening is $\delta$:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\delta}{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Definition of Secant Modulus:**

$$
E_{secant} = \frac{\sigma_{1/3}}{\varepsilon_{1/3}}
$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:

$$
E_{secant} = \frac{ \dfrac{4 P_{1/3}}{\pi D^{2}} }{ \dfrac{\delta_{1/3}}{L} } = \frac{4 P_{1/3} \cdot L}{\pi D^{2} \cdot \delta_{1/3}}
$$

Where $P_{1/3}$ is the load corresponding to $\frac{1}{3} f_c$ and $\delta_{1/3}$ is the corresponding deformation.

**Step 4 — Practical Computation (Tabular Form):**

For each load increment $P_i$ in the experiment:

$$
\sigma_i = \frac{4 P_i}{\pi D^{2}} \quad ; \quad \varepsilon_i = \frac{\delta_i}{L} \quad ; \quad E_i = \frac{\sigma_i}{\varepsilon_i}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

**Given Data:**

- Cylinder diameter: $D = 150$ mm
- Cylinder height: $H = 300$ mm
- Gauge length of compressometer: $L = 200$ mm
- Observed ultimate load: $P_{max} = 480$ kN
- Deformation at $\frac{1}{3} P_{max}$: $\delta_{1/3} = 0.42$ mm

**Required:** Compute the **Modulus of Elasticity (Secant)** of concrete.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional Area:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi (150)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 22500}{4} = 17671.46 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Ultimate Compressive Strength:**

$$
f_c = \frac{P_{max}}{A} = \frac{480 \times 10^{3}}{17671.46} = 27.16 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \;\; (\text{MPa})
$$

**Step 3 — Load at One-Third Strength:**

$$
P_{1/3} = \frac{P_{max}}{3} = \frac{480}{3} = 160 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Stress at One-Third Strength:**

$$
\sigma_{1/3} = \frac{P_{1/3}}{A} = \frac{160 \times 10^{3}}{17671.46} = 9.05 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Strain at One-Third Strength:**

$$
\varepsilon_{1/3} = \frac{\delta_{1/3}}{L} = \frac{0.42}{200} = 0.00210
$$

**Step 6 — Secant Modulus:**

$$
E_{secant} = \frac{\sigma_{1/3}}{\varepsilon_{1/3}} = \frac{9.05}{0.00210} = 4309.52 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{secant} \approx 4.31 \times 10^{4} \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 31.4 \text{ GPa}}
$$

**Step 7 — Verification Using IS 456 Empirical Formula:**

For a characteristic strength of 25 MPa (typical M25 concrete):

$$
E_c = 5000 \sqrt{25} = 5000 \times 5 = 25000 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 25 \text{ GPa}
$$

(Note: The discrepancy arises because IS 456 is conservative. The experimental value will be higher but within a 15–20% tolerance.)

## 3.3 Tabular Format for Laboratory Recording

| Sl. No. | Load $P$ (kN) | Stress $\sigma$ (MPa) | Deformation $\delta$ (mm) | Strain $\varepsilon$ (×10⁻⁶) | $E_i = \sigma/\varepsilon$ (MPa) |
|:-------:|:-------------:|:---------------------:|:-------------------------:|:---------------------------:|:---------------------------------:|
| 1 | 50 | 2.83 | 0.012 | 60 | 47,167 |
| 2 | 100 | 5.66 | 0.026 | 130 | 43,538 |
| 3 | 150 | 8.49 | 0.041 | 205 | 41,415 |
| 4 | 200 | 11.32 | 0.058 | 290 | 39,034 |
| 5 | 250 | 14.15 | 0.078 | 390 | 36,282 |
| 6 | 300 | 16.98 | 0.102 | 510 | 33,294 |
| 7 | 350 | 19.81 | 0.131 | 655 | 30,244 |
| 8 | 400 | 22.63 | 0.166 | 830 | 27,265 |
| 9 | 450 | 25.46 | 0.211 | 1055 | 24,133 |
| 10 | 480 (Ult.) | 27.16 | – | – | – |

> [!NOTE]
> The table above clearly illustrates the **progressive reduction in the tangent slope** $E_i$ as the stress level increases — a hallmark of concrete's non-linear elastic behaviour.

## 3.4 Derivation of the IS 456 Empirical Formula (Background)

The empirical relation $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ is derived from **statistical regression analysis** of extensive experimental data correlating cylinder compressive strength and elastic modulus across a wide range of concrete mixes. The general form can be written as:

$$
E_c = K \cdot (f_{ck})^{n}
$$

Where empirical constants (per IS 456) are:

$$
K = 5000, \quad n = 0.5
$$

This formula yields the **short-term static modulus** under standard conditions and does **not** account for creep, sustained loading, or long-term effects.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow – Testing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Specimen Preparation"] --> B["Curing for 28 Days"]
    B --> C["Capping and Surface Preparation"]
    C --> D["Mounting in UTM with Compressometer"]
    D --> E["Apply Seating Load"]
    E --> F["Incremental Axial Loading at 14 MPa per min"]
    F --> G["Record Load and Deformation at Each Step"]
    G --> H{"Load Reaches Ultimate?"}
    H -- No --> F
    H -- Yes --> I["Record Ultimate Load Pmax"]
    I --> J["Compute Stress and Strain Values"]
    J --> K["Plot Stress Strain Curve"]
    K --> L["Identify Secant Point at One Third fc"]
    L --> M["Calculate Secant Modulus Ec"]
    M --> N["Validate Against IS 456 Empirical Formula"]
    N --> O["Report Result in GPa"]
```

## 4.2 Test Setup Schematic (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UTM["Universal Testing Machine"]
        PLT1["Upper Platen with Spherical Seat"]
        PLT2["Lower Platen Fixed Base"]
    end
    SPEC["Concrete Cylinder 150mm dia x 300mm height"]
    COMP["Compressometer Frame with Two Dial Gauges"]
    LOAD["Hydraulic Load Application System"]
    REC["Data Acquisition Load Cell and Dial Gauges"]

    PLT1 --> SPEC
    SPEC --> PLT2
    SPEC -.-> COMP
    LOAD --> PLT1
    COMP --> REC
    PLT2 --> REC
```

## 4.3 Typical Stress–Strain Curve for Concrete

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Origin Zero Stress"] -->|Curvilinear Rising Path| B["Point at 1 by 3 fc Secant Modulus Reference"]
    B -->|Continued Curvature| C["Peak Point at fc Ultimate Strength"]
    C -->|Descending Branch| D["Failure Point"]
```

**Description of Curve Characteristics:**

- **Region O → B (0 to ~30% of $f_c$):** Nearly linear, mostly elastic.
- **Region B → C (30% to 100% of $f_c$):** Increasing non-linearity due to internal micro-cracking.
- **Region C → D (Post-peak):** Descending branch representing strain-softening and macro-crack propagation.

## 4.4 Comparison of Elastic Modulus Types (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Stress Strain Curve of Concrete"] --> B["Tangent at Origin - Initial Tangent Modulus Ei"]
    A --> C["Tangent at Midpoint - Tangent Modulus Et"]
    A --> D["Secant from Origin to One Third fc - Secant Modulus Es IS Design Value"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Value:** IS 456:2000 mandates the use of the **Secant Modulus** at $\frac{1}{3} f_c$ for all design calculations. This is the conservative, code-prescribed value.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define Modulus of Elasticity of concrete. Why is the secant modulus at one-third of the ultimate strength used as the design value?**

**Model Answer:**

The modulus of elasticity of concrete ($E_c$) is the ratio of compressive stress to the corresponding longitudinal strain within the elastic range, expressed as $E_c = \sigma/\varepsilon$.

The secant modulus at one-third of the ultimate compressive strength is used as the design value because:

- At this stress level, concrete behaves in a **largely linear and elastic** manner.
- It simulates realistic **service-load conditions** in actual structures.
- It provides a **conservative and consistent** value across varying concrete mixes.
- It is **standardized by IS 456:2000** to ensure uniform design practices.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **State the empirical formula for the modulus of elasticity of concrete as per IS 456:2000 and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
E_c = 5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}} \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

Where:

- $E_c$ = Short-term static modulus of elasticity of concrete (N/mm²)
- $f_{ck}$ = Characteristic cube compressive strength of concrete at 28 days (N/mm²)
- The constant **5000** and the exponent **0.5** are empirically derived from extensive experimental data.

**Significance:** This formula allows designers to estimate $E_c$ directly from the concrete grade without performing a laboratory test, facilitating deflection and stiffness calculations.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** Describe the procedure to determine the **modulus of elasticity of hardened concrete** as per **IS 516:1959**, with a neat sketch of the test setup. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A concrete cylinder of 150 mm diameter and 300 mm height was tested in a UTM. The ultimate load was 460 kN. At one-third of the ultimate load, the deformation was 0.38 mm (gauge length = 200 mm). Calculate the **secant modulus of elasticity** and verify the result with the IS 456 empirical formula assuming M25 grade concrete. (7 Marks)

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) – Test Procedure (7 Marks)

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Universal Testing Machine (capacity ≥ 2000 kN) with load pacing device
2. Compressometer (with two dial gauges of LC 0.002 mm)
3. Capping apparatus (sulphur or capping compound)
4. Cylindrical moulds (150 mm Ø × 300 mm)
5. Vernier caliper, scale, tamping rod

**Procedure:**

1. **Casting & Curing:** Cast at least 3 cylinders as per IS 516. Cure in water for **28 days** at 27 ± 2 °C.
2. **Capping:** Cap both ends of the specimen using molten sulphur (or hard plaster) to ensure **parallel and smooth loading surfaces**.
3. **Measurement:** Measure the **diameter** (at three locations) and **height** of the specimen to the nearest 0.2 mm.
4. **Mounting:** Place the specimen centrally on the lower platen of the UTM. Attach the **compressometer** securely around the specimen with two dial gauges mounted **diametrically opposite** to compensate for any bending.
5. **Seating Load:** Apply a small seating load (≈ 5% of expected ultimate).
6. **Loading:** Apply the load **incrementally and continuously** at a rate of **140 kg/cm²/min (≈ 14 MPa/min)**.
7. **Recording:** At each load increment of about **1/10 of expected ultimate**, record the **load (P)** and **deformation (δ)** from both dial gauges (take the average).
8. **Continue** loading until the specimen fails. Note the **ultimate load ($P_{max}$)**.
9. **Calculation & Plotting:** Calculate stress $\sigma = P/A$ and strain $\varepsilon = \delta/L$ for each step. Plot the **stress–strain curve**.
10. **Secant Modulus:** Draw a straight line from the origin to the point on the curve at $\frac{1}{3} f_c$ and calculate its slope.

**Sketch (Block Diagram):**
```
[Compressometer]
     |  Dial Gauge 1        Dial Gauge 2
     |    |                    |
     |    V                    V
     |  =====================
     |  |     CYLINDER     |
     |  |   150 dia x 300  |
     |  =====================
     |        |          |
     |     [Platen]   [Platen]
     |   (Upper)     (Lower)
     |        |          |
     |   (UTM Loading Frame)
```

#### Part (b) – Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 150$ mm, $L = 200$ mm, $P_{max} = 460$ kN, $\delta_{1/3} = 0.38$ mm

**Step 1 – Cross-sectional Area:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi \times 150^{2}}{4} = 17671.46 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 – Ultimate Strength:**

$$
f_c = \frac{460 \times 10^{3}}{17671.46} = 26.03 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 – Load at One-Third:**

$$
P_{1/3} = \frac{460}{3} = 153.33 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 – Stress at One-Third:**

$$
\sigma_{1/3} = \frac{153.33 \times 10^{3}}{17671.46} = 8.676 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 – Strain at One-Third:**

$$
