# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: List the three Non-Destructive Tests on hardened concrete covered in the KTU Concrete Lab syllabus, and state one specific property measured by each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Rebound Hammer Test (IS 13311 Part 2)** — Measures *surface hardness* of concrete, which is correlated empirically to **compressive strength**.
2. **Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity Test (IS 13311 Part 1)** — Measures the *velocity of longitudinal P-waves* through concrete, used to assess **internal homogeneity, density, and detect cracks/voids**.
3. **Rebar Locator / Cover Meter (BS 1881-204)** — Measures **cover thickness, reinforcement bar diameter, and bar spacing** using electromagnetic induction principles.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: What is the impact energy of a Type N Schmidt Hammer, and why is the median of 9–12 readings used instead of the mean?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Type N Schmidt Hammer delivers a standard impact energy of 2.207 J (0.225 kgf·m)**, making it suitable for normal-weight concrete and masonry. The median of 9 to 12 readings is used (rather than the arithmetic mean) because individual readings are sensitive to local variations in surface texture, aggregate exposure, and micro-cracks. The **median filters out outlier values** that may result from accidental impact on aggregate or a void, providing a more *statistically robust* estimate of the characteristic surface hardness as mandated by **IS 13311 (Part 2) : 1992**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** The lab examination for Concrete Lab (PCCEL508) follows a combined pattern. Part B is typically a *viva + record + analytical* question worth 14 marks with internal choice. Below are two complete 14-mark alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the working principle, instrumentation, and test procedure of the **Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity Test** as per IS 13311 (Part 1) : 1992. Discuss the three transducer configurations with neat sketches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following transit times were recorded on a 200 mm thick concrete wall using direct transmission UPV at five locations: **38, 39, 40, 38, 37 μs**. Compute the average pulse velocity, grade the concrete quality using IS 13311, and estimate the dynamic modulus of elasticity. Take density = 2400 kg/m³ and Poisson's ratio = 0.20. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL ANSWER (a) — 7 Marks

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
The UPV test is based on the transmission of high-frequency **longitudinal stress waves (P-waves)** through the concrete. A transmitter transducer (piezoelectric) converts a high-voltage pulse into a mechanical vibration at **20–150 kHz** (typically 50 kHz). This wave travels through the concrete and is received by a receiver transducer that converts the mechanical energy back to an electrical signal. The instrument measures the **transit time (t)** in microseconds. The pulse velocity is computed as:

$$V = \frac{L}{t}$$

**Instrumentation (2 marks):**
* **Transmitter (Tx) and Receiver (Rx) transducers** — piezoelectric crystals
* **Pulse generator and timing circuit** — triggers pulse and measures t
* **Display unit** — shows transit time in microseconds
* **Couplant** (petroleum jelly or grease) — to eliminate air gaps at contact

**Three Transducer Configurations (2 marks):**

1. **Direct Transmission (preferred):** Tx and Rx on opposite faces. Path L = full thickness. **Most accurate.**
2. **Semi-Direct Transmission:** Tx and Rx on adjacent perpendicular faces. Path L = diagonal. **Used for corners.**
3. **Indirect (Surface) Transmission:** Both transducers on the same face. Path L = surface distance. **Least reliable** because surface waves travel slower than P-waves. A correction factor *k* must be applied.

**Procedure (1 mark):** Clean surfaces → apply couplant → measure L → take 3 readings within ±2% agreement → compute V → average and grade per IS 13311.

#### MODEL ANSWER (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute V at each location (3 marks):**

| Location | t (μs) | V = L/t (m/s) | V (km/s) |
|:--------:|:------:|:-------------:|:--------:|
| 1 | 38 | 0.200 / 38×10⁻⁶ = 5263 | 5.26 |
| 2 | 39 | 5128 | 5.13 |
| 3 | 40 | 5000 | 5.00 |
| 4 | 38 | 5263 | 5.26 |
| 5 | 37 | 5405 | 5.41 |

**Step 2 — Average V (1 mark):**

$$\bar{V} = \frac{5.26 + 5.13 + 5.00 + 5.26 + 5.41}{5} = \frac{26.06}{5} = 5.21 \text{ km/s}$$

**Step 3 — Quality grading per IS 13311 (1 mark):**

Since $\bar{V} = 5.21 \text{ km/s} > 4.5 \text{ km/s}$ → **Concrete Quality: EXCELLENT**

**Step 4 — Dynamic modulus of elasticity (2 marks):**

$$E_d = \rho V^2 \cdot \frac{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}{(1-\nu)}$$

Substituting $\rho = 2400$ kg/m³, $V = 5210$ m/s, $\nu = 0.20$:

$$\text{Factor} = \frac{(1.20)(0.60)}{(0.80)} = 0.90$$

$$E_d = 2400 \times (5210)^2 \times 0.90$$

$$E_d = 2400 \times 27.144 \times 10^6 \times 0.90$$

$$E_d = 58.63 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} = 58.63 \text{ GPa}$$

> **Final Answer:** $\bar{V} = 5.21 \text{ km/s}$, Grade = **Excellent**, $E_d = 58.63 \text{ GPa}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the **Rebound Hammer Test** in detail — its working principle, the concept of rebound number, the IS 13311 (Part 2) procedure for taking and statistically filtering readings, and the directional angle correction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Type N Schmidt Hammer is used on the underside of a horizontal beam (vertical-down test position) at 10 points. The readings are: **28, 30, 32, 29, 31, 30, 33, 29, 30, 28**. Apply the IS 13311 rule, and using the standard Type N horizontal calibration curve, estimate the compressive strength. Given that the manufacturer's angle correction factor for vertical-down at 90° from horizontal is **+1.0** to be added to the rebound number, present the final strength estimate. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL ANSWER (a) — 7 Marks

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
The Rebound Hammer (Schmidt Hammer) works on the principle of **energy restitution** during impact. A spring-loaded hammer mass is propelled against the concrete surface with a defined impact energy (2.207 J for Type N). Upon impact, the mass rebounds; the **rebound distance** is read on a linear scale. The **rebound number R** is the ratio (in percent) of the rebound distance to the initial spring extension:

$$R = \frac{l_r}{l} \times 100$$

A higher R indicates **stiffer, harder, denser concrete** → higher compressive strength. Lower R indicates softer, weaker, or honeycombed concrete.

**Procedure (IS 13311 Part 2) (3 marks):**

1. Select a smooth, dry, clean surface area of at least **300 mm × 300 mm**.
2. Mark **9 to 12 test points** in a grid; each point **≥ 20 mm from edges** and **≥ 20 mm from any other impact point**.
3. Hold the hammer **perpendicular** to the surface; press until impact occurs.
4. Record the rebound number R to the nearest integer.
5. Repeat for all points; tabulate values.
6. **Statistical filter:** Discard any reading that deviates by more than 6 units from the median of the set. For 9 readings, discard 1 highest and 1 lowest → median of 7. For 12 readings, discard 2 highest and 2 lowest → median of 8.

**Angle Correction (1 mark):**
The standard calibration curve is for **horizontal impact**. If the hammer is used vertically (up or down), the rebound number must be corrected using a manufacturer's angle correction chart. For Type N hammer:
* Vertical-down at 90°: add correction (+1 to +2)
* Vertical-up at 90°: subtract correction (-2 to -3)

**Limitations (1 mark):**
Surface texture, carbonation, moisture, and aggregate type all influence R. Local calibration with cores is essential for accurate strength estimation.

#### MODEL ANSWER (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Arrange readings in ascending order (1 mark):**

$$28, 28, 29, 29, 30, 30, 30, 31, 32, 33$$

**Step 2 — Apply IS 13311 rule for 10 readings — discard 1 highest and 1 lowest (2 marks):**

Discard: 28, 33
Remaining: **28, 29, 29, 30, 30, 30, 31, 32** (8 values)

**Step 3 — Compute the median (1 mark):**

With 8 values, median = average of 4th and 5th values:

$$\bar{R}_{\text{raw}} = \frac{30 + 30}{2} = 30$$

**Step 4 — Apply angle correction (1 mark):**

Vertical-down test (90° from horizontal) → add correction factor:

$$R_{\text{corrected}} = 30 + 1.0 = 31$$

**Step 5 — Read f_c from calibration curve (2 marks):**

Standard Type N horizontal curve (R, f_c):
| R | 30 | 32 | 35 |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| f_c (MPa) | 25 | 30 | 36 |

Interpolating linearly for R = 31:

$$f_c = 25 + \frac{(31-30)}{(32-30)} \cdot (30-25) = 25 + \frac{1}{2} \cdot 5 = 27.5 \text{ MPa}$$

> **Final Answer:** Corrected Rebound Number = **31**, Estimated Compressive Strength = **27.5 MPa**.

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Most Commonly Lose Marks in NDT Lab Exam Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the IS code number (IS 13311 Part 1 / Part 2)** — Examiners *expect* the standard reference. Marks are deducted if not cited.
> 2. **Using arithmetic mean instead of median** for RHT readings — this is a classic 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **No path-length unit conversion** in UPV — students often write V = 0.150 / 38 × 10⁻⁶ = 3947 m/s without explicitly stating L = 0.150 m and t = 38 × 10⁻⁶ s. Show units always.
> 4. **Confusing Poisson's ratio formula** — the formula $E_d = \rho V^2 (1+\nu)(1-2\nu)/(1-\nu)$ is sometimes written upside-down by students. Memorise it correctly.
> 5. **Sketching transducer configuration incorrectly** — in direct transmission, the two transducers must be on *opposite* faces; for indirect, both on the *same* face. Drawing them on adjacent faces and labelling it "direct" loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Using mm/s instead of km/s** for UPV — quality grading per IS 13311 uses km/s; if you write m/s the threshold (4.5) is misapplied.
> 7. **Not mentioning the limitation of NDT** — every KTU 14-mark answer must end with 2-3 lines on limitations to score full marks.

---

## 5.4 KTU Viva-Voce — Probable Direct Questions

> [!NOTE]
> **Top 10 questions a KTU external examiner may ask in the lab viva on this experiment:**
>
> 1. What is the impact energy of the Type N Schmidt Hammer? *(Ans: 2.207 J)*
> 2. Why is Type N not used for mass concrete? *(Ans: Insufficient impact energy; Type M with 29.43 J is used.)*
> 3. What is the standard frequency range of UPV transducers? *(Ans: 20 kHz to 150 kHz)*
> 4. Why is direct transmission preferred over indirect? *(Ans: Direct measures pure P-wave through full section; indirect measures surface wave.)*
> 5. What is the role of the couplant? *(Ans: Eliminates air gap, ensures full energy transfer.)*
> 6. Define Poisson's ratio for concrete. *(Ans: ~0.20 for normal concrete, ranging 0.15–0.25.)*
> 7. What does an UPV of 3.0 km/s indicate? *(Ans: Poor quality or internal defect.)*
> 8. Can a rebar locator detect non-ferrous reinforcement? *(Ans: No, only steel/ferrous.)*
> 9. Why is the median preferred over the mean? *(Ans: Robust against outliers from local surface defects.)*
> 10. What is SonReb method? *(Ans: Combined Sonic-UPV + Rebound strength correlation.)*

---

## 5.5 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Lab Exam:**

* [x] The three NDT methods mandated by KTU PCCEL508 Module 2 are: **RHT (IS 13311 P2)**, **UPV (IS 13311 P1)**, and **Rebar Locator (BS 1881-204 / IS 456 guidance)**.
* [x] **Type N Schmidt Hammer** has impact energy = **2.207 J (0.225 kgf·m)**.
* [x] Standard UPV transducer frequency = **50–54 kHz** (range: 20–150 kHz).
* [x] **UPV threshold for "Excellent" concrete = > 4.5 km/s.**
* [x] **UPV threshold for "Poor" concrete = < 3.0 km/s.**
* [x] **Median** is used (not mean) for RHT readings per IS 13311.
* [x] **Direct transmission** is the most reliable UPV configuration.
* [x] **Couplant** (petroleum jelly) is mandatory for UPV to eliminate air gaps.
* [x] **Pulse velocity formula:** $V = L / t$ with L in m, t in μs, V in m/s (or convert to km/s for grading).
* [x] **Dynamic modulus formula:** $E_d = \rho V^2 \cdot (1+\nu)(1-2\nu)/(1-\nu)$, with $\rho$ in kg/m³, V in m/s, result in Pa.
* [x] **Minimum cover per IS 456:** Slab = 20 mm, Beam = 25 mm, Column = 40 mm, Footing = 50 mm.
* [x] **Rebar locator principle:** Electromagnetic induction / eddy current.
* [x] **Rebar locator cannot detect** non-ferrous (PVC, aluminium) reinforcement.
* [x] **Limitations of NDT:** Surface condition, calibration, age of concrete, aggregate type, and carbonation all affect readings.
* [x] **Calibration of NDT with cores** is essential for accurate strength estimation in real projects.
* [x] **SonReb method** combines RHT and UPV for improved strength estimation.
* [x] **Always cite the IS code number** in your answer — IS 13311 Part 1 (UPV) and IS 13311 Part 2 (RHT).
* [x] **Unit conventions:** Strength in **MPa**, cover in **mm**, bar diameter in **mm**, pulse velocity in **km/s** (for grading) or **m/s** (for calculation).

> **Final Note from Your KTU Examiner:** This experiment tests *practical skill* and *interpretive intelligence*. The lab record must be in the **standard KTU format** (Aim, Apparatus, Theory, Procedure, Observations, Calculations, Result, Precautions, Viva Questions). Always include a **labelled sketch** of the experimental setup — even a simple pencil drawing scores the full 1-2 marks allotted for "neat diagram."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study on Concrete Core Cutter, Concrete Penetrometer and Crack Detection Microscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL508 - CONCRETE LAB (MT-2)
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study on Concrete Core Cutter, Concrete Penetrometer and Crack Detection Microscope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Concrete Core Cutter

**Formal Definition:**
A Concrete Core Cutter (also called a Core Drilling Machine) is a heavy-duty rotary drilling apparatus used in Civil Engineering quality assurance to extract cylindrical *concrete core specimens* from an existing hardened concrete structure. These extracted cores are subsequently tested in a Compression Testing Machine (CTM) to evaluate the in-situ compressive strength, density, and homogeneity of the parent concrete.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Core cutting is classified as a **partially destructive test (PDT)** because, although it creates a small hole, it provides the most direct measurement of actual in-place concrete strength — far more reliable than purely non-destructive methods (NDT).

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of the core cutter like a **cookie cutter pressed into a freshly baked cake** to sample the inside. Just as a baker cuts a small cylindrical piece to check if the cake is baked properly all the way through (not just on the surface), a civil engineer uses the core cutter to extract a small cylindrical "plug" of hardened concrete from a slab, column, or beam. The plug (core) is then crushed in a lab machine to reveal the *true* strength of the concrete inside the structure — beyond what a surface test (like rebound hammer) can tell us.

**Key Specifications (KTU Standard – IS 516 Part 4 / IS 1199):**

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Core Diameter (D)** | **100 mm (preferred)**, 150 mm for max aggregate > 40 mm |
| **Length-to-Diameter Ratio (L/D)** | **2.0 (preferred range 1.0 to 2.0)** |
| **Diamond Bit Rotational Speed** | **900 – 1500 rpm** |
| **Cooling Water Flow** | Continuous (to prevent bit overheating) |
| **Extracted Core Use** | Compressive strength, density, carbonation depth |

---

### 1.2 Concrete Penetrometer (Windsor Probe System)

**Formal Definition:**
The Concrete Penetrometer (Windsor Probe Penetrometer) is a **non-destructive testing (NDT)** instrument that estimates the in-place compressive strength of hardened concrete by measuring the depth of penetration of a hardened steel alloy probe that is fired into the concrete surface by a precisely standardized powder charge.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Windsor Probe is one of the few NDT methods whose measurement is *invasive* (it does create a small impact crater) but is still classified as NDT because structural integrity is not compromised.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine pushing a thumbtack into a piece of soft wood versus a piece of hard oak — the harder the wood, the shallower the tack sinks. The Windsor Probe works exactly the same way, except instead of a thumbtack, a **high-strength alloy probe is shot** into the concrete using a controlled gunpowder cartridge. The shallower the probe's exposed length, the harder (stronger) the concrete. Engineers then look up this "exposed length" in a calibration chart (provided by the manufacturer) to estimate compressive strength.

**Key Specifications:**

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Probe Material** | **Hardened steel alloy (Rockwell C 60)** |
| **Probe Diameter** | **6.35 mm (0.25 inch)** |
| **Probe Length** | **79.4 mm (3.125 inch)** |
| **Cartridge Power Levels** | **Standard (Green), High (Yellow), Super (Red)** |
| **Standard Test Locations** | **Min. 150 mm from edges, 3 probes per location** |

---

### 1.3 Crack Detection Microscope

**Formal Definition:**
A Crack Detection Microscope (also known as a Crack Width Gauge Microscope or Pocket Microscope) is a portable, hand-held optical magnification instrument used by structural engineers to **measure the width of cracks** in concrete, masonry, plaster, or other structural surfaces with sub-millimetre precision (typically to **0.02 mm**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Crack width measurement is a *mandatory* acceptance criterion for RCC structures under IS 456:2000 — different exposure conditions permit different maximum crack widths (typically **0.1 mm to 0.3 mm**).

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine a hairline fracture on a wall that is too thin to measure with a normal ruler. A Crack Detection Microscope is like a **tiny jeweller's loupe with a built-in measuring scale** — you place the eyepiece on the crack, look through, and the magnified image of the crack has fine crosshairs and a graduated reticle that lets you read the crack width directly, almost like reading the markings on a measuring cylinder.

**Key Specifications:**

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Magnification** | **40× (typical), some models 60× or 100×** |
| **Measuring Range** | **0.0 mm to 4.0 mm** |
| **Least Count (Resolution)** | **0.02 mm** |
| **Built-in Light Source** | LED illumination |
| **Application** | IS 456 crack width limits & durability assessment |

---

### 1.4 Comparative Summary of the Three Instruments

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Reference Matrix (Very Important for KTU Viva & Theory Exam):**

| Feature | Core Cutter | Penetrometer | Crack Microscope |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Test Type** | Partially Destructive | Non-Destructive (invasive) | Non-Destructive (visual) |
| **Property Measured** | In-situ Compressive Strength | Estimated Compressive Strength | Crack Width |
| **Output** | Numerical (MPa) | Numerical (MPa via chart) | Numerical (mm) |
| **Directness** | Most direct & accurate | Indirect (correlation-based) | Direct visual measurement |
| **Reference Code** | IS 516 Part 4, BS 1881-120 | ASTM C803, IS 13311 | IS 456, IS 1199 |
| **Time Required** | Several hours (coring + curing + testing) | A few minutes per location | Seconds per crack |
| **Best For** | Final verification, forensic | Quick screening of large areas | Serviceability / durability check |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle – Concrete Core Cutter

The core cutter operates on the principle of **rotary abrasive cutting** using a diamond-impregnated cylindrical drill bit. The operational logic is broken down as follows:

- **Step 1 – Site Selection & Marking:** The engineer identifies a representative location (away from reinforcement, edges, and highly stressed zones) and marks the drilling centre using chalk or marker.
- **Step 2 – Rig Setup:** The core drill is anchored or weighted against the concrete surface to prevent lateral movement. A base plate keeps the drill perpendicular (essential to obtain a vertical core with parallel ends).
- **Step 3 – Cooling Water Supply:** Continuous water flow is fed to the bit's cutting zone. This serves **three critical functions**:
  - Cools the diamond segments (diamond loses hardness above ~700 °C).
  - Flushes away the concrete slurry (cuttings).
  - Reduces dust and airborne silica exposure.
- **Step 4 – Drilling:** The diamond bit rotates at high speed (900–1500 rpm) with slow, steady downward pressure. The abrasive diamond grinds the concrete matrix.
- **Step 5 – Core Extraction:** Once the bit penetrates the full depth, the cylindrical core is detached. The core is removed using a wedge or by carefully breaking the uncut portion.
- **Step 6 – End Preparation:** The irregular ends of the core are capped with **sulphur mortar capping** (or high-strength plaster of Paris) to make them perfectly parallel and smooth — this ensures uniform load distribution in the CTM.
- **Step 7 – Compression Testing:** The capped core is placed in the CTM and loaded to failure.

**The 'Why' Behind the Process:**
Concrete is heterogeneous (contains aggregate, cement paste, voids). A surface test like the rebound hammer only measures the *outer 30 mm* layer. By extracting a core, we get a sample that represents the bulk material, including the paste-aggregate bond and any internal defects.

---

### 2.2 Working Principle – Windsor Probe Penetrometer

- **Step 1 – Surface Preparation:** The concrete surface at the test location is ground smooth using a hand grinder to ensure a flat, clean impact area.
- **Step 2 – Probe Loading:** A standard Windsor probe (hardened steel alloy) is loaded into the gun barrel.
- **Step 3 – Cartridge Insertion:** A standardized powder cartridge (matched to the estimated concrete strength range) is inserted behind the probe.
- **Step 4 – Firing:** The gun is positioned perpendicular to the concrete surface, and the trigger fires the cartridge. The expanding gases accelerate the probe into the concrete.
- **Step 5 – Measurement:** After firing, the *exposed length* of the probe (the part sticking out of the concrete) is measured using a depth gauge. The shorter the exposed length, the harder (stronger) the concrete.
- **Step 6 – Strength Correlation:** The exposed length is fed into a **manufacturer-supplied calibration chart** (or empirical formula) to estimate the equivalent compressive strength.

**The 'Why' Behind the Process:**
The depth of penetration depends on the concrete's resistance to impact, which is empirically correlated with its compressive strength. Three probes are typically fired at one location, and the **median value** is used (to discard outliers from hitting aggregate or voids).

---

### 2.3 Working Principle – Crack Detection Microscope

- **Step 1 – Surface Cleaning:** The crack surface is cleaned of dust and loose particles (use a brush or compressed air).
- **Step 2 – Microscope Placement:** The microscope is held perpendicular to the crack, with the lens tip in contact with (or very close to) the surface.
- **Step 3 – Focusing:** The eyepiece is rotated until the graduated reticle and the crack image are both sharp.
- **Step 4 – Measurement:** The crack is aligned with the reticle, and the **width is read directly** at the location where the crack is widest (or at the specific location required by the engineer).
- **Step 5 – Recording:** The width is recorded in mm, along with the location, orientation, and a sketch of the crack pattern.

**The 'Why' Behind the Process:**
Crack width directly affects durability — wider cracks allow greater ingress of water, chlorides, and CO₂, accelerating corrosion of reinforcement and carbonation of concrete.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield — Must Memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 2-mark and 14-mark KTU questions every year.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_{\text{core}} = \dfrac{4P}{\pi D^{2}}$ | Compressive strength of extracted core | MPa |
| 2 | $f_{\text{is}} = f_{\text{core}} \times F_{L/D} \times F_{d} \times F_{dir} \times F_{mc}$ | Estimated in-situ cube strength | MPa |
| 3 | $F_{L/D} = 0.11n + 0.78$ (for $1.0 \le n \le 2.0$) | Length/Diameter correction factor | dimensionless |
| 4 | $F_{d}$ from table (diameter correction) | $D=50\,mm: 1.06$; $D=100\,mm: 1.00$; $D=150\,mm: 0.98$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $F_{dir}$ (direction correction) | Horizontal core: $1.00$; Vertical core: $1.10$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | $F_{mc}$ (moisture condition) | Air-dried: $1.00$; Soaked: $1.09$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | $L_{\text{exposed}} = L_{\text{total}} - P_{\text{depth}}$ | Windsor Probe exposed length | mm |
| 8 | $f_{c} = f_{\text{chart}}(L_{\text{exposed}})$ | Penetrometer strength from calibration chart | MPa |
| 9 | $w_{\text{crack}} = (N \times \text{LC})$ | Crack width (N = divisions on reticle) | mm |
| 10 | $LC = \dfrac{0.02 \text{ mm}}{\text{division}}$ (typical) | Least count of crack microscope | mm/div |

> **Notation:** $P$ = crushing load (N), $D$ = core diameter (mm), $n$ = $L/D$ ratio, $L_{total}$ = probe length, $P_{depth}$ = penetration depth.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Core Cutter:** Used in **structural audit** of aged buildings, bridges (NHAI specifications), and dams where strength verification is legally required before load certification.
- **Penetrometer:** Used in **rapid screening of large structures** (e.g., pavements, runways, parking decks) where speed matters more than ultimate precision.
- **Crack Microscope:** Used in **serviceability assessment** and pre/post-construction surveys to decide whether cracks are *cosmetic* (acceptable) or *structural* (need repair).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Core Compressive Strength Formula

A cylindrical core has a circular cross-section. The compressive stress in such a core, when subjected to an axial load $P$, is force per unit cross-sectional area.

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}
$$

$$
f_{\text{core}} = \frac{\text{Load}}{\text{Area}} = \frac{P}{A}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_{\text{core}} = \frac{P}{\dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}} = \frac{4P}{\pi D^{2}}\,}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The area formula $A = \pi r^{2}$ with $r = D/2$ gives $A = \pi (D/2)^2 = \pi D^2 / 4$. Substituting $A$ into stress = Force/Area and rearranging gives the boxed expression.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the L/D Correction Factor

Standard cubes have a slenderness of 1.0 (cube). Standard cylinders have L/D = 2.0. When testing a core with $1.0 \le n \le 2.0$, the strength must be scaled to the standard cylinder or cube equivalent.

The empirical linear relationship adopted (IS 516 / BS 1881-120) is:

$$
F_{L/D} = 0.11n + 0.78
$$

For $n = 2.0$ (standard cylinder): $F_{L/D} = 0.11(2) + 0.78 = 0.22 + 0.78 = 1.00$ ✓
For $n = 1.0$ (cube-like): $F_{L/D} = 0.11(1) + 0.78 = 0.11 + 0.78 = 0.89$
For $n = 1.5$: $F_{L/D} = 0.11(1.5) + 0.78 = 0.165 + 0.78 = 0.945$

> **Conversion Logic:** A shorter specimen is more restrained by the platens (end friction), giving *apparently higher* strength. Therefore, the correction factor is < 1.0 for short cores, and multiplies the raw core strength upward to get the equivalent in-situ strength.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 1 – Core Cutter Calculation (Full Step-by-Step)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Type Question]**

A 100 mm diameter concrete core is drilled from a bridge deck. The core length is 150 mm. The core is air-dried and tested in a compression testing machine. The failure load is **220 kN**. The core was drilled in a **horizontal direction** from a vertical column. Estimate the in-situ cube compressive strength.

**Step 1 – Identify given values:**

$$
P = 220 \text{ kN} = 220{,}000 \text{ N}, \quad D = 100 \text{ mm}, \quad L = 150 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 – Compute the L/D ratio:**

$$
n = \frac{L}{D} = \frac{150}{100} = 1.5
$$

**Step 3 – Compute the raw core strength:**

$$
f_{\text{core}} = \frac{4P}{\pi D^{2}}
$$

$$
f_{\text{core}} = \frac{4 \times 220{,}000}{\pi \times (100)^{2}}
$$

$$
f_{\text{core}} = \frac{880{,}000}{31{,}415.93} = 28.01 \text{ MPa}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** $4P = 4 \times 220{,}000 = 880{,}000$ N; $D^2 = 10{,}000$ mm²; $\pi \times 10{,}000 = 31{,}415.93$ mm². Dividing gives stress in N/mm² = MPa.

**Step 4 – Compute correction factors:**

$$
F_{L/D} = 0.11n + 0.78 = 0.11(1.5) + 0.78 = 0.945
$$

$$
F_{d} = 1.00 \quad (\text{since } D = 100 \text{ mm})
$$

$$
F_{dir} = 1.00 \quad (\text{horizontal core})
$$

$$
F_{mc} = 1.00 \quad (\text{air-dried condition})
$$

**Step 5 – Compute estimated in-situ strength:**

$$
f_{\text{is}} = f_{\text{core}} \times F_{L/D} \times F_{d} \times F_{dir} \times F_{mc}
$$

$$
f_{\text{is}} = 28.01 \times 0.945 \times 1.00 \times 1.00 \times 1.00
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_{\text{is}} = 26.47 \text{ MPa}\,}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The estimated in-situ cube strength is approximately **26.5 MPa**, indicating the parent concrete is roughly **M25 grade** (slightly above the design strength).

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 – Windsor Probe Interpretation

A Windsor Probe test on a concrete slab shows exposed probe lengths of **35 mm, 38 mm, and 36 mm** (median = 36 mm). Using the manufacturer's calibration chart (linear approximation: $f_c = -1.6 L_{\text{exp}} + 95$ for Green cartridge), estimate the compressive strength.

**Step 1 – Determine the median exposed length:**

$$
L_{\text{exp,median}} = 36 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 – Apply the calibration equation:**

$$
f_c = -1.6 \times L_{\text{exp}} + 95
$$

$$
f_c = -1.6 \times 36 + 95 = -57.6 + 95 = 37.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 – Note limitations:**

> [!WARNING]
> Windsor Probe gives a **rough estimate** with ±15–20% accuracy. It should never replace core testing for final acceptance, but is excellent for **relative comparison** of one area against another.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 3 – Crack Width Measurement

A crack is observed on the underside of an RCC beam. The crack detection microscope (40× magnification, LC = 0.02 mm/division) shows the crack spans **8 divisions** on the reticle at its widest point.

**Step 1 – Apply the formula:**

$$
w_{\text{crack}} = N \times LC
$$

$$
w_{\text{crack}} = 8 \times 0.02 = 0.16 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 – Compare with IS 456:2000 limits:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 456:2000 – Maximum Permissible Crack Widths:**
> - Moderate exposure: **0.2 mm**
> - Severe exposure: **0.1 mm**
> - Very severe exposure: **0.1 mm** (with special protection)

**Step 3 – Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\,w_{\text{crack}} = 0.16 \text{ mm}\,}
$$

If the structure is in **moderate exposure** (e.g., interior of a building), this crack is **acceptable**. If in **severe exposure** (e.g., coastal structure), it **exceeds the limit** and requires repair (typically epoxy injection).

---

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Test to Use?

> [!NOTE]
> **When to use which instrument (commonly asked in KTU viva):**

| Scenario | Recommended Test | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Final strength certification of a critical structure | Core Cutter | Most direct & accurate |
| Quick screening of 50 slabs in 2 days | Penetrometer | Fastest, no lab needed |
| Pre-handover crack survey of a residential building | Crack Microscope | Direct width measurement for serviceability |
| Disputed strength on an old bridge | Core Cutter | Accepted as legal evidence |
| Quality check on freshly cast pavement | Penetrometer | Real-time, no drilling |
| Forensic investigation of cracking pattern | Crack Microscope + Core Cutter | Both: width assessment + strength |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three non-destructive testing (NDT) methods used for assessment of hardened concrete. State one advantage of the Windsor Probe Penetrometer over the Rebound Hammer.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Three NDT methods:** (i) Rebound Hammer (Schmidt Hammer), (ii) Windsor Probe Penetrometer, (iii) Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (UPV) Test, (iv) Crack Detection Microscope.
2. **Advantage of Windsor Probe over Rebound Hammer:** The Windsor Probe penetrates the concrete to a depth of about 20–50 mm, giving a more representative measurement of the bulk concrete, whereas the Rebound Hammer only tests the **outer 30 mm** surface layer which may not reflect the actual in-situ strength (e.g., in case of carbonated surface). *(1 mark for listing, 1 mark for each correct elaboration = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define an L/D correction factor in core testing. Why is it required?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The L/D (Length-to-Diameter) correction factor is an empirical multiplier applied to the raw compressive strength of a drilled core to account for differences between the *tested specimen geometry* and the *standard specimen geometry* (cube or standard cylinder). *(1.5 marks)*
- It is required because shorter cores are more restrained by the platen friction during compression testing, giving **artificially higher strength values**. The correction converts the measured strength to a value that would have been obtained on a *standard* specimen. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. **Answer ANY ONE of Question A or Question B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Adapted]**

**(a) Describe with a neat sketch the working of a concrete core cutter used for assessing the in-situ compressive strength of hardened concrete. List the corrections applied to the measured core strength. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**
1. **Working Description (4 marks):**
   - The concrete core cutter consists of a hollow cylindrical diamond-impregnated drill bit driven by an electric motor.
   - The machine is anchored to the concrete surface using anchor bolts or weights. A water supply hose is connected.
   - The drill bit is rotated at high speed (900–1500 rpm) with steady downward pressure; water cools the bit and flushes slurry.
   - After full depth penetration, the core is detached and removed. Its ends are capped with sulphur mortar to make them plane and parallel.
   - The capped core is tested in a Compression Testing Machine, and the failure load is noted.

2. **Neat Sketch (1 mark):** *(A neat labelled diagram of the core cutter showing motor, barrel, diamond bit, water inlet, anchor, and concrete surface — 1 mark)*

3. **Correction Factors Applied (2 marks):**
   - $F_{L/D}$ – length-to-diameter ratio correction.
   - $F_{d}$ – diameter correction.
   - $F_{dir}$ – direction of drilling correction.
   - $F_{mc}$ – moisture condition correction.

---

**(b) A 100 mm diameter core is drilled from a hardened concrete slab. The core length is 130 mm. The core was tested in a soaked condition and failed at a load of 200 kN. The drilling direction was vertical. Determine the estimated in-situ cube compressive strength. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 – Given data (1 mark):**
$P = 200{,}000 \text{ N}$, $D = 100$ mm, $L = 130$ mm, soaked, vertical drilling.

**Step 2 – Compute L/D ratio (1 mark):**
$$
n = \frac{130}{100} = 1.3
$$

**Step 3 – Raw core strength (1.5 marks):**
$$
f_{\text{core}} = \frac{4 \times 200{,}000}{\pi \times (100)^{2}} = \frac{800{,}000}{31{,}415.93} = 25.46 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 – Apply corrections (2 marks):**
$$
F_{L/D} = 0.11(1.3) + 0.78 = 0.143 + 0.78 = 0.923
$$
$$
F_{d} = 1.00, \quad F_{dir} = 1.10 \text{ (vertical)}, \quad F_{mc} = 1.09 \text{ (soaked)}
$$

**Step 5 – Final in-situ strength (1.5 marks):**
$$
f_{\text{is}} = 25.46 \times 0.923 \times 1.00 \times 1.10 \times 1.09
$$
$$
f_{\text{is}} = 25.46 \times 1.1066 = 28.18 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_{\text{is}} \approx 28.2 \text{ MPa}\,}
$$

The structure corresponds to roughly **M25 grade** in-situ strength.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Adapted]**

**(a) Explain the working principle and procedure of the Windsor Probe Penetrometer test. Discuss its advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**
- **Working Principle (2 marks):** A hardened steel alloy probe is fired into hardened concrete by a standardized gunpowder cartridge. The exposed length of the probe, after penetration, is inversely proportional to the concrete's compressive strength. This exposed length is converted to compressive strength using a manufacturer-supplied calibration chart.
- **Procedure (2 marks):**
  1. Grind the test surface flat.
  2. Insert a probe and cartridge into the gun.
  3. Hold the gun perpendicular to the surface and fire.
  4. Measure the exposed probe length with a depth gauge.
  5. Take 3 readings per location; use the median.
  6. Refer to the calibration chart for strength.
- **Advantages (1.5 marks):** Quick, low cost, tests bulk concrete (not just surface), minimal surface damage.
- **Limitations (1.5 marks):** ±15–20% accuracy; only gives estimate; calibration chart is proprietary; affected by aggregate type.

---

**(b) Using a crack detection microscope (LC = 0.02 mm/div), a crack on an RCC column exposed to severe environment is measured. The crack spans 6 divisions on the reticle at its widest point. Is the crack acceptable as per IS 456:2000? Justify your answer with calculations. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 – Compute crack width (2 marks):**
$$
w = N \times LC = 6 \times 0.02 = 0.12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 – Recall IS 456 limits (3 marks):**
For **severe exposure** (e.g., coastal or industrial): maximum permissible crack width = **0.1 mm**.

**Step 3 – Compare and conclude (2 marks):**
Since measured width $0.12$ mm > allowable $0.1$ mm, the crack **exceeds the IS 456 limit** and is **NOT acceptable** for severe exposure. **Recommended action:** epoxy injection grouting or other approved repair to seal the crack and prevent chloride/CO₂ ingress.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake:**
> Students often forget to **state the exposure condition** before quoting the IS 456 limit. Always write: *"For [exposure category], the maximum permissible crack width is [value] as per IS 456:2000, Clause 35.3.2."* This single line carries **1–2 marks** by itself in the valuation key.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These Before the Exam:**

- **Core Cutter** is a *partially destructive* test; the core diameter is preferably **100 mm**; length preferably **100–200 mm** (L/D = 1.0 to 2.0).
- The **core strength formula** is $f = 4P/(\pi D^{2})$ — must be memorized.
- **Four correction factors** for cores: $F_{L/D}$, $F_{d}$, $F_{dir}$, $F_{mc}$ — remember $0.11n + 0.78$.
- **Vertical core** → direction factor **1.10**; **horizontal core** → **1.00**.
- **Soaked core** → moisture factor **1.09**; **air-dried** → **1.00**.
- **Windsor Probe** is an *NDT* method that measures **exposed length**; shorter exposure = higher strength; **median of 3** readings is used.
- Windsor Probe is **more representative** than Rebound Hammer because it tests deeper concrete.
- **Crack Detection Microscope** typically has magnification **40×** and least count **0.02 mm**.
- **IS 456:2000 crack width limits:** Moderate = 0.2 mm, Severe = 0.1 mm.
- **Diamond bit** is essential for coring — it grinds (not cuts) the concrete through abrasive action.
- **Cooling water** is mandatory during coring — prevents diamond bit damage and reduces dust.
- **Sulphur mortar capping** is used to make core ends perfectly parallel — essential for uniform load transfer.
- Always report core strength as the **estimated in-situ cube strength** after applying all corrections, never as raw strength.
